Preface
This book IS the second edition of a text designed tor undergraduate courses in sjgnals and systems. While ñiuch murses are frequently fjvird in ekctrical engiTieering tLLnicuIa, the concepts and techniques that form the core of the subject are of fundamental importance in all engineering disciplines. In fact, ihe scope of potemial and actual applications of the methods nf signal and system analysis continues to ej^pand as engineers are confronted
with new challenges involving the synthesis or analysis of complex proees.^es. For these reasons we feel that a course in signaU and systems not only is an essenttal eknicnt in
an engineering program bul also can be one of the most rewarding, exciting, and useful
courses that engineering students take during theirundei^gradiiate education.
Our treatment of the SiJbject of signals and systems in this second edition Tnaintains the same genera] philosophy as in the first edition but with significant rewriiing, restructuring, and addilions. These changes are designed to help both the instructor in presenting the subject material and the student in mastering it. In the preface to the first edition we stated that our tivera.!] approach to >ignaLs and systems had been guided by the continuing dcvclopjnents in technologies for signal and system design and implementation, which made it
increasingly importanT for a student to have equal famihanty with techniques suitable for
analyzing and synthesizing both continuous-time atid discrete-time systems. As we write
the preface to this second edition, that fjbs.ervaiioTi and guiding principle are even tnore true than before. Thus, while students studying signals and systems ihould certainly have a solid foundation in disciplines based on the laws of physics, they must also have a firm grounding in the use of computers for the analysis i>f phenomena and the implementation
of sysietns and algorithms. As a consequence, engineering curricula now reflect a hlend of subjects, some involving continuous-time mc-dels and others focusing on the use of com-
puters and discrete representations. For these rtiasons, signals and systems courses that bring discrete-lime and continuous-titne concepts together in a unified way play an in-
creasingly important role mthe edjcation of engineering students and in their preparation
for current and future developments in their chosen fields.
It is with these goals in mind that we have structured this book to develop in parsJlel
the methods of analysis for continuous-time and discrete-time signils and systems. This appn^ach also offers a distinct and extremely important pedagogical advantage. Specifi-
cally, we are able to draw on the similarities between continuous- and discrete-time methods in order to share insights and intuition developed in each domain. Similarly, we can
exploit the diflcrcnces between them to sharpen an understanding of the distinct properties
of each.
In organizing the material both originally and now in the second edition, we have
also considered it es^i^ntiai to introduce the student to some of the important uses of the basic methods that are developed in the book. Mot only does this provide the student with an appreciation for the range of applications of the Lechniques being learned and for diiiections for further study, but il alst^i helps to deepen understanding of the subject To
achieve this gfial we include introductory treatments on the subjects of ftUering. commu-

We in particular.

begin Chapter I by irtlr^>ilacing some qf the eletnentiiry ideas related to

the niathernatlcal representation of signals and systems. In particular we discuss transformatitins (*iuch as time shifts and scaling) of the independent variable ot a signal. We

also introduce some of the must important and basic continuous-lime and discrete-tijne

Signals, naTnely real and complex exponentials and the continuous-time and discrete- time

unit step and unit impulse. Chapter 1 also introduces block diagram representations of in-

terconnections of systems and discusses several basic system properties such as causality,
linearity and time iiivariance. In Chapter 2 wc build on these last two propertes, together

with the sifting property of unit impulses to develop the convolution -sum represeniatior

for discrete-time hneax, time-iti variant (L.T1) systems and the convolution mtcgral repre-

sentation for c continuous time LTl systems. In this treatment we use the intuition gained

from our development of the diKCrete-time case as an aid in deriving and understanding its

We continuous-time counterpart.

then turn to a discussion of causal, LTI systeitij; charac-

terized by linear constant-coefficient differential and difference equations. In this introduc-
toiy discussion we review the? hasic ideas involved in solving linear differential equation.s

(to which most students will have had some previous exposure) and we also provide a dis-

cussion of analogous methtids for linear difference equations. However, the primary focus of our developmenv in Chapter 2 is not on methods of solution, s;ince more convenient approaches are developed later using transform methods. Instead, in this first look, our intent

is to provide the student with some appreciation for these extremely impKortant classes of systems, which will bo encountered tjften in subsequent chapters, Pinally, Chapter 2 con-
-- cludes with a brief discussion of singularity functions steps, impulses, doublets, and so
-- fwnh in the context of Lheir role in the description and analysis of continuous-time LTl
systems. In particular, we stress the interpretatit^n of these signals in terms cf how they are denned under cc:nvolution^that is, in terms of the responses of LTl systems to these

idealized signals.

Chapters 3 through 6 present a thorough and self-contained development of the methods of Fourier analysis in both continuous and discrete time and together represent
we the most significant reorganization and revision iti the second editivin In partitiulgr, as indicated previously, we have introduced the concept of frequency- domain filtering at a
nnuch earlier point in the development in order t^ provide motivation for and a concrete apphcation of the Fourier methods being developed. As in the first edition, we begin the discussii^ns in Chapter 3 by emphasizing and illustratmg the two fundamental rcastms

for the important role Fourier analysis plays in the study of signals and systems in both continuous and discrete time: ( 1 ) extremeh broad classes of signals can be lepresentod as weighted sums at integrals cf complex exponentials; and (2) the response of an LTl system to a complcA exponenua) input is the same exponential multiplied by a complex-

preface

number characteristic of the system. However, in contrast to the first edition, the focus of attention in Chapter 3 is on Fourier series representations for periodic signals in both c^n-
tinaous time and discrete time. In this way we not only introduce and exammc many of the
properties of Fourier represftntations without the fidditionaZ mathematical generalization
required U^ obtain the Fourier transform for aperiodic signals, but we also can intiwluce
the application to filtering at a very early stage in the development. In particijiar, taking advantage of the fact that complex exponentials are eigenfunctions of LTl systems,
we introduce the frequency response of an LTl system and use it te discuss the concept
of frequency- selective filtering, tc introduce ideal filters, and to give several examples of uonideal filters described by diflTerential and difference equations. In this way, with a itiin-
inium of mathematical preliminaries, we provide the student *ith a deeper appreciation for whai a Fourier representation means and why it is such a useful construct.
Chapters 4 and 5 then build on the foundation provided hy Chapter 3 as wt develop
first the conrinuou^s-time Fourier transform in Chapter 4 and^ in a parallel fashion, the
discrete-time Foiuier transfotm in Chapter 5. In both chapters we derive the Fqurier trans-
form representation of an aperiodic signal as the limit of the Fourier series for a signai whose pcnod becomes arbitrarily lai^e. This perspective emphasizes the close relationship
between Fourier series and transforms, which we develop further in subsequerit sections
and which allows us to transfer the intuition developed foi Founer series in Chapter 3 to the
more genera] context of Fourier transforms. In both chapters we have included a discussion of the many important properties of Fourier transforms, with special emphasis placed
on the convolution and muldpUcation properties. In particular, the conv&Jutiori property allows us to take a second look at the topic of frequency-selective fihenng, while the mulLi plication property serves as the starting poitit for our treatment of sarnpling and mod-
m ulation in later chapters. Finally, the last sections in Chapters 4 and 5 we use transform
methods to determine the frequency responses of LTl systems described by differential and difference equations and to provide several examples illustrating how Fourier transforms can be used to compute the responses for such systems. To supplement these discussions {and later treatments of Laplace and ^-transforms) we have again included an Appondix at the end of the b[>ok that cofitains a description of the method of partial fraction expansion.
Our treatment of Fourier analysis in these two chapters is characteristic of the parallel treatment we have developed. Specifically, in our discussion in Chapter 5. we are
able to build on much of the insight developed in Chapter 4 for the continuous-time case, and toward the end of Chapter 5 we emphasize the complete duality in coniinijous-iime and discrtte-dme Fourier representations. In addition, we bring the special nature of each
domain into sharper focus by contrastii^ the differences between continuous- and discretetime Fourier analysis.
As those familiar with the Tirst edition will note, the lengths and scopes of Chapters 4 and 5 in the second edirion are considerably smaller than their ftrst edition counterparbi This is due not only to the fact that Fourier series are now deaU with in a separate chapter but also to our moving several topics into Chapter 6. Ttie result, we believe, has several
signittcant benefits. Firsts the presentation in three shorter chapters of the basic concepts and results of Fourier analysis, together with the introductitin at the concept of frequencyselective filtering, should help the student in oigardzing his or her understanding of this
material and in developing some intuition about the frequency domain and ^preciation
m for its petentia] appLcatiocis, Then, with Chapters 3-5 as a foundation, we can engage

While each ol the topics in Chapter 6 was present in the first edition* we believe that by

of Fotiri&r analysis, we have both simplified the introduction of this important topic in

In response to suggestions and prefertncea expressed by many users of the first edition we have modified notation in the discussion of Fourier transrortns to be more con-

sistent with notation most typically used for continuous-time and discrete-time Fourier

transforms. Specifically^ beginning with Chapter 3 we now denote the continuous-time

Fourier transform as Xijat) and die discrete-time Fourier transform as XieJ"^). As with all

options with notation^ there is not a utiique best choice for the notation for Founer trans-

fortns. However, it is our feelings and that of many of our colleagues, that the ntimtion used

in this edition represents the preferable choice.

Our treatmcT^t of sampling in Chapter 7 is concemeti primarily with the sampling

theorem and its implications. However, to place this subject in perspecti ve we begin by dis-

ctjssing tfie general concepts of representing a contintaous-time signal in terms of its samples and the reconstruction of signals using interpolation. After using frequency-domain

methods to derive the sampling theorem, we consider both the frequency and time domains to provide intuition conccniing the phenomenon of aliasing resulting from uudersampling. One of the very impoftant uses of sampling is in the discictJe-time processing of continuous-time sigr^als, a topic that we explore at $onie length in this chapter Following this, we turn to the sampling of discretft-time sigrals. The basic result underlying discrete-
m time sampling is developed a manner that pariUlels that used in continuous time, and

the applications of this result to problems of decimadon and interpolation are described. Again a variety of other applications, in both continuous and discrete time, are addressed

in the problems.

Once again the reader acquainted with our firstedicion will note a change, in this case
involving the reversal in the order of the presentation of samphng and communications We

have chosen to place sampling before commurucaiions in the second edition both because
we can call on simple intuition to motivate and describe the processes of sampling^and

reconstruction from samples and also because this order of presentation then allows us

in Chapter 8 to talk more easily about forms of communicahon systems that ^je closely related to sampling or rely fundamentally on using a sampled version of the signal to be

transmitted.

Our treatment of communicatioiis in Chapter 8 includes an in-depth discussion of

continuous-time sinusoidal amplitude mydulation (AM), which begins with the straight-
AM forward application of the multiplication propeny to describe the effect of sinustiida]

in the frequency domain and to suggest how the oiiginal modulating signal can be recov-

ered. Following this, we develop a number of additional issues and applications related

to sinusoid^ moduJation, including frequency -division multiplexing and single sideban^l
modulation. Many other examples and applications are described in the prjblems. Several

additional topics are covered in Chapter 8. The first of these is amplitude modulation of

a pulse train and time-division multiplexing, which has a close connection to the topic of

sampling in Chapter 7. Indeed we make this tie even more explicit and provide a look into

the impoitant field of digital communications by introducing and briefly describing the

topics of pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM) and intersymbol interference. Finally, our

di^LcuRSion t>i frequency modulation (FM) provides the reader with a look at a nonlinear

FM moduladon problem. Althou^ the analysis of

systems is not as straightforward as for

AM the

case, our introductory treatment indicates how frequency-domain methods can

FM be used to gain a sigDihcant amount of insight into the characteristics of

signals and

systems. Through these discussions at^d the many other aspects of modulation and communicatio^ns explored in the problems in this chapter we believe that the student can gain

an appreciation both for the nchness of the field of comntiunications and for the central

role that the tools of signals and systems analysis play in it,

Chaptesrs 9 and 10 treat the Laplace and ^-trao$fonn&. respectively. For the most part,
we focus an the bilateral versions of these transforms^ although in the last section of each

chapter we discuss unilateral transfonns and their use in solving differential and differ-

ence equations with nonzero initial conditions. Both chapters include discussions on: the

close relationship beiween these transforms and Fourier transforms; the class of rational

transforms and their representation in terms of poles and zeros; the region of conveigence

of a Laplace or ^-transform and its relationship to properties of the signal with which it is associated; inverse transforms usmg partial fraction expansion; the geometric evaluation

of system funocions and frequency responses from pole-zero plots; and basic transform
properties. In addition, in each chapter we exaniine the properties and uses of system

functions for LTI systems. Included in these discussions are the determination of system

functions for systems characterized by differential and difference equations; the use of sys -

tern function algebra for interconnections of LTI systems; and the construction of cascade,

parallel- and direct-form block-diagratn representations for systems with rational system

functions.

The tools of Laplace and ^-transforms form the basis for our examination of linear
feedback systems in Chapter 11. We begin this chapter by describing a number of the

important uses and properties of feedback iiystems, including stabilizing unstable systems,

designing tracking systems, and reducing system sensitivity. In subsequent sectiauK we use the tools that we have developed in previous chapters to exatnine three topics that are of

importance for both continuous-time and discrete-time feedback s>stem&. These are root

locus analysis, Nyqnist ploL^ and tbo Nyqui si criterion, and log -magnitude/phase plots and

the concepts of phase and gain margins for stable feedback systemji. The subject of signals and systems is an extraordinarily rich one, and a varieiy of

approaches can be taken in designing an introductory course. It was our intention with

the first edition and again with this second edition to provide instructors with a great deal of

mci

flexibilily in slnictudrig their presentations or the subject. To obtain this flexibility and kT

maximize the usefulness of this biiok for instructoi^, we have chosen to present thorough^

in -depth treattnttits of a cohesive set of topics that forms the core of most inuoduciory

LOLiTses on sigriiils and systems. In achieving this depth we have of necessity omitted m-

troduciions to topics such as descriptions of randum signals and stale spaLt; models thatitre

sometimes included in first courses on signals and systems. Traditionally, aL manv schools,

such topics arc not included in introductory courses but rather are developed in more depth

in lollow-on undergraduate courses or in courses explicitly devoted to their investiiialLon.

Although we have rioi mcluded an intmduction to state space in the book, mstnictor^ of

introducu^ry courses can easily incorporate it into the treatments of differential and dif-

ference equations thai can be found throughout the book. Iti particular, the disLussions

Slate-space representations.

A typical one-semester course at the sophomore-junior Icvei using this book would

cover Chapters 1-5 in reasonable depth (although various topics in each chapter are easily

omitted at the tjiscreiinn of the itistnictor) with selected topics chosen from the rcmair^ing

chapters. For example, one possihility is to present several of the bajiic topics in Chapters

to the use of system function concepts to analyze feedback syslemK. A variety of alternate

fonnats arc possible, including one that incorporates an inaoduction to stale space or one in which more focus is placed on continuous-lime systems by de-emphasizmg Chapters 5 and to and the discrete- time topics in Chapters 3, 7, 8, and 1 1

in addition to these course fontiats this book can be used a^ the basic text for a

thorough, iwo-semesier sequence on line^u' systems Alternatively, the portions of the btiok

not used in a first course on signals and systems car, logedier vfith other sources, form the
basis for a -iubsequent course. For example, much of the material in this book forms adirect

bridge to subjects such as state space analysis, control systems, digital signal process.ng,

communications and statistical signal prctcessing. Consequently, a follow-on course can be

conscructed that uses some of the topics in this book together with supplementary material

in order to provide an introduction to one or more wf these advanced subjects. In fact, a
nevi^ course following this model has been developed at MIT and has pt\)vcn not onl> n,
be d popular course among our smdcnts but also a crucial cotnponent of our signals and
systems curriculum.

Foreword
The concepts of signals and systems arise in a wide variety of nelds, and the ideas and
techniqucK associated with these concepts play an important role in such diverse areas of science and technoJogy as communications, aeronautics and astronautics, circtiit design^ acoustics, seismology* biomedical engineering, energy generation Aiid distributi\)n systetns, chemical process control, and speech processing. Aithough the physical nature of
the signals and systems that arise in these various disciplines may be drastically differ&nt,
they all have two very basic features in common. The signals, which are functions of one or more independent variables, contain information about the behavior or nature of ^i^Jnc ffhenomenon, whereas the systems respond to particular signals by producing other signals or some desired behavior. Voltages and currents as a function of time in an electrical tirt:uit are examples of signals, and acircmt is itself an example of a system, which in this case responds to applied voltages ajid currents. As another example, when an automobile driver depresses the accelerator pedal, the automobile reijponds by increasing the speed of the vehicle. In thisi case, the system is the automobile, the pressure on the accelerator
pedal the input to the system, and the automobile speed the response. A computer program
for the automated diagnosis of electrocardiograms can be viewed as a system which has as its input a digitized electrocardiogram and which predoces estimates of parameters suth
A as heart rate as outputs. camera is a system that receives light from different sources A and reflected from objects and produces a photograph, robot arm is a system whose
movements are the response to control inputs. In the many contexts in which signals and systems arise, there are a variety of prob-
lems and questions that are of importance. In some cases, we are presented with a specific system and are interested in characterizing it in detail to understand how it will respond to
various inputs. Examples include the analysis of a circuit in order to quantify its response to different voltage and current sourue^i and the determination of an aircraft's response
characteristics both to pilot commands and to wind guais. In other problem.^ of .sjgnal and sysiem aaalysi^, rather than an^yzing existJng sys-
tenis£ our interest may be focused on dcijigning systems lo process signals in piirticular ways_ One very comncion context in whicti such problems arise is in the design of systems to enhance or restore signals that have been degraded in some way. For example, when
a pilot is communicating with an air trafHc control tower, the coimniinication can be de-
graded by die high level of background noisfe in the cY;ckpiL In this and many similar cases,
it is possible to design systems that will retain the desired sigiul, in this case the pilot's
A voice, and reject (at least approximately) the unwanted Signal, i.e., the noise. simile
set of objectives can also be found in the general area of image restoration and image enhancement. For example, images from deep space pmbes or earth-observing satellites typically represent degraded versions of the scenes being imaged because of limitations uf the imaging equipment, atmospheric effects, and errors in signal transmission in retuming the images to earth. Consequently, images returned from space are routinely pnH.cssed by systems to compensate for some of these degradarions. In addition, sjcii images are usu-
xxiii

jociv

Fore\ËOrd

ally processed lo enhance cermin features, sucb as lines (correfipondmg, foi example, to nver b^ds oi faults) or regional boundaries in which there are sharp contraNts in oolor or
darkness.
In addition to enh^cemeni ai^d restoration, in many apphcations there is a need to design systems to extract specific pieces of information from signals. The cstimstion of
heart rate from an elettrocardiograin is one examplcK Another arises in economic forecast-
ing. We may. for example, wish to analyze the history of an economic time seties, such as
a set of sttjck market averages, in order to estimate trends and other characteristics such
a& ^asonal \aiiHtions that may be (if use in making predicnons about Future behavior. In other applications, Th¨ focas may be on the design of signals with particular properties.
Specifically, in communicadotis applications considerable attention is paid to designing
signals to meet the constraints and requirements for successful transmission. Forexan:ple, long distance coramunic^ion through the atmosphere requires the use of signals with frequencies in a particular part of the electromagnetic spectrum. The design of communication signals must also take into account the reed for reliable reception in the presence of both distortion due to transmission through the atmosphere and interference from other signals being transmitted simultaneously by other users.
Another verj important class of applications in which the concepts and techniques
of signal and system analysis arise are those in which we wish to modify or control the
characteristics of a given system, perhaps through the choice of specific input signals or by combining the system with other systems. Illustrative of this kind of application is the design of control systems to regulate chemjcal processing plants. PlariL*i of this type are equipped with a variety of sensors that measure physical signals such as temperature, hu-
midity, and chemical cotnposLdon. The control system in ^uch a plant responds to thes^ sensor signali^ by adjusting quantities such as flow rates and temperature in order to regulate the ongoing chemical process. The design of aircraft autopilots and computer contrcl
systems represents another example. In this case, signals measuring aircraft speedy altitude^ and heading are used by the aircraft^s control system in order to adjust variables such
as throtLle setting and the position of the rudder and ailerons. These adjustments are made
to ensure that the aircraft follows a specified course, to smooth out the aircraft's ride, and to enhance its responsiveness to pilot commands. In both this case and in the previous example of chemical process control, an important concept, referred to as feedback, plays a major role, as measured signals are fed back and used to adjust the response characteristic i
of a system.
The examples in the preceding paragraphs represent only a few of an extraordinarily wide variety of applications for the concepts of signals and systems. The importance of these concepts stems not only from the diversity of phenomena and processes in which they arise, but also from the collection of ideas, analytical techniques, and methodologies that have been and are boing developed and used to solve problems involving signals and
systems. The history of this development esiends back over many centuries, and although most of this work was nwtivated by specific applications, many ofthese itieas have proven
to be of central importance to problems in a far larger variety of contexts than those for which they were originally intended. For example, the tools of Fourier analysis, which
form the basis for the frequency-domain analysis of signals and systems, and which we
will develop in sotne detail in this book, can be traced from problems of astronomy studied by the ancient Babylonians to the development of mathematical physics in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

Foreword

xxv

In some of the e^amples that we have mejitionedj the signals vary continuously in
tinie, ^^he^KL^ in others, their evolution is described only at discrete points in time. For
example, in the analysis of electrical circuits and mechanical systems we are concerned with signals that vary continuously. On the other hand, the daily closing stock maiiet
average is hy its very nature a signal that evolves at discrete j>oinls in time (i.e., at the
close of each day). Rather than a curve as a function of a concinuous variable, then^ the
closing ^tock market average is a sequence of numbers associated with the discrete time itistants at which it is specified. This disttnctioti in the basic description of the evolution of
sigitals and of the systems that respond to or process these signals leads naturally to two parallel frameworks for signal and system analysis, one for phenomena and processes that are d&scribed in continuous time and one for those that are described iti discrete time.
The t{mcepts and techniques associated both with contitiuous-dme signals and systems and with discrctotime signals and systems have a rich history and are conceptually
closely related. Historically, however, because their applications have in the past been suf-
ficiently diflerent, they have for the most pan been siudied and developed somewhat sepa-
rately. Continuous-time signals and systems have very string rooU in p^oblcmi^ associated with physics and, in the more recent past, with electrical circuits and communications. Tho tEJchniques of discrete-time signals and systems have strong roots in numerical analysis. statistics, and time- series analysis associated with such applications as the analysis of economic and demographic data. Over the past several decades, however, the discipline*
of continuous- time and discrete-lime signals and systems have become increasingly entwined and the applications have become highly inierreUted, The major impetus for this
m has come from the dramatic advances technology for the implementation of systems
and for the generation of signals. Specifically, the continuing developmem of high-speed
digital computet^, integrated circuits, and sophisti^^ated high-density device fabrication
techniques has made it increasingly advantageous to consider processing continuous-time signals by representing them by time samples ti,e,, by converting them to discreie-time signals). As one example, the computer control systern for a modem high-performance
aircraft digitizes sensiir outputs such as vehicle speed in order to produce a sequence of sampled measurements which are then processed by the control system.
Because of the growing interrelationship between continuous-time ^gnals and sys-
tems and discrete-time signals and systems and because of the close relationship among the concepts and techniques associated with each, we have chcsen in this text to develop the concepb of continuous-time and discrete-time i^ignals and systems in parallel. Since many of the Cfincepts are similar (bot not identical), by tre^iting them in paratleL, insight and intuition can be shared and both the similarities and differences between them become better focused. In addition* as will be evident as we proceed through the material, there are some concepts that are inherently easier to understand in one fran>ework than the other
and, once understood, the iasight is easily transferable. Furthermore, this parallel treatment
m greatly facilitates our understanding of the very important practical context which cot^-
tmuoLis and discrete time are brought together, namely the sampling of continuous-time signals and the processing of continuous-time signals using discrete-time systems.
As we have so far described them, the notions of signals and systems are extremely
general concepts. At this level of generality, however, only the most sweeping statements can be made about the nature of signals and systems, and their properties can be discussed
only in the most elementary terms. On the other hand, an important and fundamental notion
in dealing with signals and systems is that by carefully choosing subclasses of each with

aocui

Foreword

p^kul^ properties that can then be exploited, we can analy^^e and characterize these
signals and systems in great depth. The principal focns in this bouk is on the particular class of linear time -invariant systems. The properties of linearity and time invanance that defjne this diss lead to aremarkahle set of concepts and techniques which are not only of
major practical importance but also analycicall^ tractable and intellectually satisfying.
As we have emphasized in this foreword^ signal and system analysis has a long his-
tory out of which have emerged some basic techniques and fundamenul principles which have extremely broad areas of application. Indeed, signal and system analysis is constantly
evolving and developing in refiponse to new problems, techniques, ^ind opponunities, We
fully expect fhis development to accelerate in pace as improved technology make^ posMble the implementation of increasingly complex systems and signal processmg techniques
Ln the future we will see signals and systems tools and concepts applied to an expanding scope of applications, hor these reasons, we feel that the topic of signal and system analy-
sis represents a body of knowledge that is of essential concern to the seienttst and engineer
We have chcsen the set of topics presented in this book, the argani^ation of the preseE-
tatJon, and the problems in each chapter in a way that we feel will most help the reader
to obtam a scrlid foundation in the fundamentals of signal and system analysis; to gain an understanding of some of the very important and basic applications of these fundamentals to problems in filtering, sampling, communications, and feedback system analysis; and to develop some appreciatlDn for an extremely powerful and broadly applicable approach to formulating and solving complex pmblems.

1 Signals and systems

1.0 INTRODUCTION
As described in the Fc}rewoTd, the intuitive notitms ot signals and systems dnae in a rich Va-
riety of contexts. Moreover, as we will see in this book^ there is an analytical framework
that IS, a language for describing signals and system.^ andanextren^ely powerful set of tools
-- for aualyzing them that applies equally well to problems in inany fields. In this chapter^
we begin our development &f the analytical fr<imework for signals and syirems by introducing their mathematical description arid representations. In the chapters that follow, we
build on this foundation in order to develop and describe additional concepts and methods that add considerably both to our underetanding of signals and systems and to our ability to analyze and solve problems involving signals and systems that arise in a broad array of
applications.

1.1 COISfTINUOUS-TlME AND DISCRETE-TIME SIGNALS

1

Examples and Mathematical Representation

variations of some form. Forexample, consider the simple circuit in Figure l,K In this case,

over time

the human vocal mechanism, which produces speech by creating fluctuations in acous-

tic pressure. Figure 1,3 is an illustration of a recording of such a speech stgnal, obtained by

Signals ar^' represented matheTnatically as functions of one or irtcte independent variables. For example, a speech signal can be represented mathematically by acoii&tic pressure as a fnnciion of time, and a picture can be repreijented by brightness* as a func-
tion of two spatial variables. In this book, we focus our attention on signals involving a
single independent variable. For convenience* v/e will generally refer to the independent
variable as time, although it may not in fact represent time in specific applications. For
example, in geophysics, signals representing variations with depth of physical tjuantities sucli as density, porosity, an<i electrical resisdvity are used to study the structure of the earth. Also, knowledge of the variations of air pressure, temperature, and wind speed with altitude are e;(tremel> important in meteorological investigations. Figure L5 depicts atyp-
ical esarnple of annual average vertical wind profile as a flmction of height. The measured variations of wind speed with height are used in examining weather pattemsn as well as wind conditions that may affect an aircraft during final approach and landing.
Throughout this hook we will be considering two basic types of signals: continuons-
time signals and discrete-time signals. In the case of continuous-time *agnals the independent variable is continuous, and thus these signal s are defined for a continuum of values

1 .2 TRANSFORMATIONS OF THE INDEPENDENT VARIABLE
A central concept in signal and system analysis is that of the transformation of a signal.
For example, in an aircraft control system, signals corresponding to the actions of the pilot are transformed by elecuical and mechanical systems into changes in aircraft thrust or the positions of aircraft control surfaces iuch as the rudder or ailerons, which in lorn are transformed thTT>ugh the dynamics and kinematics of the vehicle into changes in aircraft velocity and heading. Also, in a tiigh-fidelity audio system, an input signal representing music as recorded on a cassette or compact disc is modiJied in order to enhance desirable characteristics, Lo remove recording noise, or to balance the several components of the
signal (e,g,, treble and bass). In this section, we focus on a very limited butimpi>rtant class
of elementary signal transformations that involve simple modificaticm of the independent
variable, i.e., the time a\is. As we will see in this and subsequent sections of this chapter,
these elementary transformations allow us to introduce several basic properties of signals
m and systems. In later chapters, we will find thet they also play an hnpottantmle defining
and character! King far richer and important classes of systems.

7 .2.2 Periodic Signals

An important clasis of signals that we will encinunter frequently throughout this book is
A the class ^Ipenodic signals. periodic continnous-time signal jU) has the property that
there is a positive value of T for which

= + x{r)

j:(/ T)

tl.ll)

for all values of r. In other words, a periodic signal has the property that it is unchanged by a
time shift of T. In this case, we say that i(0 \spenodic with period T Periodic continuous-
time signals arise in a variety of contexts. For example, as illustrated Jt^ Problem 2.61,
the natural response of systems in which energy is conserved, such as ideal LC circuits
without resistive energy dissipation and ideal mechanical systems without frictional losses,
are periodic and, in fact, are composed of some at the basic periodic signals that wc will
introduce in Section 1.3.

While there are many similarities between continiiou5-^time and discrete-time signals, there are also a number of important <^fFerences, Oiie i>f these c<>ncems the discrete-tiioe e>;p[)nential signal e-''^"". In Section 1.3.1, we identified the following two properties of iu

are de^nite differenties between each of these and its contLnuous-time counterpart. The fact that the first of the&e properties is different in discrete time i* a direct conse-
quence of another extremely important distinction between discrete-time and continuoustime complex cxpenentiaU. Spet^ificaJly* consider the discrete-time complex exponential with frequejicy <tju + 27r:

In contrast, consider che signal \[ti] = cos{S7t/31), depicted in Figure 1.25(b\ which we can view as the set of samples of M/) = cos<87r^/3I) at itiieger points in time. In this case, x(t) is periodic with fiindamental period 31/+. On the other hand, x\7i] li pe-
nodic with fundamental period 31, The reason for this difference is that the discrete-time
signal is defined only for integer values of the independent variable. Thus, there is no
sample at time I = 31/4, when x(Ocoti^pletesone period [starting fromf = 0). Similarly, ihere is no sample at r - 2 31/4 or r - 3 - 31/4. when x{t) has completed two or three periods, but there is a sample at È ^ 4-31/4 ^ 31, when x(0 has completed /our perioJs.
This can be seen in Figure 1.25(b), where the pattern of j|nj values does not repeat with each single cycle of positive and negative values. Rather, the pattern repciits after tour such iryclcs, munely, every 31 points.
SimilarLy, the signaL xIk] = cos(rt/6) can be viewed as the set of samples tjf the signal xH) = cos(//6) at integer lime points. In this case, the values of x(r) at integer
sample points never repeat, as theic sample poinbi never ^ipsin an interval that is an exjci multiple of the period, 127r, cf x{ry Thus, x[n] is not periodic, although the eye visually interpolates between the sample pointn, suggesting the envelope x{n, which is periodic.

Although ojr tliscussioti af the unit impulse in this section has been somewliat infcimial, it does provide us with some important intuition about this signal that vvLll be
useful throughout the book. As we have stated, the nnit impulse shouM be viewed as an idealization. As we illustrate and discuss in more detail ir. Section 2.5, any real physi-
cal system has sonae inertia associated with it and thiis doe> not respond instantansously to inputs. Coitsequently, if a pul&e of sufficiently short duriilion \^ applied to such a system, the system response will not be noticeably influenced by the pulse's duration or by the details of the shape of the pulse> for that matter. Instead, (he primary charat:tenstit;
-- of the pulse that will matter \b the net, integrated effect of ibe pulse i.e., Jts area. For
systems that respond much more quickly than others, the gylse will hiive to be of much
shorter liuratiun before the details af the pulse shape or its duration njo longer mailer Nev-
-- ertheless, for any phy^jical system, we can always find a pulse that is *'short enough/
The unit impulse then is an idedization of this concept L'le puls^ that is short enough
for any system. As we wih see in Chapter 2, the response of a Eysiera to this idealized
pulse plays a crucial role in iiignal and system analysis, ind in the process of developing and understanding this role, vfe will develop additional insight into the idealised
signal.^

1.5 CONTlNUOUS-nMEAND DISCRETE-TIME SYSTEMS

Physical systenis in the broadest sense are sj\ interconnection of components, devices,

ar subsystems. In contexts ranging from signal processing and communications to elec-

tromechanical motors, automotive vehicles, and chemical-processing plants, a yy.\tem can

be viewed as a process in which input signals are transformed by the system or cause the

system to respond in some way, resulting in other signals as outputs. For example, a high-

fidebty system takes a recorded audio signal and generates a reproduction of that signal

If the hi-fi system has tone controls, we can change the tonal quality of the reproduced sig-

vM nal. Similarly, the circuit in Figure LI can be viewed as a system with input voltage

vM, and output voltage

while the automobile in Figure 1 .2 can be thought of as a system

with input equal to the force /<r) and output equal to the velocity vit) of the vehicle. An

image-enhaticemetit system transforms an input image into an output image that ha.s some

desired properties, such as improved contrast.

A continutfus-time system is a system in which continuous-time input signals are

applied and result in continuous-time output signals. Such a system will be represented

pictoriaUy as In Figure h41(a), where xit) is the input and yit) is die output. Alternatively, we will often represent the inputoutput relation of a continuous-tmie system by the

notation

41

As the preceding examples suggest, the mathematical descriptions of systems froin

a wide variety of applicationii frequently have a great deal in common, and it is this fact

thai provides considerabJe motivation for the deveJopment of broadly applicable tools for

signal and system analysis The key to doing this ^iuccessfully is identifying cliLsses of

systems that have two important characteristics: (1) The systems in this class have prop-

erties and strut^tureK that we can exploit to gain insight into their behavior and to develop

effective tools for their analysis; and (2) many systems of practical imponance can be

accurately modeled using systems in this class. It is on the first of these characteristics

that most of this btjok fycuses, as we develop tools for a panicular class of systems re-

ferred to as linear, time-invariant systems. In the next section, we will introduce the piop-

crtie-^ that characterize this tlass^ as well as a number of uther very important ba^sit; system

properties.

The second characieristic mentioned in the preceding paragraph is of obvious impor-

tance for any system analysis technique to be of value in practice. It is a well-established

fact that a wide range of physical systems (including those in Examples 1. 8-1. 10) can
m be v^ell modeled within the class of systems on which we focus this book. However,

a critical point is that any model used in describing o' anaJy^ing a physical system lep-

rcsenis an idealization of that system, and thus, any resulting analysis is only as good

m as the model itself For example, the simple linear niwdel of a resistor

e^j. (1 80)

and that of a capacitor in eq. (1.81) are idealizations. However, these idealizations are

quite accurate for real resistors iind capacitors in many applications, and thus, analy-

ses employing such idealizations provide useful results and conclusions, as long a.s the voltages and ctinrents remain within the operating conditions under which these simple

linear models are valid. Similarly, the use of a linear retarding force to represent fnc-

tional effecti in eq. (1.83) is an appro ximation with a range of validity. Consequently,
although we will not address this issue in the book, it is important to remember that an essential component of engineering practice in using the methods wc develop here

consists of identifying the range of validity of the assumptions that have gone into a model and erisuring that any analysis or design based on that model does nat violate those

assumptions.

1 ,5,2 Interconnections of Systems
An important idea that wc will use throughout this book is the concept of the interconnecdon of systems. Many real systems are built as interconnections of several subsystems. One example i.s an audio system, which involves the intercomicction of a radio receiver,
compact disc player, or tapodeck with an amplifier and one or more speakers. Another is a digitally controlled aircraft, which is an interconnection of the aircraft, described by its equaiions of motion and die aerodynamic forces affecting it: the sensors, which measure various aircraft variables such a.s accelerations, rotation rates, and heading; a digital au-
topilot, which responds to the measured variables and to command inputs from the pilot
(e.g., the de^sired course, altitude, and speed); and the aircraft's actuators, which respond to inputs provided by the autopilot in order to use the aircraft control surfaces (rudder,
tail, ailerons) to change the aerodynamic forces on the aircraft. By viewing such a system as an interconnection of its components, we can use oiu- understanding of the component

systems and of how they are interconnected in order to analyze the operatjon and behavior

of the overall system. In addition, by descnbing ii system in terms of an interconiiecdoti of
simpler subsystems, we may in fact be able to define useful ways in which tu synthesize
complex systems out of simpler, basic building blocks.

in (he figure denotes

addition, so that the output of the parallel interconnection is the sum of the outputs of Systems 1 and 2. An example of a parallel interconnection is a simple audio system with

of more than two systems, and we can combine btitb cascade and parallel intcrtronnections

other than the current time. For example, the delay in eq, (1-93) mpst retain or store the preceding value ol the inpuf. Similarly^ the accumulalcr in eq. (1 92) tnust "remember" of store mformation atwut past inputs. In particular, the accumulator computes the running

In many physical systems, memory is directly associated with the storage ot energy.

and output values, our formal definition also leads to our referring to a system as having

systems having diis dependence on future values inight at first seem unnatural, (hey in fact
form an iniip>ortant c^ass of systems, as we discuss further in Section 1.6.3.

1.6,3 Causality
A system is causal if the output at any time depends only on values of the input at the
present time and in the past. Such a system is often referred to as being notutnticipative, as

the system output does not anticipate future values of the input Consequently, if two inputs toa cau&a] system are ideniica] up to ^ome point in time ^o t>rno, the corresponding outputs
must also be equal up to this same time. The RC circuit of Figure 1,1 is causal, since
the capacitor voltage responds only to the present and past values of the source voltage Similarly, the motion of an automobile is causal, since it doe* r\iA anticipate future actions of the driver. The systems described in eqs. (1.92) - (1.94) are also causal, but The systems defined by

1.6.4 Stabifity
Smbility is another important system property. InformaJly^ a stable system is one in which smalL inputs lead tt> responses that do not diverge. For eKample, consider the pendulum in Figure 1.46(a), in which the input is the appJied Force x(t) and the output is the angular deviation y{t) from the vertical. In this case, gravity applies a restoring force that tends to return the penduluiri to the vertical position^ and frictional losse,'^ due to drag tend to slow it down. Consequently, if a small force xir) is appJied, the resulting deflection from vertical will also be small. In contract, for ihe inverted pendulum in Figure l.46(bX the effect of gravity is to apply a force that tends to fnt^fieajf the dtviadonfrom vertical. Thus, a small applied force leads to a large vertical deflection causing the pendulum to topple over, despite any retarding forces due to fricti&n.
The system in Figure 1 .46(a) is an example of a stable system, while that in Figure 1 .46(b) is unstable. Models for chain reactions or for population growth with unlimited food supplies and no predators are examples of unstable systems, since the system response grows without bound in response to small inputs. Another example of an unstable system is the model fora bank account balance in eq, (1.85), since if an initial deposit
IS made (i.e,£ ^[0] = a positive amount) and there are no subsequent withdrawals^ then
that deposit will grow each month without bound, because of the compounding effect of
interest payments.

figure i .46 (a) A stable pendulum;
(b) an unstable inverted pendulum

There are also numerous examples of stjible systems. St^ility of physical systems

dissipates energy and this circuit is a stable system. The system in Eximple 1 9 is also

The preceding examples provide us with an intuitive understanding of che concept

of stahihty. More formally, if the input to a stable system is bounded (i.e., if its magnitude

docs not grow without bound), then the output mnst also be bounded and therefore cannot
diverge. This is the definition of stability that we will use throughout this hook. For exam-
m ple, consider applying a constant force fit) = FXo the aucomobile Figure 1 2. with the

vehicle iniiiaUy at rest. In this case the velocity of the car will increase, but not without

t -6,5 Time Invctriance

Conceptually, a syst&in is time invariant if the behavior and characteristics of the system

RC are fijted over time. Ft>r example, the

circuit of Figure 1.1 is tinK invariant if the

re^stance and capacitance values R and C are constant over time: We would expeot to

get the same resuJts from an experiment with tbi^ circuit today as we would if we ran the
C identical eT^peiimeni tomorrow. On the other hantl if the values of li and are changed

or fluctuate over time, then we would expect the results of our experiment to depend on
m the tinie at which we run it. Similarly, if the frictional coefficient b and mass of the

automobile in Figure 1.2 are constantf we would expect the vehicle to respond identically

independcEidy of whan we drive it. On the other hand, if we load the auto's trujik with

heavy stiitcases one day, thus increasing m, we would eitpect the car to behave differently

than at other times when it is not so heavily loaded.

The property of time invanance can be descKbed very simply in terms of the signals

and systems language that we have introduced. Specificaliy, a system is time invariant if

1.6.6 Lpnearity
A Uneaf system, in c^intinuouFi time or discrete timSi is a system that possesses the impor-
tant property of superposition: If an input consists of the weighted sum of several signals,
-- -- then the output is the superposition that is, the weighted sum of the responses of the
system to each of those sign^l^. More precisely, let >'|(0 be the response of a continuous
hme system to an inpuc xi{t% and let y2{t) be the output corresponding to the input ^^(OThen the s>stem is linear if:

1. The response to x^it} + xjit) iiy\(t) + ytit).
1. The response Lo axy(t) i$ ayi(t), vi^here a is any complex constant.

T^e first of these two properties is known as the additivity property; the second is knoivn us the scaling ^t homogeneity property. Although we have written this description using continuous-time signals, the same definition holds in discrete time. The systems specified by eqs. (1.91H1 100), (1,102)-(1.104X and (1,119) are linear, while those defined by
eqs. (1.101) and (1.114) are nonlinear. Note that a system can be linear without being time invariant, as in eq, (1.119), and it can be time invariant without being Imcar, as in eqs. (l.lt)l) and (1.114).
The two properties defining a linear system can be combined into a single statement

continuous time: ax\{l^ + bx^it} -^ ay\{t) + by^ii). discrete time: a.Ti[n] + &J2["] --^i^yd'^^ + by2\.n\.

(1.121) (1.122)

= the definition of linearity that if rVÈ[n], k

k

This very important fact is known as the superposition property^ which holds for linear
systems in both continuous and discrete time.

input which is zero for all time results in an output which is zero for all time. For example, if -*["]--£>[¨], then the homogeneity property tells us that

1.7 SUMMARY

In this chapter, we have developed a number of basic concepbi related to continuous-tiTne

We anddiscrete'titne signals and systems.

have pres&nted both an intuitive picture of what

signals and systems ar^ through several examples and a mathematical representation for signals and systems that we will use throughout the book. Specifically, we introduced

graphical and mathematical representations of signals and used these representations in

We performing transformations of the independent variable.

aiso defined and exainiiied

several basic signals, both in continuous time and in discrete time. These included cotn-

plex exponential sigtials, sinusoidal signals, and unit impulse and step functions. In ad
dition, we investigated the concept of periodicity for continuous-time and dificrete-time

signals.

In develtjping sotne of die ekmentary ideas related to systems, we introduced bl(>ck

diagrams to facilitate our discussions concerning the interconnection of systems, and we

defined a number of important prnperties of systems, including causality, stability, time

invariance, and linearity.

The primary ft>cus in most of this book will be on the class of linear, time- in variant

(LTI) systems, both in continuotis time and in discrete time. These systems play a par-

ticularly important role in system analysis and design, in part due to the fact that many

systems encotititered in nature can be successfully modeled as linear and time invariant, Furthernnore, as we shall see in the following chapters, the properties of Imeanty and Lime

invariance allow us to analy7e in detail the behavior of LTI systems.

1.39, The role played by u{t\ Sit), and other singularity functions in die study of linear time-invariant systems is that of an idealization of a physical phenomenon, and* as we will see, the use of these idealizations aJlow us to obtain an exceedingly important and very simple representation of such systems. In using singuliirity functions*
we need, however, to be careflil , In panicular, we must remember that they are ideali;Kation&^ ^uid thus, whenever we perform a caJcuIation using dtem, we are implicttZy
assuming that ihis calculation represents an accurate description of the behavior of the signals that they are intended to i^ali^. To illustrate, consider the equ^tLon

Linear time -invariant systems
2.0 JNTRODUCTION
In Section I 6 we iticn>duced and discussed a mimber of basic system properties, Twg of
theic, linearity and time invariance, play a fundamental role in signal and system analysis for two major reaiioos. First, tnany physical processes possess these properties and thus can be modeled as linear lime-invariant (LTIJ s>steii)&. In addition, LTI systems can be analysed in considerable detail, providing both insighc into their properties and a set of powerful tx>ols that form the core of signal and system anaJy.sis.
A principal objective of this book is to develop an aoderstandinE of these proper
ues and tools and to provide an introduction to several of the very important applications
in which die tools are used. In diis chapter, we begin the development by deriving and
e^amimng a fundamental and exLremely useful representation for LTI system*^ and by in-
troducing an important clasft of these systems.
One of the primary reasons LTI systems are amenable to analysis is diat any such system possesses the superposition propMty described in Section 1 .6.6. As a consequence, if we can represent the input to sn LTI system in terms of a linear cojnbination of a set of basic signals, we can then use superposition to compute the output of the system in terms
of its responses to these basic signals.
As we v^'ill see in the following sections, one of the important characteristics of the
unit iir^ulse, both in discrete time and in continuous titne, is that very general signals can be represented as linear combinations of delayed impulses. This fact, together with
the properties of superposition and time invariance, will allow us to develop a complete
m characterization of any LTI system temis of its response to a unit impulse. Such a
representation* referred to as the convolution sum in the discrete-rime case and the convo-
luiion integral in continuous time, provides considerable analytical convenience in dealing
74

Sec. 2.1

DisCfete-Ti[ne LTj Systems: The Convolulittn Sum

75

with LTI systems. FolJowing ^jur devel^jpment of the convolution sum and the convolution integral we use these charactehzatiofls to examine some of the other properties of LTI sys-
tems. We then oonsider the class of continuous time systems described by lin&ar constant-

coefficient diffcrerti^ equations and it^t di&crete-time counterpart, the class of systems

We described by linear constant-coefficient difference equations.

will return to examine

these two very inipoitatit classes of systems on a number of occasiotis in subsequent chap-

a number of Hjiher signals that arc cbsely related to it in order to provide sotne additional

insight into these ideaJized signals and, in paiticular, to their use arid interpretation in the

ct>ntext of analyzing LTI systems,

The im[>oriai]ce of the sifting property of eq^s. (2,1) and (22) lies in the fact that it repie-

s^nts x[n] as a superposition of scaled versions ofa verj' simple setof elemenlary funt;tions,

namely, shifted unit impulses b[n - k], each of which is nonzero (with value 1 ) at a single

point in Lime specified by the corresponding value of Jt. The response af a linear system

to x[n] will be the .superposition of the scaled responses of the system to each of these

shifted impulses. Moreover, the property of time invariance tells us thai the responses of a

time-invariant system to the time-shifted unit impulses are simply lin^e-shifted versions of

one another. The convolution -puna representation for discrete-time systems that arc both

linear and time invariant results from putting these two basic facts together

Discrete'Time LT Systems The Convolution Sum

SI

By considering the effect of the superposition sum on each individual output sample^

we obtain another very useful way to \isualiie thccaJculationof >[¨] using the convolution
A sujh. In particular, consider the evaluation of the oiitput value at some specific time n.

particularly ccinvenient way of displaying this calculation graphically begins with the two

signaJs j:[k] and k[n - k] viewed as functions of jt. Multiplying these two functions^ we

nbtain a sequence g[k] = x\k\h\n -- k], which, at each time k, is seen to represent the

We contribution of jif[Jt] to the output at Lime n

conclude that summing all the samples

in the sequence of ^[k] yields the output value iit the elected time n. Thus, to calculate

y[n] for all values of n requires repeating this procedure for each value of ^r. Fortunately,

changing the value of n h<LS a very simple graphical interpretation for the two signals .T[k] and ^[rt - ;:!, viewed as functions ofjt. The following examples illustrate this and the use

of the aforementioned viewpoint in evaluating convolution sums.

These examples illustrate the usefulness of visualizing the calculation of the con-
volution suiu gr^hically. Moreover, in addition to providing a useful way in which to calculate the response of sa LTI system^ the convolution sum also provi<les an extremely
useful representation for LTt systems that allows us to examine their properties in great
detail. In particular^ in Section 2.3 we will describe some of the properties of convolution
and will also examine some of the system properties Introduced in the previous chapter in order to see how these propertaes can be characterized for LTI systems.
2,2 CONTINUOUS-TTME LTf SYSTEWS; THE CONVOLUTION INTEGRAL
la analogy with the results derived and discussed in the preceding section, the goal of this section is to obtain a complete characterization of a continuous-time LTI system in terms
-- of its unit hnpulse response. In discrete lime, the key to our developing the convolution
sum was the sifting property of the discrete-time unit impulse that is, the maihenmica]
representation of a signal a^ the supeipositioi) of scaled and shifted unit impulse functions.
Intuitively, then, we can think of the discrete-lime systMn as responding to a sequence of individual impulses. In continuous time, of course, we do not have a discrete sequence of input values. Nevertheless, as we discussed in Section 1 .4.2, if we think of the unit im-
pulse as the idealization of a pulse which is so short that its duration is incousequentid for
any real, physical system, we can develop a representation for arhttrary continuous-time
signals in tertns of these idealized pulses with vanishingty small duration, or equivalently,
impulses. This representation is developed in the next subsec^on, and. following tbet, we wi^l proceed very much as in Section 2.1 to develop the convolution integral representation
for continuous-time LTI systems.

While we have chosen to use the same symbol ^ to denote beth discrete-time and

As in dist^rete times we see thai a continuous-time LTI system is completely char-

unit impulse 5(f), In the next section, we explore the implications of this as we examine

a number of the properties of convoludon and of LTI systems in both contiruous time and

discrete time.

The procedure for evaluating the convolution integral is quite similar to that f{>r its

2,3,3 The Associative Property
Another important and useful pioperty of convolulion is that it is cssaciative. That is. in
discrete time

and in contmuouit time

This property is proven by straightforward manipulations of the summations and integrals involved. Exaitiples verifying it are given in Probfem 2.43,
As i ccjoscquence of the associative |>ropeirtj, the expressions

and

yit) - xit)*h:iO*h2ii)

(2.61)

are iinambiguoiVis. That is, accor<fing to eqs. (2.58) and (2.59), it does not matter jn which
order we cotivolve these signals.
An interpretation of the associative property is illustrated for discrece-tiine systems
in Figures 2.25(a) and (b). It> Figure 2.25(a),

= y{n\

According to the associative propeny, the series intereonnection of the rwo systems in Figure 2.25(a) IS equivalent to the single system in Figure 2,25(b), This can be general ieeti CO ati arbitrary number of LTL systems in cascade, and the analogous interpretation and condusicm also hold in continuous time.
By using ttie commutative property together with the associative property, we find
another very important property of LTl systems. Specifically, from Figures 2.25(a) and
Èb), we can conclude that the impulse response of the cascade of two LTI systems Ls the convolution of their iitdividual impulse responses. Since convolution is commutative, we
can compute this convolution of /ii[h] and/i2[n] in either order Thus. Figures 2,2fi(b)and (c) are equivalent, and from the ^^sociitive property, these are in turn equivaieivt to the
.system of Figure 2,2-'5[d), which we note is a cascade combination of two system!?^ in
Figure 2.25(a), but with the order af the cascade reversed. Consequently, the unit impulse response of a cascade of two LTE systems does not depend on the order in which they are
cascaded. In fact, tfiis holds for an arbitrary number of LTI systeitis in cascade: The order in which they are cascaded does not matter as far as the overall system impulse response is concerned. The sanie conclusions hold in continuous time as welJ.

23.6 Causality for LTI Systems

Tn Section 1 6.3, we tt\troduHMd the property of causality: The output of a causal system depends only on the present and past values of the input to the system. By using the con-

volution sum and mtegral* we can relate this property Vft a corresponthng property of the

impulse response of an LTI ^ystetti. Specifically, in order for a discrete-time LTI system ro

k> be causal, y{Tt] must not depend on x[k] fi>r

n. From eq, ^2.!i9), ive see that for this in

> mu be true, al 1 of the cue Oi cj en tsh[n- k] that multi ply values of jc[ k] for k n

st be zero

This then requites ihat the impulse rcspotise of a causal diserete-time LTI system satisfy

the condition

h[n] -

forrt <0.

(2.77)

According to eq. (277), the impulse response of a causal LTI system rausi be zero before

the impulse occurs, which is consistent with the intuitive concept af causality. Mote gener-

ally, as shown in Problem 1 .44, causaJity for a linear system is equivalent to the condition

of mitiul rest. i,e,, if the input to a causal system is up to some poir^t in Lime, then the

output must also he up to that time. It is impiJitam to emphasize that the equivalence

of causality and tl^ condition of initial rest applies only to linear systems. For example,

a^ discus.sed in Section 1.6.6. ihe system ylrt) = 2xn\ -h 3 is not linear. However, it is

On causal and, in fact, memfjryless.

-- the other hand, if x\n\

0, y[n\ - 5 =?^ 0, so it does

not satisfy the CDnditioti of initial rest.

V-jt a causal discrete- time T^TI system, the condition in eq. (2.77> implies that the

convolution f,um representation in eq. (2.39) bectinies

2.4 CAUSAL LTI SYSTEMS DESCRtBEO BY OfFFERElNfTlAL
AND DfFFERENCH EQUATIONS

An extremely important class of continue us -time sy^iems is that for which [he input and

otilput are related through a Ihti'ar comtani-coejficient differential equanon liquations of

this type arise in the Jescription of a wide variety of systems and physical phenomena. For

RC example, as we iUustrattd in Chapter 1 , the response iif thÈ

circuit in Figure 1 , 1 and

the motion of a vehicle subject to acceleration inputs and fnctional fortes, as depicted in

Figure L2, can both be described through linear constant-coefficient differential equations.

Similar differential equations arise in the description tif mechanical systems c:{)ntaining

restoring and damping forces, in the kinetics of chemical reactions, and in many other

contexts as veil.

Correspondingly^ an important class of discrete-time sy.stems is that for which the input and output are related through a lineat constant-Cifefficient difference! equation. Equa-
tions of this type are used to describe the sequential behavior of many different processes. For instance, in Example 1.10 we saw how difference equations arise in describing the accumulation of savings in a bank account, and in Example l.U we saw how they can

be used to describe a digital simulation of a continuous -time system described by a dif-

ferential equation. Difference equations also arise qu^te frequently in the specification of

discrete-time systems designed to perform particular operations on the input signal F<ir
m e\ai.iple> the system that calculates the diiTerence between successive input \alues, as
eq, (1.99), and the system described by eq. i 1 .104) that computes the average value of the input over an interval are described by difference equations.

Througliout this book, there will be many occasions in which we will consider and
examine systems described by linear constant-coefficient differential and difference equa-
tions. In this section we take a tirst look at these systems tc* introduce some of the basic

ideas involved in solving differential and difference equations and to uncover and explore
some of the properties of systems described by such equations. Jn subsequent chapters, we
develop additional tools for the analysis i>f signals and systems that will add considerably

btJth to our ability to analyze systems described by such equations and to our understanding ot their characteristics and behavior.

2.4.1 Unea r Con stant-Coeffi cient DifferentiaJ Eq uation^
To introduce some of the important ideas concerning systems specilied by linear constant-
coefficient differential equations, let usconsiHec a first-order differential equation as in eq. (1.85), viK.

-^^- -H 2y(0 = xisX

(2.95)

where yir') denotes the output of ihe system and jf(r) is the input. For example, comparing
eq, (2,y5) to the differential equation (1,B4) for the velocity of a vehicle subject to applied
and frictinnal forces, we see that eq. (2.95) would correspond exactly to this system if
y(0 were identi^ed wilh the vehicle's velocicy v'(r), if jc(f) were taJien as the applied force
m /(r), and if the parameters in eq. (1.84) were normalized uniL*; such that bltn = 2 and
1/m = I.
A very imp^nant point abom differential equations such an eq. (2.95) is. that they
provide an implii.it specificatian of the system. That is, they describe a relationship between the input and the output, rather than an e?(plicit ej^pression for the system output
as a function of the input. Jti order to obtain an e?tplicit expression, we must solve the differeniiaJ equation. To firid a solution, we need more information than that pnjvided by
the differential equation alone. Fur example, to determine the speed of an automobile at
the end of a 10-second interval when it has been subjected to a constant acceleration of I m^sec^ for 10 seconds, we would also need to >:jiow how fasi the vehicle was moving at the jwrr of the intervaL Similarly, if we are lold that a constant source voltage of I volt is applied to the /?C circuit in Figure 1.1 for 10 seconds, we cannot determine what the capacitor voltage is at the end of that interval without also knowing what the initial capacitor
voltage is.
More generally, to solve a differential equation, we must specify one or iiMire auxiliary conditions, and once these are specified, we can then, in principle, obtain an explicit
expression for the output in termsof the input, fn other words, a differential equation such as eq. (2.95) describes a constraint between the input and thtf output of a system, but to
characterize the system completely, we must also specify auxiliary conditions. Different
choices for these auxiliary conditions then lead to different relationships between the in-
put and tht output. Fcr the most part£ in this book we will focus on the use of differentia]
equations to describe causal LTI systems, and for such systems the auxiliary conditions take a particular, simple form. To illustrate this and to uncover some of the basic properties of the solutions to differential equations^ let us take a look at the solution of eq. (2.Q5) for a specific input %ignat ;t(/)/

ample, for the most part we will use the condition of initial rest for systems described

It is important to emphasize that the condition of initial rest does not specify a zero

consider again the ciicuit in Figure 1.1, also discussed in Example 1 .8. Initial rest for this

example corresponds to the statement that, until we connect a nonzero voltage source to the

cirtuit, the capacitor voltage is zero. Thus, if we begin to use the circuit at noon today, the

mjtial capacitor voltage as we connect the voltage source at noon today is ^ero. Similiiriy,

if we begin to use the circuit at noon tomorrow instead, die initial capacitor voloage a^ we

connect the voliage source at ntxm toniorrow is zero.

This example also provides us with some intuition as to why the condition of LTiitial

rest makes a liystem described by a linear constant -cocftic ient differential eqimtion lime

invariant. For example, if we perform an experiment on the circuit, starting from initial
rest, then, assuitiing that the coefficients R and iTdon^t change over time, we would expect

to ÈCt the same results whether we ran the experiment today or tomorrovv_ That is, if we

perform identical expentncnts on the two days* where tlie circuit starts frojn initial rest at
-- noon on each day, then we would expect to see identical resptmses i.e , responses that

are simply time-shifttjd by one day with respect to each other.

An equation of the form of eq, (2,113) oreq. (2.115) is called a r^curjiive equanon,
since it specifies a recursive procedure for determining the output in terms of the input and
previous outputs. In the special case when A^ = 0, eq. (2, 1 1 5) reduces to

y[¨] ^Sf-W^'tJ,

(2.116)

This is the disciete-time counterpart of the continuous-time system given in eq. (2. UO), Here, y[n\ is an explicit function of the present and previous values of the inpul. For this
reason, eq, (2.116) is often called a nonrecursive equation, smce we do not recursively use previously computed values of the output to compute the present value of the output. Therefore, jtist as in the case of the system given in eq. (2.11(5), we do not rteed auxiliary
cunditions in order to determine y{n]. Furlhennore, eq, {2, 11 6) describes an LTl system, and by direct cortiput^tion, the impulse response of this system is fcund ti) be

y 0, otherwise

Thai is, ¨[. (2.116) is nothing more than the convolution sum. Note that the impulse re-

sponse forit has finite duration; that is, it is nonzerti cinly over a finite time interval. Because

of this property, the System specified by eq. (2.116)is often cabled ^finite impulse rej^ponse

{fia) system.

2.4.3 BPocfc Diagram Representations of FirstOrder Systems Described by Differentiar sna Difference Equations
An importiint property of systems described by linear cons tant-coeffi dent difference and
differential equations is that they can be represented in very simple and natural ways in terms of block diagram interconnections of elememary opi^rations. Thni is significant
f<>T A nuTvibeT i>f reasons. One is ^hat it pnivides a pictorial representatior ^vhich can add
to our understanding of the behavior and properties nf these systems In addition, such representations can be of considerable value for the simulation or implementation of the
m systems. For example, the block diagram representation to be introduced thjs section
for continuous -time systems is the basis for early analog computer simulations of systems described by differential equations, ai:d It can also be directly translated into a program for the .sjniijlatiiin of such a system on a digital computer. In addition, the corresponding representation for discrete-time difTerenee etjuations suggests simple and efficient ways in vvhich the systems that the equations describe can be implemented in digital hardwarc.

2.5 SINGULARITY FUNCTIONS
m In this section, we take another look at the continuous-time unit impulse function order
to gain additional incuitions about this important idealized signal and to introduce a set of
related signals known coUectively as singularity functions. In particular, in Section 1 .4.2 we suggested Q^at a continuous-time unit impulse could be viewed as the idealization of a pulse that is '*short enough" so that its shape and duration is of no practic^il consequence i.e., so that as far as the response of any particular LTI system is concerned, all of the area
under the pulse can be thought of as having been applied instantaneously. In this section,
we would first like to provide a concrete example of what this means and then use the
interpretation embodied vwthin the exatnple to show that the key to ihe use of unit im pulses
m and other singularity functions is the specification of how LTT systems respond to these
idealijKd signals; i.e., the signals are in essence defined in terms of how ihey behave under
convolution with other siignals.

2.5.2 Defining the Unit Impulse throu^ Convolution

As th& preceding example illusirates, for ^ smiJl enough, the signals S^(i), r\{t\ ri(0 +
d^{t), and rj<r) + taU) all act lite itnpalses when applied to an LTI system. In fact, there are many other signals for which this is true as well. What it suggests is that we should think of a unit impulse in terms of how an LT! system responds to it. While usually a function or signal is defined by what it is at each value of the independent variable, the primary importance of the unit impulse is not what it is at each value of r, but rather what it does under convolution. Thus, frot^ the point of view of Hnear systemfi analysis, we may alternatively define the unit impulse as that signal whith, when applied lo an LTI system, yields the impulse response. That is, we define S(r) as the signal for which
