2,6 SUMMARY
In this chapter, we have deveLoped important reprcscritations for LTI systems, both in discrete time and in continuous time. Iji discrete lime we derived a representation of signals as v^eighted sums of shifted unit impulses, and we then used this to derive the convolu rion sum representation for the response of a discrete-time LTI system In continuous time we
derived an analogous representation of concinncus-time signals as weighted integTal$ of
shifted unit impui^s. and we used this to derive the convolution integral representation
for continuous- time LIT systems These representations are extremely important, as they aJlow us to compute the response of an LTI system Lo an ajbitrary input in terms of the systern's response to a unit impulse. Moreover, in Section 2.3 the convotutiansjum and integral provided us with a means of analyzing the properties of LTI systems and, in particular, of relating LTI system properties, including causality and stability, to corresponding proper-
ties of the unit impulse response. Also, in Seetion 2.5 we developed an interpretation of
m the continuous-lime unit impulse and other related singularity functions terms of their
behavior litider convolution. TTris interpretation is particularly usefui in the arialysis of LTI
systems.
An important class of continuous-time systems consists of those described by linear
consitant-cocfficient differential eqirations. Similarly, in discrete time, linear constanl-
coefficieni difference equations play an equally important role. In Section 2.4, we exam-
ined simple examples of differential and difference equations and discussed sonne of the properties of systems described by these types of equadons. Jn particular, systems described b> linear constant-coefficient differential and difference equations together with the condition of initial rest are causal and LTI. In subsequent chapters, we will develop additional totjls that greatly facilitate our ability to analyze such systems.

Extension problems introduce applications, concepts, or methods beyond those pre-
sented in the tej:t.

part (c). Thus, we see that the particular choice for hU) in parts {b) and (c) i^ matched to the signal x{t) ta produce maximum output. Thi*i is an extremeiy important property in a number of applications, as ive will now indicate.
In conununication problems^ one often wishe;^ to transmit one of a small number of pos&ibLe pieces of infoEmation. For example, cf a complex message
is encoded into a sequence of binary digits, wccan imagine a system that trans-
mits the information bit by bit. Each bit c a^i then be transmitted by sending one signal, say, xt,(t) , if the bit is a 0, or a different signal ^,(0 if a 1 is to be com municated. In this case, the receiving system for these signals must be capable of reco^izing whether xty(t)0T j] it) has been received. Intuitively, what makes sense is to have two systems in the receiver, one tuned to jcqU) and one tuned
t& X)\t)^ where, by "tuned," we mean tiiat the system gives a large output afier
the signal to which it is tuned is received. The property of producing a large output when a paiticuJar signal is received is exactly what the matched filter
posse-vses.
In practice, there is always distortion and interference in the transmission
and reception processes. Consequently, we want to maximize the difference be-
tween the response of a matched filter to the input to which it is matched and the response of the filter to one of the other signals that can be transmitted. To illustrate this point, consider the two signals Xf)(t) and xid) depicted in Figure P2.67(b). Let Zfl denote the matched filter for X(,it), and let L| denote the matched filter for xi{t).

(ii) Compare the values of these responses at f -- 4. How might you modify
;jfrh(0 so that the receiver would have an even easier job of distinguishmg between X[)(i) and X[(r) in that the response of Èo to x^U} and L[ to x^it)
would bodi be zero at r - 4?

Fourier SERIES
REPRESENTATION OF
PERIODIC SIGNALS

3,0 flMTRODUCnON

The representation and analysis of LTT systems through the convolution sum as developed

in Chapter 2 is based on representing ^gnals as linear combin^om of shifted impulses.

In this and the foUowirig two chapters, we explore an alternative representation for signals

and LTI systems. As in Chapter 2, the starting point for our discussion is the development

of a representation of signals a5 hnear combinations of a set of basic signals. For this

alternative representation we use complex exponentials. The resulting representations arc known as the continuous'time and discrete-time Fourier series and transform. As we will

see, these can be used to construct broad and useful classes of signals.
We then proceed as wc did in Chapter 2. That is^ because of the superposiuon prop^

erty, the respon se of an LIT system to any input consisting of a linear combination of basi c

signals is the same 1 inear combination of the indivi^hial responses to each of the basic sig-

nals. In Chapter 2, these responses were all shifted versions of the unit impulse response,

leading to the convolution SLiin or integral. As we wiU find in the current chapter, the re-

LH sponse of an

system to a complex exponential also has a particularly simple form,

which then provides us with another convenient representation for LTI systems and with

another way in which to analyse these systems and gain insight into their properties.

In this ch^er, we focus on the representation of continiicjus-time and discrete-time

periodic signals referred to as the Fourier series. In Chapters 4 and 5, we extend the anal-

ysis CO the Fourier transform representation of broad classes of aperiodic, finite energy

signals. Together, these representations provide one of the most powerful and important

sets of tools and insights for analysing, designing, and underst^ding signals and LTI sys-
tems, and we devote considerable auention in this and subsequent chapters to exploring

the uses of Fourier methods.

Wle begia in the next section with a brief historical perspective in order to provide
^me insight into the concepts and issues that we develop in moFC deiatl in the sections

3,1 A HrSTOniCAL PERSPECTIVE

The development of Fourier analysis has a long history involving a great many individuaJs and the investigation of many different pliysical phenomena.^ Tlie concept of using
-- *trigonometric sums" that h, sums of harmonicall> related sines and cosines or periodic
complex exponentials-- to describe periwiic; phenomena goes back at least as far as the Babylonians, who used ideas of this type in order to predict astronomical events,^ The
modem history of the subject begins in 174-8 with L. Euler, whe examined the motion of
a. vibrating string. In Figure 3.1, we ha\e indicated the fli^t few of what are known as the "normal modes" of such ¨ strir^g. If we consider the vertical deflection fit, x) of the
string at time t and at a distance jf along the string, tlien for any fixed instant of time, the
norma] modes are harmonijcaLLy related sinusoidal fuiictions of x^ What Eulcr ivoted was that if the contiguration of a vibrating string at some point in time is a linear combination
of these normal modes, so is the configuration at any subsequent bme. Furthemiore, Euler showed that one could calculate the coefficients for the linear combination at the later time in a very straightforward manner from the coefficients at the earlier time. In doing thi^,
Euter performed the same type of calculation as we will in the next section in <ieriving
one of the properties of trigonometric sums that make them so useful for the analysis of
LIT systems. Specifically, we will see that if the input to an LTl system is expressed as a
linear combination of periodic complex exponentials or sinusoids, the output can also be expressed in this form, with coefficients that are related in a straightforward way to those
of the iflput.
The property described in the preceding paragraph would not be panicularly useful, unless It were true that a large class of interesting functions could be represented by linear combinations of complex exponentials. In the middle of the 13th century, this point was the .subject of heated debate. In 1753, D. Bernoulli argued on physical grounds that all physLcal motions of a string c&uld be represented t?y linear combinaticns of normal modes, but he did not pursiie this mathematically, and his ideai^ were net widely accepted. In fact, Euler himself discarded trigonometric series, and in 1759 J. L. Lagrange strongly criticiKed the use of trigonometric series in the examination of vibrating strings. His criticism was based on his own belief that it was impossible to represent signals with comers (i.e., with discontinuous slopss) uang trigonometric series. Since such a configuration arises from

21, 176S. in Auxerne, France^ and by the time of his entrance into the contioversy con-
cerning trigonometric series, he had ah^ady had a lifetime of expenences. His many
-- ctintributions in paniculai, those concemed with the series and transforni that c;aiTy his -- naiTie are made even ndore impressive by the circimisiaTices under which he wT>rked
His revolutionary discoveries, although not completely appreciated during his own life-
tjtne, have had a major impact on the development of mathematics and have been and still are of greai importance in an extremely wide range of scientific And engiEieeririg disci-
plines.
In addition to hi& studies in tnathematics, Fourier led an active political life In facr,
during the years that followed the French Revolution^his activities almost iedio his downfall, as he narrowly avoided the guillotine on two separate occasions. Subsequently. Fotuier becamean associate of Napoleon Bonaparte, accompanied him on his expeditions toEgypl (during which time Fourier collectfed the information he would use later as the bisis for his treatiseii cm Egyptology), and in 1802 was appointed by Bonaparte to th^ position of prefect of a region of France centered in Grenoble. It was there, while serving as prefect, that Fourier developed his ideas on trigonometric series.
The physical motivadOTi for Fourier*s work was the phenomenon of heat propagation and diffusion. This in itself was a significant step in that niost previous research in mathematical physics had dealt with rational and celestial mechanics. By 1S07. Fourier had completed a work, Fourier had found ^ries of harmonically related sinusoids to be
useful in representing the temperature distribution through a body. In addition, he claimed that **any'' periodic signal could be represented by such a series. While his treatment of
this topic was significant, many of the basic ide^ behind it had been discovered by oth-
R ers. Also, Foinier*s mathematical argutnents were stili imprecise, and it remained for L.
Dirichlet in 1829 to provide precise condirions under which aperiodic signal could be represented by a Fourier series,^ Thus, Fourier did not actually contribute to the mathematical theory of Fourier series. However, he did have the clear insight to sec the potential for this
series representatior^ and it was to a great extent bis work and bis claims that spurred much
of the subsequent work on Fourier series. In addition, Fouriei look this typo of representa-
-- -- tion one very large step farther than any of his jwedecessors: He obtained a representadon
for aperiodic signals not as weighted sums of harmonically related sinusoid5 but as
weighted integrals of sinusoids that are not all harmonically related. It is this extension
from Fourier series to the Fourier integral or transform that is the focus of Chapters 4 and 5, Like the Fouriei series, the Fourier transform remains one of the most powerful tools for the analysis of LTl systems.
Four distmguished mathemaricians and scientists were appointed to examine the
-- 1807 paper of T^ourier. Three of the four S, F_ Lacroix, G. Monge, and P. S. de Laplace
were in favor of publication of the paper, but the fourth, J. L, Lagrange, remained adamant in rejecting trigonometric seriesn as he had done 50 years earlier. Because of Lagrange's vehement objections, Fourier's papor never appeared. After several other atten^ts to have his work accepted and published by the Instinit de France, Fouriei' undertook the writing of another version of h is work, wbi ch appeared as the text Thiorie analytique de la chaleurf'

A Historical Perspective

181

This book was published in 1822, 15 years after Fourier had first presei^te4 his results to
the fnstitut.
Toward the end of his lift Fourier received some of the recognition he deserved,
^ but the most significant tribute to him has been tl£e enormous impact of his work on
many disciplines widiin the fields of tnathetnadc!;^ science, and engineering. The theory
of integration, point-set topology, and eigenfunction expansions are just a few e^tamples of topics in rfiaiheTnatics that have their roots in the analysis of Fouriei series and integrals.^ Furthermore, in addition lo the <?riginal studies of vibr^on and heat diffusion^ there are numerous other problems in science and engineering in which sinusoidal signals, and therefore Fourier series and transforms, play an important role. For example, sinusoidal signals arise naturally in describing the motioTi of the planets and the periodic behavior of
the earth's climate. Alternating-current sources generate siniuoidal voltage^ and currents,
and, AS we will see, the tools of Fourier analysis enable us to analyze the response of an
LTl system, such as a circuit, to such sinusoidal inputs. Also, as illustrated in Figure 3.3, ^aves in the ocean consist of the linear combination of sinusoidal waves with diffeient spatial periods or wavelengthsr Signals transmitted by radio and television stations are sinusoidal in nature as well, and as a quick pemsal of any teuton Fourier analysis will show, the range of applications ir which sinusoitial signals arise and in which the tools of Fcurier analysis are us&ful extends far beyond these few c^amples.

While many of the applications in the preceding paragraph, as well as the origiival work of Fourier and his contemporaries on problems of mathematical physics, focus on phenomena in continuous time, the tools of Fourier analysis for discrete-time signaJs and systems have their own distinct historical roots and equally rich set of applications. In par-
ticular, discrete-time concepts and methods are fundamental to the discipline of numerical analysis. Formulas for the proceisiog of discrete sets of data points to produce numerical approximations for interpolation, integration, and differentiation were being investigated as early as the time of Newton in the 1600s. In addition, the problem of predicting the motion of a heavenly body, given a sequence of observations of the body, spurred the

What has emerged nut of this long history is a powerful and cohesive frannework for

3.2 THE RESPONSE OF LTI SYSTEMS TO COMPLEX EXPONEIMTtALS

As we indicated in Section 3,0, it is advantageous in the study of LTI systems to represent
signals as linear combinations of basit; signals that possess the following two properties:

1* The set of basic signals can be used to construct a broad and useful olass of signals,
2, The response of an LTI system to each signal should be simple enough in structure
to provide us with a convenient representation for the response of the systern to any signal constructed as a linear combination of the basic signals.

Much of the importance of Fourier analysis results from the fact thai bmh of these prop-

erties are provided by the set of complex exponential sigi^als in contiguous and discrete
time--i.e., signals of the form e^' in conrinuous time and z'^ in discrete time, where s and

z are complex numbers. In subsequent sections of this and the following two chapters,
we will examine the Jirst property in *ome detail In this section, we focus on the second

property and, in this way, provide motivation fur the use of Fouriei series and transfomis

in the analysis of LTI systems.

The importance of complex exponentials in the study of LTI systems stems from the

fact that the response of an LTI system to a complex exponential input is the same complex

exponential with only a change in amplitude; diat is,

3.4 CONVERGENCE OF THE FOURIER SERIES
Although Eulfer and Lagrange would have been happy with the results of Examples 3.3 and 3.4, they would have objected to Example 3.5, since x(t) is diScontijiuous while each of its harmortic components is continuous. Fourier, on the other hand, considered the same e^^unpJe and maintained that the Fourier series representation of the square wave is vaJid. In fact, Fourier maintained that any periodic signal could be represented hy a Fourier series. Although this is not quite true, it is true that Fourier series can be used to represent an extremely large class of periodic sigtials^ including the square wave and all other pericolic signals with which we will be concern^ in this book and which ate of interest in practice.
To gain an understanding of the square-wave example and, more generally, of the question of the validity of Fourier series representations, let us examine the problem of approximating a given periodic signal Mt) by a linear combination of a finite number of
-- harmonically related complex exponentials that is, by a finite series of the fonn

Let us turn now to the question of when s. periodic signal xiO does in fact have a Fourier series lepresentation. Of course, for any signal, we can artennpt to obtain a set of Fourier coefficients through the use of eq. (3.39). However, in some cases, the integral in eqn (3-39) niay diverge; that is, the value obtained for some of the a^ may be infinite Moreover, even if all of the coefficients obtained ftom eq. (3.39) arc finite, when these
coefficients are substituted into the synthesis equation (338), the resulting infinite series
rnay not convene to the original signal x{t}.
Fortunately, there are noconvergencedifficpltiesfbr taige classes of periedic signals. For example, every continuous periodic signal hns a Fourier series represent^ion for which
^ the energy Ejv in the approximation error approaches as goes to ¨. This is also true
for many discontinuous signals. Since we will find it very useful to include di^ontinuoua
signals such as s^uarc waves in our discuEsions, it is wonhwhile to investigate the issue of convergence in a bit more detail. Specifically, there are two somewhat different classes of conditions that a periodic signal can satisi^ to guarantee that it can be represented by a Fourier series. In discussing these, we will not attempt u> provide a complete mathematical justification; more rigorous treatments can be found in many texts on Fourier analysis.*

To gain some additional understanding of how the Fotirier series converges for a

periodic signal with discontinuities, let us return to the example of a square wate. In

particular, in 1898,'^ an American physicist, Albert Michelson, constructed a harmonic

analyzer, a device that, for any periodic signal xit), would compute the truncated Fourier

series approximation of eq, (3.52) for values of A^up to 80. Michelson tested his device on
many lunctions, wilh the expected re&ult that jc^(r) looked very much like j(r). However^

when he tried the square v^ave, he obtained an important and, to him, very surprising re-

sui;. Michelson was concerned aboui the behavior he observed mrd thought that his <levLce

might have had a defect. He wrt;ie about the problem to the famous mathematical physicist

Josiah Gibbs, who investigated it and reported his explanation in 1S99,

What Michelson had observed i^ illustrated in Figure 3.9, where we have shown
N ^ Xf^S) *3r several values of lor x{t), a symmetrjt square wave {T ^T-^'). In each case,

the partial sum js superimposed on the original square wave. Since the square wave satis-
£ -- fies the DirichJtJt conditions, the iimit as A^ * of x>^{t) at the discontinuities should be

We the averuge value of the discontinuity.

see from the figure that this is in fact the case,

since lot any N^ x^lji) has exactly that value at the discontinuities. Funhermote, for any
other value of/, say, r - /] , we are guaranteed tliat

hm xnU^) ^ jt(/iX

Therefore, the squared error in the Foiirier series representation of the square wave has

zero area, as in cqs. O.Sy} and {3.54).

For this example, the interesting effect that Michelson observed is that the bohavior

of the partial sum in the vicinity of the discontinuity exhibits rippks ai^d diat the peii>c am-

plitude of these ripples does not seem to decrease with increasing N. Gibbs showed that

these are in fact the case. Specifically, for a discontinuity of unity height, the partial sum

9% exhibits a maximum value of 1.09 (i.e., an overshoot of

of the height of the discon-

N tinuity), no matter how large becomes. One must be careful to ;nterpret this correctly,

however As stated before, for m\y fixed value off, say, t = ^i , the partial sums will con-

verge to the correct value, and at the discontinuity they will converge to one-half the sum

of the values of the signal on either side of the discontinuity. However, the closer t\ is cho-
N sen to the point of disconnnuity, the larger must be in order to reduce die error below a

specified amount. Thus, asA^^increases, the ripples in the partial sums become compressed

toward the discontinuity, but for any finite value of A^, the peak amplitude of the ripples

remains constant. This behavior has cume to be known as the Gibbs phenomenon. The im-

plication is that the truncated Fourier series approximation JtA'fO of a discontinuous signal

x(t] will in general exhibit high-frequency npples and overshoot x{t) near the disconti-
N nuities, ff ijuch an approximation is used in practice, a large enough value of should

be chosen so as to g.uarantee that the total energy in these ripples is insignificant. In the
limit, of course, we know that the energy in the approximation error vanishes and that the

Fourier scries representation of a discontinuous signal such as the square wave converges.

3.5 PROPERTfES OF CONTINUOUS-TIME FOURTER SERIES
As mentioned earlier, Fourier series representations possess a number of important prop-
enies that are useful for developing conceptual insights into such representations, and they
can also help to recluce the cotnplcJtity of the evaHuation of the Fourier serie^^ of many signals. In Table 3,1 we have summansed these properties, several of ^hich are considered in the problems at the end of this chapter. In Chapter 4, in which we develop the Fourier transform, we will see that most of these properties can he deduced from corresponding
properties of the condnuous-time Fourier transform. Consequendy we limit ourselves here to the discussion of several of Uiese properties to illustrate how they may be derived, in-
terpreted, and used.
Throughout the foKowing discussion of selected properties from Table 3,1, we will
find it convenient to vi^ a shorthand ttotation ttj indicate the relationship between a periodic signal and its Fourier series coefficients. Specifically, jiuppose that jr(r) is a periodic
signal with period T atid fundatnental frequency w^ = IttIT. Then if the Fourier seiies coefficients of x(t) are denoted by a^^ we will use the notation

to Siignify thepaiiing of a periodic sigr^al with its Fourier series coefficients.
3.5.1 Linearity
Let xii) and y{t) denote two periodic signals with period T and which have Fourier series coefftcienU denoted by ajt and ^t- respectively. That is,

yir) ^-^ b,.

Since xii) and y{i) have the same period 7", it easily foliov^ that any linear combination

of the two signals will also be periodic with period T. Furthermore, the Fourier series

= coefficients t^ of the litiear combination of j;(/) and >^/), ^(0

Axii) + By{t), are given

by the sanw linear combination of the Fourier series coefficients for x{t) and yit). Thai i;^,

-- + z(t) = Ax{t) + By(t} * ^ ft = Auft Bb^.

(3,58

We The proof of this follows direcdy from the application of eq, (3,39),

also note that

the linearity property is easily extended to a linear combination of an arbitrary number of

signals with period 7\

3.5.2 Time Shifting

time periodic sigtials. As aconse<iuetice, there are no mathematical issues of conveyance
such as those discussed iti SectitJn 3,4.

3,9 FILFERING

In a variety of applications, it is of interest to change the relative ampiimdes of the h^-

queticy components in a signal or perhaps eliimnate some frequency components entirely,

a process referred to 2&fiitermg. Linear time-tn variant systems that change the sh^je of the

spectrutn are often referred to as frequency-shaping jdters. Systems that are designed to

pass some tiiequencies essentially undi^torted and significantly attenuate or eliminate oth-

ers are refened to as frequÈncy-:seltt:ti^filttrs. As inddcaied by eqs. (3h 1 24) and (3.131),

the Fourier series coefficients of the output of an LTI system are those of the input multi-

plied by the frequency response of the system. Consequently, Altering can be coaveniently

accomplished thn^ugh the use of LTI systems with an appropriately chosen tiiequency re-

m^ods sponse^ ai>d frequency -domain

provide us with llie idea) tools to exanutie this

^ vet>' important class of applications. In this and

following two sections, we take a first

look at filtering through a few examples.

3.9.1 Freq u e^cy-Sh^pi ng Filters

One application in which frequency -shaping filters are often encountered is audio sys-
tems. For exaniple, LTI filters are typically included in such systems to permit the listener to modify the relative amounts of low-frequencj energy (bass) and high -frequency energy (treble). These filters correspond to LTI sysiems whose irequency responses can be

changed by manipulating the tone controls. AJso, in high-fidelity audio systems, a so-called

equalizing filter is cften incladed in the preamplifier to compensate for the frequencyresponse characteristics of the speakers. Overall, these cascaded filtering stages are fre-

quently refeiTed to as the equalizing or equalizer circuits for the audio system. Figure 3,22 illustrates the three stages of the equalizer circuits for one parti cular series of audio speak-
ers In this figure, the magnitude of the frequency response for each of these stages is shown

on a log-log plot. Specifically, the magnitude is in units of 201ogi(j |/f(/ai)|, referred to as
decibels or dB. The frequency axis is labeled in Hz (i.e., <a{2TT} along a logarithmic scale. As will be discussed in more detai I in Section 6. 2 .3 , a logarithmic displ ay of the magnitude
of the frequency response in this form is conunon and useful.

Taken together, the equahzing circuits in Figure 3.22 arc designed to conipcnsate for the frequency response of die speakers and the room in which they are located and to allo^^

the listener to control the overall frequency response. In particular, since the three sy stems

are connected in cascede, and since each system modifies a complex exponential input

Ke^*^J by multiplying it by the system frequency lesponse at that frequenL;y, it follows that

the overall frequency response of the cascade of the three systems is the productof the three
frequency responses. The fir^t two filters, indicated in Figures 3.22(a) and (b), together

tnake up the control stage of the system, as the freqitency behavior of these filters can be

edjustedby the listener. The third filter, illustrated in Figure 3,22(c), is the equalizer stage, which has the fixed ft-equency response indicated. The filter in r^gure 3-22(a) is a lowfrequency filter controlled by a two-position switch, to provide one of the two frequency

responses iiidicated. The second Pterin the control stage has two continuously adjustable

slider switches to vary the frequency response within the limits indicated in Figure 3, 22(b),

removed by (requeacy-^lective fijferijtg. Another in^^Ttant ^^hoation of fireqiKncys^fecUve iiltcfs is in communication systems. As wfc di^n^s in cEe^J in Chftpier B, tiie basis for amplitude modulation (AM) g^s^ms is the transmi^isicn of mformatton ^ii' ttiany diffetettt sources fti^ultajieowsly by puttiins the infoi^nation from each <;hafiii^I inio ^ ^eparmi^ fceqiicncy b¨wi asid tsbraciiftg (fee iodiv jd¨a) cEwssnels or bards ai the receiver

237

channels and frequency-shaping filters (such as the equaJiz&r illustrated In Figure 3,22)

for adjusting the quality of the tone form a major part of any home radio and tele^sion

receiver.

While frequeticy selectivity is not the only issue of concern in applications, it^ broad

h^ in^xirtaitte

led to a widely accepted set of terms describing the characteristics of

frequency- sekctive filters. In particular, while ttie nature of the frequenciejs to be passed by a frequency-selective filter varies considerably from application to application, several

basic types of filter are widely used and have been given names mdicative of their func-

-- tion. For example, ^iowpussfilter is a filter that passes low frequencies i,e-, frequencies

= -- A around ii>

and attenuates or rejects higher frequencies. highpa^s filter is a filter

th&t passes liigh frei^uencies and attertuates or tejects low ducs, and a bandpass filter is a

filtef that passes a band of frequencies and attenuates frequencies both higher and lower

than those in the band that is passed. In each case^ the cutofffrequencies are the frequen-

cies defining the' boundaries betw&en frequencies that are passed and frequencies that are
-- m rejected i,e., the frequencies the passband and stophand.

Numerous questions arise in defining and assessing the quality nf a frequency-

How How selective filter.

effective is the filter at passing frequencies in the passband?

How effective is it at attentuating frequencies in the .¨itopband?

sharp is the transition

-- near the cutoff firequency i.e^* from nearly free of distortion in the passband to highly

attenuated in the stopband? Each of these questions involves a comparison of the charao-

tenstics of an actual frequency-selective filter with those of a filter with idealised behavior.

Specifically, an ideal frequency-selective Jilteris a. filter that exactly passes complex ex-

ponentials at one set of frequencies without any distortion and completely rejects signals

at all other frequencies. For example, a continuous-time ideal lowpa.ss filler wirh cutoff

w frequency w^ is an LTl system that passes complex exponentials e^*^^ for values of in the

^ ^ range -- Wt

<iic ^TMf rejecui signals at all other frequencies. That is, the frequency

response of a continuous-time ideal lowpas.*^ filter is

As wc will see oia numerous occasions, ideal filters are quite useful in describing ide-
alised system configurations for a variety of applications. However, they are not realizable in practice and must be approximated. Furthermore, even if ihey could be realized, st>Tne ot the characteristics of ideal filters might make them undesirable for particulai' applications, and a nonideal filter might in fact l^e preferab^CH
In detail, the topic of filtering encompasses many issues, including design and imple-
mentation. WMle we wiU not delve deeply into the details of filter design methodologies,
in the remainder of this chapter and the following chapters we will see a number of other
examples of both ccntinuous-time and discrete-time filteft and will develop tive concepts and techniques that form the basis of this very important engineering discipline.

3 JO EXAMPLES OF CONTINUOUS-TIME FILTERS DESCRIBED
BY DIFFERENTIAL EQUATIONS
In many applications, frequency-selective filtering is accompUshed through the use of LTT systems described by linear constant-coefficient differential or difference equations. The reasons for this are numerous. For example, many physical systems that can be inter-
preted as perfoniiii^ filtering operations are characterized by differential or difference
A equations. good example of this that we will examine in Chapter 6 is an automobile
suspension system, which in part is designed to filter out high-fircquency bumps and ir-
A Tegularities in road surfaces. second reason for the use of filters described by differen-
tial or difference equations is that they are conveniently implemented using either analog or digital hardware. Furthermore, systems described by differential or difference equations offer an extremely broad and flexible range of designs^ allowing one, for example, to produce filters that are close to ideal or that possess other desirable characieristics. In
this and live next section, we consider several examples that illuJitrate the implementation
of continuoua-time and discnete-tinie frequency-selective filters through the use of dif-
ferential and difference eqtiations. In Chapters A-6, we will see other examples of these classes of filters and will gain additional insights into the properties that make them so use-
ful.

analogous tirst-order differentia] equaLions. Becatise of their simplicity, these examptes of

RC and, in fact, have only a single parameter (natncly,

in the electrical case) that con-

trols both Che frequency response and time response behavior of the system. By designing

more complex filters, implemented using more energy storage elements {capacitances and

inductances in electrical filters and springs and damping devices in mechanical filters),
we obtain ftltere described by higher order differential equations. Such filters offer con-

siderably more flexibility in terms of their characterisiics, allowing, for example, sharper

passband-stopband transition or more control over the trade-offs between time response

and freqiacncy response.

3.M EXAMPLES OF OJSCRETE-TtME FILTERS DESCRIBED BY DIFFERENCE EQUATfONS
As with their continuous-time counterparts, discreie-dme filters described by linear
ccjnsiant-coefficient difference equations are of considerable importance in practice. In-
deed, smce they can be efficiently implemented in special- or general -puipose digital systems^ filters described by difference equations are widely used in practice. As jn al-
most all aspects of signal and system analysis, when we examine discrete-lime filters
described by difference equations, we find both strong similarities and important differences wiih the continuous-time case. In particular, discrete-time LTI systems described by difference equations can either be recursive and have impulse responses of infinite Icngdi <11R systems^ or b& nonrecursive and have finite-length impulse responses {FIR systems). The former are the direct counterparts of continuous-time systems described by differential equacions illustrated in the previous section, while the latter are also of
considerable practical importance in digital systems. These two classes have distinct sets of advantages and disadvantages in terms of ease of implementation and in terms of the order of filter or the complexity required to achieve particular design objectives. In this
section we limit ourselves to a few simple examples of recursive and nonrecursive filters, while in Chapters 5 and 6 we develop additional tools and insights that allow us to analyze
and understand the properties of these systems in more detail,

3. T t . 1 First-Order Recursive Discrete-Time Filters

3.12 SUMMARY

In this chapter, we have introduced and developed Fourier series representatJonEi; for both

continuous-time ^nd discrete-time systems and have used these representations to take a

first look at one of the very important applications of die methods of signal and system

analysis, namely, filtering. In particular, as we discussed in Section 3,2, oife of die primary

motivations for the use of Fourier .';enes is the fact that complex exponential signals are

We eigenfunctiona of LTl systems.

have also seerij in Sections 3.3-3.7, that any pericwiic

-- signal of practical interest can be represented in a Fourier series i.e., as a weighted sum

250

Fourier Series Representation of Periodic SrgnaJs

Chap. 3

of hamiDnically rehtted compLe}^ exponentials that share a common period with the signal being represented. In addjtitjri, we have seen that the Fourier series representation has a number Df Important properties which describe how different characterii^tics of signals arc

reflected in their Fourier series coefficients.
One of the most important properties of Fourier series is a direct consequence of the
eigenfunctton property of complex e?iponentiab. Specificaliy, if a periodic signal is appli^ to an LTl system, then the output will be periodic with the same period, ax^d each

of the Fourier coefficients of the output is the corresponding Fourier coefficient of \hs
input multiplied by a complex number whose value is a function of the frequency coire-

spotiding to th^ Fourier coefficient. This function of frequency is characteristic: of the LTJ
system and is referred to as the frequency response of the system. By examining the frequency response, we were led directly to the idea of filtering of signals tising LTI systems, a concept that has nunierous applications, including several that we have descriljed. One

important class of applications involves the noti<?n of frequency-selective filtering--i.e.,

the idea of using an LTI system to pass certain specified hands of frequencies and stop

We or significantly attentuate others.

mtroduced the concept of ideal frequency- selective

filters and also gave several examples of frequency-selective filters described by linear

constant-coefficient differential or difference equations.

The purpose of this chapter has been to begin the process of developing both the tools of Fourier analysis and an appreciaiiox^ for llie utility of diese tools in applicmions. It\

the chapters that follow, we: continue with this agenda by developing the Fourier iransform

representations for aperiodic signals in continuous and discrete time and by taking a deeper

look not only at filtering, but also at other important applications of Fourier metheds.

to the basic, advanced, and extension categories, respectively,

In Chapter 3£ we developed a representation of periodic signaU a* linear combinations of

We complex exponentiaJs.

also saw bow this Tepresentation can be used in describing the

efTect of LTI systems on signals^

In this and the following chapter, we extend these concepts to apply to signals that are not periodic. As we wilt see^ a rather large class of signals, including all signals with finite

ener^, can also be rtpiesented through a linear combination of complex exponentiais.

Whereas for periodic signals the complex expot^ential building blocks are baimonically

related, for aperiodic signals they are inlinitesimally close in frequency, and the represen-

tation in temis of a linear combinaiion takes (he form of ao integral rather than a ¨um. l^e

resulting spectmtn of coefficients in this represencaiiDn is called the Fourier transfonn, and

the sjinthesis integral itself* which uses these coefficienis to re|Kesent the signal as a linear

combination of complex exponentials, is called the inverse Fourier transfomiH

The development of this representation for aperlfKlic signals in continuous time is
one oF Fourier *s most important contiibucions, and our development of the Fourier trans-

form follows very closely tbe approach he used in his original work. In particular^ Fourier

reasoned that an aperiodic signal can be viewed as a periodic signal with an infinite period. More precisely^ in the Fourier series representation of a periodic signa], as the period

increases the fundamental frequency decreases and the harmonically lelated components

become closer in frequency. As the period becomes infinite, the frequency components

form a continuum and the Fourier series sum becomes an integral. In the next section we develop the Founer series representation for continuous-time aperiodic signals, and
in the sections that follow we build on this foundarioti as we explore many of the important

.

Sfic. 4.1

^^ Representation of Aperiodk; ^^g'^l^:

Continuous-Time Fourier Transform

285

properties of the continuous-ttme Fourier transform that form the foundation of freqoency-
domaln methods for continuous-time signds and systems. In Chapter S, we parallel this
development for discrete-ttme signaJs.

complex esponertlials occur at a continuum of frequencies ami, according to die synthesis equation (4.S), have "amplitude" Xij(o}idti)/27r). sitius<^idal signals at different frequencies.

U Equations (4,11) and (4J 2) are the aperiodic counterparts of eqs. (3.5 and (3.54) for

^ Just with periodic signals, there is an alternative set of conditions which are suffi-
cient to ensure that i(0 is equal to xit) for any f except ata discontinuity, where it is equal to the average of the values on either side of the discontinuity. These conditions, again
referred to as the Dirichlet conditions, require that:

1. xit) be absolutely integrable; tiiat is,

< \jiif)\dt

oc.

(4,13)

2. M*} ^a^e a finite number of maxima and uunima within any finite interval. 3. Mi) have a finite number of discontinuities within any finite interval. Futtiermore,
each of these discontinuities must be finite.
Therefore, ahsoluiety mtegrable ^i^nals that are continuous or that have a finite number of discontinuities have Fourier trartsforms.
Although the two alternative sets of conditions that we have given are sufficient to guarantee that a signal has a Fourier transform, we will see in the next section that peri-
odic $igna]s, which are neither absolutety integrable nor square integrable over an infinite interval, can be considered to have Fourier transforms if impulse functions are permitted in the transform. This has the advantage that the Fourier series and Fourier transform can
he incorporated into a common frameworlt, which we will find to be very t:onvenient in
subsequent chapters. Before examining the point farther in Section 4.2, however, let us consider several examples of the Fourier transform.

A common tllLStr:ition of eq. (4,34) is the effect on frequency content that results
when an audiotape is recorded at one speed and played back at a tiifferenl speed. If the pUybact speed is higher than the recwding speed, cotr^spomliTig tD compression in lime
> (i.e., d 1)* then the spectrum i* expaiwjed in frequency (i.e., the audible effeci is that the
playback frequencies are higher). Conversely, the signal played back will be scaled down
< in frequency if the playback speed is slower than the recording speed (0 a < 1). Fot
example, if a recording of the sound of a small bell ringing is played back at a reduced
speed, the result will sound like the chiming of a larger and deeper sounding bell. The scaling property is another example of the inverse relationship between time
and frequency that we have already encountered on several occasions. For example, we have seen that as. we increase the period of a ^inusaidal signal, we decrease its frequency. Also, as we saw in Exartiple 4,5 (see Figure 4. Jl). if we consider the transfoin]

then as we increase IV, the inverse (ransform of X{j<o) becomes narrower and taller and
W approaches an impulse as --> ¨, Finally, in Example 4.8, we saw that the spacing lo the
frequericy domain between impulses in the Fourier transform of a periodic impulse train is inversely proportional to the spacing in the time domain.
The inverse relationship between the time and frequency domains is of great importance in a variety of signal and systems contexts, including filtering and filler design, and we wjU encounter its cotisequ^nces on Qumeroiis CiCcasioTis in the rtmaindcr of the boot. In addition, the reader may very well come across the implications of thi^ property in studying a wide variety of other topics in science and engireering. a linear combination of harmonically related complex exptmentials, as in cl (33g) then die response of an LTI system to this input can also be represented by a Fourier series. Because complex exponentialsareeigcnfunctions of LTI systems, the Fourier serries coefficients of die output are those of the input multiplied by the frequency response of the system evaluated at the corresponding harmonic frequencies.
In this section, we extend this result to the situation in which the signals areaporiodic.

4.5 THE MULTJPLICATION PROPERTY

The convolution property states that convolution in the iTtne domain cotresponds to mullip]it;atioii in the/requency dgmain. Because (jf duality between the time and frequency
domains, we would expect a dual propeny also to hoM (i.e., that muhiplication in the time
dotnain cciiresponds to convolution in the frequency doma^in). Specifically,

^ r(:) ^ mpiO

R(j(o} = ^[Sijo})* Fijian.

(4.70)

This can be shown by exploiting duality as discus.<>ed in Section 4.3,6, together with the ci^nvolution property, or by directly using the Fourier transform relatioris in a manner anal-
ogous to the procedure used in deriving the convolution property.
MuIttpHcation of one signal by another can be thought of as using one signal to scale or modulate the amplitude of the other, and consequently, the multiplication of two sijenali i.s ofren referred to as amplitude modutatiotXr For this reason, eq. (4.70) is sometimes

Sec. 4.5

The Multiplication Property

323

referred to aij the modulaUon property. Ab we shcilL see in Chapters 7 and 8, this property has several very important applications. To illustrate eq. (4,70), and to suggest one of the
applications that we will discuss in subsequent chapters, let us consider ^veral exuTRples,
Exampfe 4.21

4.5. T Frequency-Selective Filtering with Variable Center Frequency

My As suggested iiiExamples 421 and 4.22 and developed more

it^ Chapter S, one of the

importatit applications of the multiplication property is amplitude modulation ir commu-

nicaljon systems. Another importnnt application is in the implementation of fiequency-

jjclective bandpass filters with turiable center frequencies that can be adjusted by the
simple tum of a dial. In a frequency-selective bandpass filter built with elements such

as resistors, operational amplifiers, and capacitors, the cenier frequency depends on a
number of element values, all t>f which must be varied simultaneously in the correct way

if the center frequency is to be adjusted directly. This is generally difficult and cumber-
some in comparison with building a filter whose characteristics art fixed. An alternative

to directly varying the filter churacteriEtity is to ust a fixed frequency-sekcltve iiher anrl

From the preceding examples, we see how the techniques of Fourier aralysis allow
us to reduce problems concerning LTl systems characterized by differetwial equations to straightforward algebraic problems. This important fact is illustrated further iti a number ofthe problems at the end of the chapter. In additioin {see Chapter 6)> the algebraic structure of the rational transforms eticountered in dealing with LTI systems described by differenua\ equations greatly facilitate the analysis of dieir frequency-domain properties and the development of insights into both the time-domaiti and frequency-domain characteristics
of this important class of systems.

4.8 SUMMARY
In this chapter, we have developed the Fourier transform representation f-ar continous-tttne sigtials and have exanuoed many of the properties that mal[È this transform so useful. In particular, by viewing an apeiiodLc signal as the limit <^f a periodic signal as the period becomes arbitrarily large^ we derived the Fourier transform lepresentation for aperiodic
signals from the Fourier series representation for periodic signals developed in Chapter 3. Tn addition, periodic signals themselves can be represented using Fourier transforms consisting of trains of impulses located at the harmonic frequencies of the periodic signal and with areas proportional to the correspoDding Fourier series coefficients.
The Fourier transform possesses a wide variety of importajit properties that describe how different characteristics of signals are reflected in their transforms, and in

)

354

The Continuous-Tline Fourier Trarisform

Chap. 4

this chapter wc have derived and examined many of these properties. Among them are
two thiit have particular .significance for our study of signai;^ and systems. The first is the convolution propeit>, which is a direct consequence of ihe etgenfunciion property of complex exponential signals and i^ich leads to the description of an LTI system in terms
of its frequency response. This description plays a fundamental role in the frequency-
domain approach to the analysis of LTI systems^ which we will continue to explore in subsequent chapters. The second property of the Fourier transfomn that has extremely
important implications is the multiplication property, which provides the basis for the
frequency-domain analysis of sampling and modulation systems. We examine these syj^-
tems further in Chapters 7 and S.
We have also seen that the tools of Fourier analysis are particularly well suited to
the examin^don of LTT systems characterized by linear constant-coefficient differential equations. Specifically, v^e have found that the frequency response for such a system can be determined by inspection and that the technique of partial- fraction expansion can then he used to facilitate the calculation of the impulse response of the system. In subsequent
chapters, wc will find that the convenient algebraic structure of the frequency responses
of these systems allows us to gain considerable insight into their characteristics in both the time and frequency domain s.

(b) One important concept in system analysis is the bandwidth of an LTI system. There are numy different mathematical ways in which to define bandwidth,

but they are related to the qualitative and intuitive idea that a system with fre-

quency response G(/w) essentially "stops" signals of the form e-**^* for vaJues of

itf where G(^Jifi) vanishes or is small and "passes" those complex exponent! aJ'^

in the band of frequency where G< J tu) IS not smalL The widd:i of this band is the bandwidth. These ideas will be made mueh clearer in Chapter 6, but for ro^iv we

will consider a special definition of bandwidth for those syf^tems with frequency

Tespon^es that have the pr^jperties specified previously for H[j<o). Specifically,

unc definition of the bandwidth B^. cif such a system is the width of the rect-

angle of height Af < jO) that has an area equEvl to the area under f/(jt>). This is

4,52, Inverse systems frequently find application in problems involving imperfect measuring devices. For example, consider a device for measuring the temperature of a liquid. It is often reasonable to modeJ such a device as an LTJ system that^ because of the response characteristics of the measuring element (e.g., the mercury in a thermometer), does not respond instantaneonsly to temperature changes. In particular, assume that the response of this device to a unit step in temperature is

In Chapter 4. we mtroduced the i^ontinuouj^-fime Fc-urier transform and deveJoped the
n;any charactensitics of that tmnsfomi which makfi the methods of Fourier analysis of
m atich great value analyzing and understanding the properties of continuous-time signals
^d systems. In the current chapter, we complete our development of the basic tools of
^ouner atialysis by introducing and examining the discrete-time Fourier transfonti

In our discussion of Fourier series in Chapter 3, we saw that there are many similari-

ties and strong parallels in analyzing continuous-tinM and discrete-time signals However

there

infinite senes representation required for continue us -time periodic signals A^ we will see

in this chapter, there are coiresponding differences between continuous -time and discrete-

tune Fourier transforms.

In the remainder of the chapter we take advantage of the sioiilanties between
continuous-bme and discrete-ume Fourier analysis by following a strategy essemialK
idenucal to that used in Chapter 4. hi particular, we begin by extending the Fauner se
nes descnption of periodic signals in order to develop a Fourier transform representation
for discrete-time aperiodic signals, aiid wt follow with an analysis of the properties and
charactenstics of the discrete-time Fourier transform that parallels that given in ChatH
tor 4. By doing this, we not only will enhance our understanding *>f the bAsic concepts of homier analysis thai are common to both continuous and discrete time, but also will con-
trast their differences in order to deepen our understanding of the distinct characteristics
ot each.

jc(/). In this section, we apply an analogous procedure to discrete-time signals in order to

Equations (58) and (5.9) are the discrete-time counterparts of eqs. <4.8) and (4.9). The function X^e^"^) is refened to as the discrete-rime Fourier transform and the pair of equations as the discrete-time Fourier transfbrmpair Equation (5.8) is the synthesis equa-
tion, eq. (5.9) the analysis equation. Our derivadon of these equations indicates how an
aperiodic sequence can Ik thought of as a linear combination of complex exponencials.
In particular, the synthesis equation is in effect a representation of x\_n\ as a linear combination of compJe)( exponentials ioiinitesimally cJose in frequency ajid with amplinjdes Xie^*^)idu}t2'TT\. For this reason, as in continuous time, the Fourier transform Jt(eJ") will often be referred to as the spectrum of i[nj. because it provides us with the infonnation
on how x[n\ is composed of complex exponentials at different frequencies.
Note also that, as in continuous bme. our derivation of die discrete-time Foiuier transform provides us with an important relationship between discrete-time Fourier scries and transforms. Tn particular, the Fourier coefficients ay of a periodic signal x{n\ can be expressed in terms of equalJy spaced samples of the Fourier transfomi of a finite-durabon, aperiodic signal x[rt] diat is equal to l[ri\ over one period and is zero otherwise. This fact
is of considerable importance in practical signal processing and Fourier analysis, and we
look at it further in Problem 5 .41
As our derivadon indicates, the discrete-time Fourier transform shares tnany similanties with the continuons-tinie case. The major differences between the two are die periodicity of the discrete-bme transform X(t:^*^) and die finite interval of integration in the synthesis equation. Both of these stem from a fact that we have notod -several bmes before: Discrete-time complex exponentials dial iliffer in frequency by a multiple of 27r ^ne identical. In Section 3.6 we saw diat, for periodic discrete-time signals, the implications of this statement are that the Fourier series coefficients are periodic and that the Fourier
series representadon is a finite sum. For aperiodic signals, the analogous implications are diat XieJ"^) is periodic (with period 277) and that the syndie&is equation involves an inte^ gration only over a frequency interval that produces distinct complex exponentials (i.e.,
any inter^-al of Icngdi 2ir). In Section L3.3> we noted one further consequence of the pe-

5,3 PROPERTIES OF THE DISCRETE-TIME FOURIER TRANSFORM
As ivilh the continuous-time Fourier transform, a variety of properties of the discrete-time
Fourier transform provide further insight into the transform and, in addition, are often useful in reducing the cotnpJexity in the evaluation of tiansfomris aitd inverse transforms.
In this and the following two sections we consider these properties, and in Table 5. 1 we present a. concise sumEnary of them. By comparing this table with Table 4.1, we can
get a clear picture of some of the similarities and differences between cont]nuous4lme
and discietc-time Fourier transform properties. When ihc derivation or interpretation of
a discrete-time Fourier transform property is essentially identical to its cotjtinuous-time
counterpart, we will simpiy state the property. Alsq, because of the close relationship between the Fourier series and the Fourier transform, many of the transform properties

tranaJate directly into corresponding properties tbr the discrete-time Fourier series, which
we summarized iti Table 3,2 and btiefly discussed in Section 3.7.

herween the cDntinuous-time and discrete-time domains.

5.4 THE CONVOLUnON PROPERTY
Tn Section 4,4, we discussed the importance of the contintjoui-time Founer transform with
regard to its eiTeci on the operation of convolution and its use in dealing with continuous-
time LTl systems. An identical relation applies in discrete time, and this i^ one r>f the
pnncipd reasons that the discrete-time Fotuier transfonti is of such great value in representing and analyjzJTig discrete-time LTI .systems. Specifically, if jr[jil, A[n], and >(ril are the input, in^uise response, and output, respectively, of an LTl sy.stem, so that
y[n] - \[n] * h[rjl
then

Yie^'') = Xie^'^iHie^'^X

l5,4S>

where Xie^'^lH{e^'^), and K(^^^) aie the Fourier transf4>mis of :*[¨], h[n], and \[nl re-
spectively. Furthermore, comparing eqs. (3.122) and (5.9), we see thai the frequency re-
sponse of a discrete-time LTI system, as first definedin Section 3.8, is the Fourier transform of the impulse response of the system.
The derivation of eq. (5.48) exactly parallels that carried oui in Section 4 4. fn particular, as in continuous time, the Fourier synthesis equation (5.S£ for jc[ii] can be inter-

Sec. S.4

The Convolution Property

393

p^ete^<i dj^ a decompOiiition of x[n] into a Linear combination of complex exponentials with
^ infinitesima] amplitudes proportional u> X(È^*^}. Each of these exponentials is eigen-
function of the system. In Chapter 3, we used this fact to show that the Fourier ^erie^
coefficients of the respond of an LTI system to a periodic inpot are simply tbe Fourier coefficients of the input multiplied by the system'*! frequency response evaluated at the cotre&potiding harmonic frajuencies. The convolution property (5h43) represents the extension of this result to dperiodk Lnpots and outputs by using tbe Fourier transform rather
than the Fourier series.
As in continuous time, eq. (5.48) maps the convolution of two signals to the simple
algebraic operation of multiplying their Fourier transforms, a fact that both faciUtate^ the analysis of signals and system!* and adds significantly to our understanding of the waiy in which an LTT system responds to the input signals that are applied to it. In particular, from
eq. (5.48), we see that the frequency response Hie^*^) captures the change in conplex
amplitude of the Fourier transform of the input at each frequency w. Thus, tn frequency-
^ selective filtering, for example, we want i/(c^") 1 over the range of frequencies L-orrespondlng to the desired passband and H{ef'^) ^ over the band of frequencies to be
eliminated or signiticantly attenuated.

5.9 SUMMARY
In this chapter, we have paralleled Chapter 4 as we developed the Fourier transform for
discrete-uine signals and examined many of its importatit prapetiies. Throughout the chaf^
ter, we have seen a great many similarities between continuou^-tiine and discrete-time Fourier analysis, and we have also seen some important differences. For example, the re-
latikjnship between Fourier seiies and Fourier transforms in discrete time is ei^actl^ analogous to that in continuous time. In particulars our derivatioti of the discrete-time Fourier trjnsfomn for aperiddic signals from the discrete- time Fovixier series representations is very much the aame as the con;esponding continuous-time detivation- FuTtherTnore. many
of tiie properties of continuous-dnie transfyrms have exact discrete-time counterparts. On
the other hand* in contrast to the continuous-time case, the discrete-^ime Fourier transform of an aperiodic signal is aJways periodic with period 2^7. In addition to similarities and
differences such as these, we have described the duality relationships among the Fourier
representations of continiiDus-tiine and discrete-time signals.
The most important simjhaiities between continuous- and discrete -time Fourier attal-
ym are in their uses in analyzing and representing signals and LTI systems. Specifically,
Uie convolution properly provides us with the basis for the frequency-domain analysis of
LTT systems. We have already seen some of the utility of this approach in our discussion of

400

The Discrete-Time Fourier Transform

Chag 5

m ilitering in Chapters 3-5 and t?ur examinacioFJ of Nystems describttl by linear constant-
coefficient differential or difference equations, and we will gain i further appreciation for its utility in Chapter 6, in which we examine filtering and time'Versus- frequency i.ssue? in
more detail. In addition, the mtiltipiit:atiora properties in continuous and discrete time are essential to our developmenr of sampling in Chapter 7 and coniTminications in Chapter 8.

Time and frequency
characterization

6.0 INTRODUCTION
The frequency -domain characterization of an LTl system in terms of iL. frequency re-
sponse represents an alternative to the time doinaiji characterisation through convolution. Iti ^aJyzing LTI systems, it is often psirticularly convenient to utihze the frequency domain because differentiaJ and difference equations and convolution operations in the time
domain become algebraic operations in the frequency domain. Moreover, concepts such as frequency^-seiective filtering are readily and simply visuaJiied in the frequency domain However, in system design, there are typicaJiy both time-domain and frequency-domain
consideraiions. For example, as we bridly discussed in Examples 4. IS and5. 1 2, and a;; we
will illustiate in more detail in this chapter, significant oscillatory behavior in the itnpujse
response pf a frequency-selective filter may be undesirable, and conseqLiently, we cnay
wish to sacrifice the level of frequency selectivity in a filler ir order to meet the required tolerances on impulse response behavior. Situations such as thi.s are the mle rather than
the exception in practice, as in most applications we ivould like lo specify or conshain certain characteristics of a system in boih the time domain and the frequency domam^
frequently resulting in conliicttng requirements. ?fcnce. in system design and analysis, it is important to relate time domain and frequency -domain characteristics .iud trade-offs. Introducing these issues and relationships is tlie primary focus of the chapter.

6. 1

THE MAGNFTUDE-PHASE REPRESENTATIOW OF THE FOURIER TRANSFORM

The Fourier transform is in getwral complex valued and, as we discussed, can be repre-
sented in terms of its real and imaginary comiponet^ts or in terms of magnitude and phase,

Similarly the magnitude-phase representation of the discnete-time Fonrier transform

Xie'"^) = \X(e^n\e''^^^-'"'\

(6.2)

In the fodowirig discussion, we concentrate for the most part on the continiious-tiTne case
in describing and illustratii^ several points related to magnitude -phas¨ re-prviseniations. The essential points appl> equally to the discnete-dme case.
From the Fourier transform synthesis equation (4.8), we can think of X{j(o) as pro-
viding us with a decomposition of the signal jf(/) into a ^'sum" of complex exponentials at
different frequencies, fn fact, as discussed in Section 4.3.7, ]XXJtit)\^ may he interpreted
as the energy-density spectrum of xU)^ That is, \Xij{i>)\'^dii>f2'rr can be thought of as the amount of energy in the signal xU) that lies in the infiitiie&imal frequeiKy band between
w jw liTid -h dof. Thus, the magnitude jA'(/iw)] describes the basic frequency content of a
signal--i.e., |a:(jcu)| provides us with the information about the relative magnitudes of the
complex exponentials that make up x(i). For example, if ]X[ jw)l = outside of a small
band of frequencies centered at zerot then j(f) will display only relatively low- frequency
oscillations.
The phase angle <X{jwX on the other hand, docs not affect the amplitudes of the
individual frequency components, but instead provides us with infoimation concerning the
relative phases of these exponentials. The phase relationships captured by '^.X{jiii) have a significam effect on the nature of the signal x{t) and thus typically contain a substantial amount of information about the signal. In particular, depending upon what this phase function is, we can obtain very different-looking signals, even if the magnitude function remains linchanged. For example, consider again the example illustrated in Figure 3 J. In this case, a ship encounters the superposition of three wave trains, each of vi^iich can he modeled as a sinusoidal signal. With fixed magnitudes for these smusoids, die amplitude of their sum may t)e quite small or very iarge, depending on the relative phases. The implications of phase for the ship, therefore, are quite significant- As another illustration of
the effect of phase, txjnsider the sigt^

different relative phases.
In general, changes in the phase function of Xijoi) lead to changes in the time-
domain characteristics of the signal x<t) In some instances phase distortion may be
important, whereas in others it is not. For example, a well-known property of the auditor> system is a relative insensidvity to phase. Specifically, if the Fourier transform of a spoken sound (&.g., a vow^I) is subjected to a disionion such that the phase is changed but the magnitude is unchanged, the effect can he perceptually negligible, although the waveform
in the time dtimain may look considerably different. While mild phase distortions such as
those affecting individual sounds do not lead to a loss of intelligibility, more severe phase

6.2 THE MAGNITUDE-PHASE REPRESENTATION OF THE FREQUENCY RESPONSE OF LTf SYSTEMS
From the convolution propeny for continuous-tiine Fourier transforms, the transform y(Jio} of the output of an LTI system k related to die transfomi Xij<tf) of the input to the system by the equaticn
-- where //(/Qj) is the frequency response of the system i.e., the Fourier transform wf the
system^s impulse response. Similarly, in discrete time, the Fourier transforms of the input X(ei*^) and ouput Yief"^) of an LTI system with frequency response Wtf^'") are related by
Thus, the effect that an LTI system hai on the input is to change the complex amplj-
tudeof each of the frequency components of the signai. By looking at this efTect in icmn, of the magnitude-phase representation, we can understand the nature of the effect Ln more

428

Time^nd Frequency Characterization of Signals and Systems

Chap. E

detail. Specifically, in continuous time,
\Ëijui)\ = iHiMlXiMjl
and
<.Y(Jo?) = <H(£ + <X(£,

{6.5) (6.6)

and exacdy analogous relationships hold in the discrete-time case. From eq. (6.5), \te see
that the effect an LTI systeni has on the magnitude of the Fourier transform of the system is to scale it by the magnitude of the frequcincy response. For this reason, \HiJtii)\ (oi
\H{e-^^)\) is commonly referred to as the gain of the system. Also, from eq. (6.6), we see that the phase of the input <X{jii)) is modified by the LTI system by addit^g the phase ^.H{jii)) to it and ^.H{iia) is typically referred to as the phase shifi of the system. The phase shift of the system can change the relative phase relationships among the compo-
nents of the input, possibly resulting in significant modifications to the time domain char-
acteristi:;s of the input even when the gain of the system is constant fnr a|] frequencies. The changes in the magnitude and phase thai result from the application of an input to an LTI iystem may be either desirable^ if the input signal is modified in a useful way, or
undesirable, if the input is changed in an unwanted manner. In the latter case, the effects in eqs. (6.5) and (6-6> arc commonly referred tc as magnitude and phase dhtonions. In
the following sections, we describe several concepts and tools that allow us to understand these effects a bit more thoroughly.

6.2. f Linear and Nonlinear Phase

When the phase shift at the frequency ^(J is a linear function of tw, there is a particularly
straighlftirwiiTd interpretation of the effect in the time domain. Consider the continuoustime LT[ System with frequency response

mjo>) = f-^"'^

In the discrete-time case, the effect of linear phase is similar to that in the continuous-

of samples. When the phase slope is not an integer, the effect in the lime domain is siime-
what more complex and is discussed in Chapter 7, Section 1.5 Informally, the effect is a
time shift of the envelope of the sequence values, but the values diemselves may change.
While linear phase shifts lead to very simple and easily understood and visualized changes in a signal, if an input signal is subjected to a phase shift that is a nonlinear function of w, then the complex exponential components of the inpul at different
frequencies will be shifted in a manner that rcsuhs ir a change in their relative phases

6.2.3 Log-Magnjtude and Bode Plots
In graptiJcally displaying continuous-tinie or discrete-time Fourier transforms aiid systcn:i frequency r&sponscs in polar fonrt, it i^ tiften convenient to use a logarithmic scale for the
magnitude of the Fourier transform. One of the piincLpai r^a^ons for doing this can be seen

iic 1.2

Tlie Maflnrtude -Phase Representation of the Frequeni:^ Response of LT I Systems

4J7

from cqs. (6.5) and (6.6), whicli relate the magnUude and phase of the output of an LTl
system 10 those of the input and frequency response. Note that the phase relalionship is additive, while the magnitude relationship invt>lves the product of \H(jfit)\ and |X(jw)j. Thus, if the magnitudes of the Fourier transform are displayed on a logarithmic amplitude scale, eq. (6.5) takes the form of an additive relationship, namely,

log|n>)| - log|//(/¨i)| + loglA'O)!,

(6.16)

with an exactly analngous expression in discrete time.
Consequcndy, if we have a graph of the log magnitude and phase of the Fourier

transform of the input and the frequency response of an LTl system, the Fourier transform

of the output is obtained by adding the log-magnitude ploL<; and by adding the phase ploLs. In a similar fashion, since the frequency response of the cascade of LTl systems is the
product of the irdividual frequency responses, we can obtain plots of the log magnitude and
phase of the overall frequency response of cascaded systems by adding the corresponding ploti for each of the component systems. In addition, plotting the magnitude of the Fourier transform on a logarithmic scale allows detail to be displayed over a wider dynamic range. For example, on a hnear scale, the detailed magnitude characteristics in the stopband of

a frequency-selective filter with high attenuation are typically not evident, whereas they are on a logarithmic; scale.

Typically, the specific logarithmic amplitude scale used is in units of 2(1 logj^^^ in-
ferred to as decibels^ (abbreviated dB). Thus, dB corresponds to a frequency response with magnitude equal to 1 . 20 dB is equivalent to i gain of 10, - 20 dB corresponds to an attenuation of 0, 1 , and so on. Also, it is useful to note thai 6 dB approjtimateiy corresponds
to a gain of 2.

For continuous-time systems, it is also common and useful to use a logarithmic
frequency scale. Plots of 201og,^!//(jVo)| and <H(Jti}) versus log^uC^j) are referred to
as Bode plots. .\ typical Bede plot is Illustrated in Figure 6.8. Note that as discussed

in Section 4.3.3, if Mf) is real. Chen \H(Jfa)\ is an even function of at and <Hij<a} is

an odd function of to. Because of this, the plots for negative cu are superfluous and cart

tie obtained immediately from the ploLs for positive m. This, of course, makes it pos-

w > sible to plot frequency response charaeteristics versus logifl(tu) for

0, as in the

figure.

The use a t>f logarithrciic frequency scale offers a number of advantages in continuous time. For example, it often aUovvs a much wider range of frequencies to be displayed than does a linear frequency scale. In addition, on a logarithmic frequency scale, the shape

6.4 TIME-OOMAJN AND FREQUENCY-DOMAJN ASPECTS
OF NONIDEAL FVLTERS
The characten sties of ideal filters are not always desirable in practice For example, in many filtering contexts, the signals lo be separated do not always lie in totally disjoint
A frequency bar^ds. typical situation might be that depicted in Figure 6.15, where the spectra of two signals overlap shghtly. Tn such a case, we may wish to trade off the fi)-- delity with which die filter preserves one of these signals--^s ay, ;( i (I against the level A to which frequency components of the second signal xiit) are attenuated, fiher with
a gradual transidon from passband to stopband is generally preferable when nUering the
superposition of signaJs with overlapping spectra.

X{j^)

Figure 6, 1 5 Two spectra that are
Èli^htl^ overlapping.

Another consideration is suggested by examining the step responses of ideal lowpass filters, shown in Figure 6,14, For both continuous time and discrete time, die step response

asymptotically approaches a constant equal to the value of die step. In the vicinity of the

discontinuity, however, it overshm>LH this value and exhibits ringing. In sotne situations,

this time-domain behavior tnay be undesirable.

Moreover, even in cases where the ideal frequency-selective charaeterisdcs are desirable, they may not be attainable. For example, from ^s. (6^18) and (6.19) ano Fig-

When ure 6, 12, it is evident diat the ideal lowpass filter is noncausal.

filtering is to be

carried oat in real time, however^ causality is a tieces?>ajy constraint, and thus, a causal
A approximation to the ideal characteristics would be required. funher consideration that
motivates providing some flexibility in the filter characteristics is ease of implementation. In general, the more preci^ly we try to approximate or implement an ideal frequency-

selective filter, the more complicated or costly the implementation bccomen^ whether in
m terms of components such as resistors, capacitors, and operational amplifiers ctmtinu
ous time or in terms uf memory registers, mLiltiphers, and adders in discrete time. In many

contexts, a precise filter characteristic may not be essential and a simple filter will suffice.

For all of these reasons, nonideal filters are of of considerable practical importance,
and the characteristics of such filters are frequently si^ecified or quantified in terms of
m several parameters both the firequency and dme domain. First, because the magritude

characteristics of the ideal frequency-selective filter may be unachievable or undesirable.

Sec 6 4

Time-Domam and Frequency-Domain Aspects of Nonideal Fitters

445

it h preferable to allow some flexibility iji the behaviyr of the filter in the pa^sbanJ and
in the siopbantl, us well as to permit a more gradual transition between the passband and stopband, as opposed to the abrupt trans ition chai'acteristic of ideal filters. For example,
in ^e case of lowpaas filters, the specifications may allow some deviatioD from unitj gain
in the passband and from jero gain, in the slopband, as well as including both a pa-'^^band edge and stopband edge with a transition band between them, Thiis, specifications for a continuous-time lowpass filter are often stated to require the ma^itnde of the frequency
m rciipOTise of the filter to be restricted to the nonshaded area indicated Figure 6 16. In
this figure, a deviation from unily of plus and minus 5] is allowed in the passband, and a de'tiation of ^2 from zero is allowed in the stopband. The amount by which the frequency response differs from unity in the passband is referred to as the passhand ripple, and the amount by which it deviates from zero in the stopband is referred to as the sfophand ripple. The frequency <x>p is refeircd to as the passband edge and w^ as the stopband edge. The frequency range from tOp to (Os is provided for the transition fn>m passband to stopband and is referred to as the transition band. Similar definitions apply to discrete-time lowpass filters, as well as to other continuous- and discrete-time frequency-selective filters.

Tn addition to the specification of magnitude characteristics in the frequency domain, in some cases the specification of phase characteristics is al$o important In particular, a linear or nearly linear phase characteristic over the passhand of the filter is frequently desyable.
To control the time-domain behavior, specifications are frequently imposed on the step response of a filter. As illustrated in Figure 6.17, one quantity often of interest is the
-- risetntie jj. of the step response i.e., the interval over which the step response rises toward
its final value, in addition, the presence or absenceof oscillatory behavior, or ringings in the step response is often of importance. If such rtTigiiig is present, then there are three other quantities that are often used to characterize the nature of these oscillations: the overshoot
A of the final value of the step response^ the ringing frequency <i>n and the senling time
--ts i e., the time required for the step response to settle to widtin a specified tolerance of
its final value.
For nonideal lowpass filters, a trade-off tmiy be observed between the width of the transition band (a frequency -domain characteristic) and the Jiettling time of the step response (a time-domain charactcnstic). The foU^iwing exampic illustrates this trade-off.

(See, for example. Problem 6.45.)

6.7 EXAMPLES OF TIME- AND FREQUENCY-DOMAIN ANAL^IS OF SYSTEMS
Throughout this chapter^ we have illustrated the importance of viewing systems in both the
time domalti and the frequency domain and the importance of being aware of trade-offs in
the behavior between the two domain;*. In diis section, we illustrate some of these issues further. In Section 6.7.1 ^ we discuss these trade-offs for condnudus time in the context of an automobile suspension systeTn. In Section 6.7.2, we discuss an important class of
discrete-lime filters referred to as moving-average or noiu-ecursive systems.

Sec 6.7

Examples of Time- and Frequency-Domain Analysis of Syslems
