6.7.1 Anafysis of an Automobile Scjspensjon System
A number of the points thai we have made concerning the chaiacteri sties anJ trade-i?ffs
in con tiiiuous -time systems can be illustrated in the intcrptetation of an automobile sus-
pension system as a lowpasA filter Figure 632 shows a diagrainmatic representation of a
simple suspension system camprised of a spring and dashpol (shock absorber). The roaJ surface can be thought of as a superposition of rapid small-amplitude changes in devaiion (iiigli frequencies)^ represenling Ihe roughness of the road suifaiceT and gradual c;hangeH in elevation (low frequencies) due to the general topography. The automobile stispensioti system is generally inteTided to fiUer out rapid variations in the ride caused by the road surface (i.e., the system acts as a bwpass filter).
The basic purpose of the su^jension system i^ to provide a smooth ride, and there i^ no sharp, natural division between the frequencies to be passed and those to be rejected.
Thus, it is reasonable to accept and, in fact, prefer a lowpass filter that has a gradual

4M
Ë^t) + yo

Time and Freflifency CharactflJ'izatian of Signals and Systems

Chap. 5

Chassis

Sprang,

Road
surface

Reterence elevation

Figure 6h32 O^agramrnatic representation of an automotive suspension sy^em. Here, >b represents the distance tHtween ttie chassis and the road
+ surface wtien ttie automodite is at rest y{t] y^ ttve position of ttie chassts
aE>ove the referer^^e eJevation^ and x{t) the elevation of the road above the reference eJevation.

transition from passbandto stopband. Furthermore* the time-domain characteristics of ihe system are important. If the impulse respotise or step response of the suspension systetii
exhibits ringing, then a large bump in the road (modeled as an impulse input) or a curb
(modeled as a step input) will result in an uncomfortable oscillatory response. In fact, a
common test for a suspension system is to ititroduce an excitation by depressing and then
releasing the chassis. If the response exhibits ringing, it is an indication that the shoct absorbers need to be repbced.
Cost and case of implementation al^o play an impcrtant role in the design of au-
tonnobile suspenfiion systems. Many studies ha\e been carried out to determine the mosi
desirable finequency-response characteristics for suspension systems from the poi nt of view
of passenger comfort. In situations where the cost may be warranted, such as for passenger
railway cars, intricate and costly suspension systems are used. For the automotive industry, cost is an important factor, and simple* less costly suspension systems are generally
used. A typical automotive suspension system consisL<; simply of the chassis connected to
the wheels through a spring and a dashpot. In the diagrammatic representation in Figure 6,32, >¨ represents the distance be-

associated with the ^hock asoibcr^. As the natural freqti^ckcy w,, is decreased, the liuspen:^LOn will tend to Hlter out slower road variations, thus providing a smoother nde. On the other hand, we see from Figure 6.34 that the rise time of the system increases, and thus the

far a trade-off between dme-domain and frequency-domain characteristics. Typically, a suspension system with a low value of Wn* so that the rise time is long, is characterized as 'soft*' and one with a high value of cun> so chat the rise tim^ is short, is characterized a^

6,7.2 Example; of Discrete^Time NonrecursJve Filteri
In Section 3.1 1, we introduced the two basic clas,':es of LTI filters described by ditference
equations, namely, recursive or infinite itnpulse response {UK) fihen; and nonrecursive or finite impulse response (FTR) fihers. Both of these classes of filters are of considerable
importance in practice and have their own advantaged and disadvantageSr For example,
recursive filters impleraented as interconnections of the firsr- and second-order systems described in Section 6.6 provide a fiexihie class of filters that can b& easily and efficientty
implemented and whose characteristics can be adjusted by varying the number and the parameters of each of the component first- and second-order subsystems. On the other hand, as shown in Problem 6.64, it is aot possible to design a causal, recursive filter with exactly linear phase, a property that we have seen is often desirable since, tn that case, the effect of the phase on the output signal is a simple time delay. In contrast, as we show in this section, iworecursive filters c<m have e^acdy linear phase. However, it is generally true that the same filter specificatioTis reqiiire a higher order equation and hence more coefficients and delays when implemented with a nonrecursive equation^ t;ompaTed with a recursive difference equation. Consequently, for FIR filters, one of the principal trade-off;* between the time and frequency domains isi that increasing the fie?iibiiity in spccifyitig thefreqtiency domain characteristics of the filter, incliiding, for eA^Jnlple. achieving a higher degree of frequency selectivity, requires an FIR filter with an impulse response of longer duration.
One of the most basic nonrecursive filters^ introduced in Section 3.11.2, is die
moving-average filter For this class of fillers, the output is the average of the values of the input over a finite window;

^^"^ ^ ^vTi^7;2:^/t" - ^]k = -f<t

(6.78J

Sec. 6-7

Examples of Time- and Frequency-Dorrain Analysis of Systems

477

The corresponding impulse response is a rectangular pulse, and the frequency response is

^ ^ N + M -^V

"
sinC(j/2)

in Figure 635, we show the log magnitude for*/ H-N+ 1 = 33 andM + A'-i^ i = 65.THe
main^ center lobe of each of these frequency responses corresponds to the cftective passband of the corresponding filter. Note that, as the impulse response increases in length, the width of the main lobeof the magnitudeof the frequency resporse decreaaes_ Thi^ provides

another example of the trade-off between the titnc and frequency domains. Specifically,

in order to have a narrower passband, the filter in eqs. (6.73) and (6.79) must have a longer impulse response. Since the length of the impulse response of an FIR filter has y

direct impact on the complexity of its implementation, this implies a trade-off l>etween frequency selectivity and the complexity of the filter, a topic of central concern in filter

design.

Moving" average filters are commonly applied in economic analysis in order to at-

tenuate the short-term fluctuations in a variety of economic indicators in relation to longer

term trends. In Figure 6.36, we illustrate the use of a moving-average filter of the form of

eq. (6.78) on the weekly Dow Jones stock market index for a lO-year period. The weekly

Dow JcMies index is shown in Figure 6, 36(a), Figure 6.36(b} is a 51-day moving aver-

N M age (ie-t =

= 25) applied to that index, and Figure 636(c) i$ a 201-day moving

N M ^ average (i.e.,

^ ItX)) applied to the index. Both moving averages are considered

useful, with the 51 -day average cracking cyclical [i.e., periodic) trends that occur during

the course of the year and the 201 -day average primarily emphasizing trends over a longer

time frame.
The more general form of a discrete-lime nonrecursive filter is

M so that the output of this filter can be thought of as a weighted average of ^ + + 1
neighboring points. The simple moving-average filter in eq. (6.78) then corresponds to
setting all of these weights to th e same value, namely, 1/(jV +3f + 1 ), However, by choosing these coefficients in other ways, we have considerable flexibility in adjusting the filter's

frequency response.

There are, in fact, a variety of techniques available for choosing the coefficients in

eq. (6.S0) so as to meet certain specifications on the fllier^ such as sharpening the transition

N+M+1 band as much as possible for a filter of a given ler^lh (i.e.^ for

^Jted). These pro-

cedures are discussed in detail iti a number of text^,^ and although we do not di^cu^;^ the

procedures here, it is worth emphasizing that they rely heavily on the basic concepts and
tools developed in this boot. To illustrate how adjustment of the coefficients can influence

An important property of the examples we have gi ven is that they all have Mrc> or 1 in -
ear phase characteristics. For example, the phase of the moving-average filter of eq. <6.79)
is w[(Af - M)/2]. Also, since the impulse response in eq. (6.32) is ital and even, the im-
puhe response wf the filter described by that equation i? real and even, and thus has zero
phase. From the symmetry properties of the Fourier triinsform of real signals, we know that any nonrecursive filter with an impulse response that h real and even will have a frequency response //(e^'^) that is real and even and, consequently, has zem phase. Such a < filter, of course^ is noncau^sal, since its impulse response h[n] has nonzero values for a 0.
However, if a causal filter is required, then a simple change in the impulse response can achieve this, resulting in a system with linear phase. Specifically, since h[n] is the impulse response of an FIR filter, it is identically zero outside a range of values centered at the origin

6,8 SUMMARY

In this chapter* we have built on the foundation of Fourier analysis of signals and systems

developed in Chapters 3-5 in order to exajnine in more detail the characteristics -of LTI

systems arvd the effects they have on signals. In particular, w& have takeo a careful look at

the magnitude and phase characteristics of sigiwls and systems, and \ve have introduced

We log-m^goicude and Bode plots for LTI systems.

have also discussed the impact of

phase and phaj;e distortion on signals and i^ystems. This examination led us to understand

the special role played by linear phase characteristics* which impart a constant delay at all

frequencies and which, in turn, led to the concept of nonconstant group delay and disper-

sion associated with systems having nonlinear phase characteristics. Using diese tools and

insights, we took another look at frequency-,selective filters and the time-frequency (rade-
offs involved. We examined the properties of both ideal and non -ideal frequency-selective

fihei^s and saw that time-frequency considerations, causality constraints, and implemen-

tation issues frequently make non -Ideal Alters, with transition bands and tolerance limits in d^ passhands and stopbands, the preferred choice.

Chap. 6 Problems

463

We also esamined in detail the ttme-frequency characteristics of first- and second-

We order systems in both continuous and discrete time.

noted in particular the trade-off

between the response lime of these systems and the frequency-domain bandwidth. Since

first- and second-order systems are the bui Jding blocks for more complex, higher order LTI

systems, the insights developed for those basic systems are of considerable use in practice.

Finally, we presented several examples of LTI systems in order to illustrate many of

the points developed in the chapter. In particular, we examined a simple model of an ^to-

mobile suspension system to provide a concrete example of the time-res pons e-frequency-

resporise concerns tlut drive system design in practice. We also considered several

examples of discrete-time nonrecursive filters, ranging from simple moving- average

We filters to higher order FIR filters designed to have enhanced frequency selectivity.

saw, in addition, thai FIR filters can be designed so as to have exactly linear phase. These

examples, the development of the tools of Fourier analysis diat preceded them, and the

insights those tools provide illustrate the considerable value of the methods of Fotirier

analjsis in analyzing and designing LTI systems.

The system given in (iv) is often referred to as a non-^minimum-phase system^ wKile the sysiem specified in i\\) is referred tn as being a minimum phas^. The correAponding impulse responses of (iv) and (vi) are referred u> as a non -minimum-phase signal and a rn^ni mum-phase signal, respectively. By comparing the Bode plots of these two frequency responses, we can see that

they have identical magnitudes; however, the magnitude of the pha.^ic of the

system of <iv) is larger than for the system of (vi).
We tan also note differences in the time-domain behavitir of the two sys-

terns. For example, the impulse response of the mini mum-phase system has
more of its energy concentrated near t = Q dian does the impxiLse response of
the non-minimum-phase system. In addition, the step response of (\\ ) mittally
has the opposite liign from its asymptotic v^iie as r --^ cc, while this i^ not the
case fc3r the system of (vi).

The important concept of minimum- and non-mimmum-phafte systems

can be extended to more general LTI systems than the simple first-order systems
we have treated here, and the distinguishing characteristic* of these fiy stems can be described far more thoronghSy than we have done,

6.49, The time constat^t provides a mcasurie of how fast a first-order system responds to
inpuLs. The idea of measuring the speed of response of a system is also important for higher order systems, and in this problem we investigate the extenj^ion of the time
constant to such systenns. (a) Recall that the time constant of a first-order systetn with impulse response

is 1/a, which is the amount of time from r = that it takes the system step

= r&sponse i(0 to settle within \/e of its final value [i.e,, 5(¯^)

limf_£p>s<0]

Using this same quantitative definition, find the equation that must be solved in

order to deteirnine the time constant of the causal LTl system described by the

differential equation

n-^ ~d^ +

+ lOyit) = 9x{t).

(P6.49-1)

(b) As can be seen from part (a)> if we use the precise definition of the time constant set forth there, we obtain a simple expression fof the tirtie constant of a firstorder system, hut the calculations are decidedly more complex for the system of eq. (P6 49-1), However, show that this system can be viewed as the parallel interconnection of two first-order systems. Thus, we usually think of die sjistem of eq. (P6,49-l) as having two time constants, corresponding to the two firstorder factors. TATiat are the two time constants for this system'
<c) The discussion given in part (b) can be directly generalized to all systems with
impulse responses that are linear combinations of decaying exponentials. In any system of this type, one can identify the dominani time constants of the system, which are simply the largest of the time constants. These represent the slowest parts of the system response, and consequently, they have tbe dominant
effect on how fast the system as a whole can respond. What is the dominant
time constant of the system of eq. (P6.49-1)? Substitute this time constant into
the equation determined in part (a). Although the number will not satisfy the equation exacdy, you should .(^ee that it nearly does, which is an mdication that it is very close to the time constant defined in part <a>. Thus, the approach we have outlined in part (b) and here is of value in providing insight into the speed
of response of LTl systems without requiring excessive calculation.
(d) One important use of the concept of dominant time constants is in the reduction
of the order of LTl systems. This is of great prectical signi fierce in problems

Dhap. 6 Problems

501

t
Figure F6.49
involving the anaJysis of complex systems having a few dominant time constants and other very small time constants. In order to reduce the compleuty of the model of the system to be analyzed^ one often can simplify the fasi parts
of the system. That is, suppose we regard a complex system as a parallel interconnection cf ftrst- and second-order systems. Suppo^ also that one of these
sub^ystens, with impulse response ft(t) and step response s{t), is fast-- that is,
that s{i) settles to its final value j(i£) very quickly. Then we can approxirnate this subsystem by the subsystem that settles to the same final value instantaneously.
Thai is, if i(0 is die step re^)onse to our approxxtnation, then
This is iUustraied in H^uie P6.49, Note that the impulse response of the ap-

Hie utility of an approximation such as this depends upon the specific ^>phcation. In particular, one must take into accoutit
both how widely separated the different time constants are and also the namre of the inputs to be considered. As you will see from your answers in Ihis part of
the problem, the frequency response of the approiimate system is essentially the same as the frequency response of the original system at low frequenctes.
That is, wtien the fast parts of the system are sufficiently fast compart to Ihe
rate of fluctuation of the input, the approximation becomes useful.

Under certain condidGns, a continuous-time signa] can be completely represented by and

recoverable from knowledge of its values, or samples, at points equally spaced in time.

This somewhat surprising propeity foll&ws from a basic result that is refeTTcd to as the

sampling theorem. This theorem is extremely important and useful. It is exploited, for

example, in moving pictures, which consist of a sequence of individual fi-ames, each of which represents an instafltaneous view (i.e., a sample in time) of acontinuously changing
scene. When these samples are viewed in sequence at a sufficiently fast rate, we perceive

an accurate i^presentationof the original continucusJy moving scene. As another e?;ainple

printed pictures typically consist of a veiy fine grid of points, each ccrresponding to a

sample of the spatially continuous scene representBd in the pictuierif the samples are

sufficiently close together, the picture appears to be spatiaJly continuous, although under

a mollifying glass its representation in terms of samples becotnes evident.

Much of the importance of the sampling theorem also Ues in its role as a bridge

between continuous-time
me fact that under certain

signals and
conditions a

discrete-time signals. As we will see in this chapter,
continuous-time signal can be completely recovered

ftorti a sequtaicc of its samples provides a mechanism for representing a continuous-time

signal by a discrete-time signal. In many comejcts^processrag discrete-time signals is more

flexible and is often preferable to processing contitiuous-time sighals. This is due in laige

m part to the dramatic development of digital lechnoJogy over ibe past few decades, result-
ing the availability of inexpensive, lightweight, prograimnable, and easily reproducible

discrete-time systems. The concept of sampling, then, suggests atl CKtremely attractive

and widely employed method for using discrete-time systeiti technology to implement

continuous-time systems and process continuous-time signals: 1^ exploit sampling to

S

Sec 71

Representatiori of a Continuous-Time Signal by Its Samples: The Sampling Theorem 5 1

convert a continuous-time signal to a discrete-time signal, process the discrete -time signal using a discrete-ume system^ and then convert bact to continuous time.
In the following discussion, we introduce and develop the concept of sampling and
die process of reconstructii^ a continuous-time signal from its samples. In this discus-
sioft, wp both identify the conditions under which a coTitinuous-dme ^ignsil can \x exactly
reconstructed from its samples and examine the consequences when these conditions are not ,satisfied. Following this, we explore the processing of condituous-time signals that have been converted to disctete-time signals through sampling. Finally, we examme the
sampling of discrete-time signals and the related concepts of decimation and interpola-
tion.

7, 1 REFRESENTATTON OF A CONTJNUOUS-TlME SIGNAL BY ITS SAMPLES:
THE SAMPLrNG THEOREM
In general, in the absence of any additional conditions or infomiadon. we would not expect
that a signal could be uniquely specified by a sequence of equally spaced samples. For
example, in Figure 7.1 we illustrate tJnee different continuous-time signals, alt of which
have identical values at integer multiples of T; that is.

Clearly, an infitiite number of sigtials can generate a given set of samples. As we
-- will see, however, if ^ signal is band limited i,e., if its Fourier transform is zero outside
-- a finite band of frequencies nnd if the samples are taken sufficiently close together in
relation to the highest frequency present in the signal, then th& samples uniqueiy specify
the signal, and ve can reconstruct it perfectly This result^ known as the sampling theorem.
is of profound importance in the practical application of the methods of signal and system
anatysis-

7.1.2 Sampling with a Zenc^-Order Hold

The sampling theorem, which is most easily explained in terms of impul^e-lram sampling.

estabJishey the fact that a band-limited signal is uniquely represented by its samplcii. In

practice, however, narrow, large- amplitude pulses, which approximate impulses, are also

relatively difficult to generate ^nd transmit, and it is often mare convenient lo gener;ite the sampled signal in a form referred lo as a Zjero-order hold. Such a system samples xit) ac

a given instant and holds that value until the next instant at which a sample is taken, as illustrated in figure 7.5. The reconstruction of xif) from tbe output of a zero-order hold

can again be carried out by lowpasjj filtering. However, in this case, the required filter

no longer has constant gain in Che passband. To develop the required filter characteristic, we first note that the output j^>(r) of the zero-order hold can in principle be generated bv

7.2 RECONSTRUCTION OF ASiGNAL FROM ITS SAMPLES
USING I^^TFRPOLATION
Interpolation, that is, the fitting of ^ continuous signal to a set of sample values, is a commonly used procedure for reccujitructing a function, either approximatet^ or exactly,
from samples. One simple interpolation procedure is the zero^rder hold discussed in
Section 7.1. Another useful form of interpolation is linear interpolation, whereby adjacent sample points are connected by a sLriiight line, as illustrated in Figure 7.9. In more

The effect of undersampting, whereby higher frequencies ^re reflected mto Ic^wer

frequencies, is the principle on which the stroboscopic effect is based. Consider, for exam-

ple, the situation depicted in Figure 7.1 8, in which we have a disc rotating at a constant rate
^ wich a single radial line marked on the disc. The flashing strobe acts !t j^ampljng system,

When since it illunriinates the disc for extremely brief time Intervals at a periodic rate.

the

strobe fiiequ&ncy is much higher than the rotational speed of the disc, the speed of rotation

of the disc is perceived correctly. When the strobe freq|uency becomes less than twice the

rotational frequency of the disc, the rotation appears to be at alower ti^uency than is actu-

ally the case. Furthermore, because of phase reversal , the disc will appear to be rotating in

the wrong direction^ Roughly speakiiig, if we track the position of a fixed line on the disc
< at successive samples* then wbai lOo ct^ ^ < 2ati), so that we sample somewhat more fre-

quently than ottce per revolution, samples of the disc will show the fixed line in positions

that are successively displaced in a counterclockwise direction, opposite to the cloctwise

= rotationof the disc itself. At otfce flash per revolution, correaponding to toj(

&j[>, the radial

line appears stationary (iÈ,, the iDtational frequertcy of the disc and its harmonica have
A been aliased to zero frequency). similar effect is commonly observed in Western movies.

where the wheels of u. stagecoach appear to be rotating more shiwlj than would be consisifirtt with the CL>ach s forward motion, and sometimes Id the wrong direcuon. In this case, the sampling pTiot:ess corresponds to the fact that moving pictures are a sequence of individual frames with a rate (usually between 18 and 24 frames per ^lecond) corresponding
to the sampling frequency.
The preceding discussion suggests inccrpreting the strrboscopic effect as an exampleof a useful application of aliasing due to undcrsampling. Another practical application of aliasing arises in a measuring instnunent referred to as a sampling o^ciUoscope, This instniment is intended for obser\ing very high-frequency waveforms and exploits the prin ciples of sampling to alias these frequencies into ones that arc more easily displayed The sampling oscilloscope is explored in more detail in Problem 7.38.

7.4 DJSCRETE-T1ME PROCESSING OF CONTTNUOUS-TJME SIGNALS

Tn many applications, there is a significant advantage offered in processing a continuous-
time signal by first converting it to a discrete-time signal and, after discrete-lime process-
ing, converting back to a continuous-time signal. The discrete-time signal processing can be implemented with a general- or special-purpose computer, with microprocessors, or
with any of the variety of devices that are specifically oriented toward discrete-time signal
processing.
in broad terms, this approach lo continuous-time signal processing can be viewed as the cascade of three operations, as indicated in Figure 7. 19, where x^{t) artd y, it'} are continuous-time signals and x^[n\ andyj[nj are the discrete -time signals corresponding to
Xc{t) and >'t (/). The overall system is, of course, a continuous-time system in the sense that its input and output are bodi continuous- time signals. The theoretical ba^sis for converting
a continuous-time signal lo a discrete- time signal and reconstructing a continuous time signal frorn its discrete-rime representation lies in the sampling theorem, as discussed in Section 7,1. Through the process of periodic sampling with the sampling frequency consistent with the conditions oF the ?jampling theorem, the continuous-time signal xAi) is exactly represented hy a sequence of instantaneous sample values xAnTY, that is, the discrete-time sequence x^[n\ is related to .^^(0 by

To understand further the relationship between the contmuous-time signal x^t) ^nd
i ts discreie-time representation x-d [n\ , it is helpful to represent CID as a process of pe riodic
sampling followed by a mapping of the impulse train to a sequence. These two steps are illustrated in Figure 7.21. hi the first step, repi^senting the sampling process, the impulse train Xp{i) corresponds to a sequence of impulses with amplitudes corresponding to the sait^ples ijf Xc{(} and with a time spacing equal to dte sampling period T. Jn the conver-
sion from the impul.'w: train to the discrete-time sequence^ we obtain jr</[¨], correspotiding to the same seqnence of samples of x^it)^ but with tmity spacing in terms of the new in-
dependent variable n. Thus, in effect, the conversion from the impulse train sequence of samples to the discrete-time sequence of samples can be thought of as a normalization in time. This itormalizaiion in converting Xp{t)t!Q JCrfi^J is evident in Figures 7.21 (b) and (c),

cm output of the discrete-time filter is band limited, dowtisampling or decimation

be

applied-

Just as in some applications it is useful to downsample, there are situations in which

it is useful to convert a sequence to a higher equivalent sampiing rate, a process referred

ttJ as upsarr^ling or interpolation. Upsampling is basically the reverse of decimation or
downsampling. As illustrated in Figures 7.34 and 7.35^ in decimation we first sample and then retain only the sequence values at the sampling instants. To opsample, we reverse the process* For example, referring to Figure 7.34, we consider upsampling the sequence

Figure 7.37.

7.6 SUMh/lARY
In this chapter we have developed the concept ^rsanipling, whereby a continuous-lime or discrete-time sigtia.1 is represented by a seqiienc:e of equally sp^ed &amples. The con-
ditions under which the signal is exactly recoverable from the samples i$^ embodied in the sampling theorem. For exact leconstructioTi, this theorem requires that the signal to be satnpled be bat^d limited and that the sampling frequency be greater than twice the highest frequency in the signal to be sampled. Under these conditions, exact reconstruction of the original signal is carried ouc by tneans of ideal lowpass ftlterlng. The time-domain inteipretation of this ideal reconstruction procedure is often referred to a.s ideal bandlitnited interpolation, [n practical implementations, the lowpass fiher is approximated and the interpolation in the tiirie domain is no longer exact In some instances, simple inter-
polation procedures such as a zero-order hold or linear interpolation (a first-order hold)
suffice.
If a signal is undersampled(i.e,, if the sampling frcquenc> is less than that required by the sampling theorem), then the signal reconstructed by ideal band-limiced interpolation will be related to the original signal through a foam of distoition referred to as alia.'iing.
In many instances, it is important to choose the ^lamphng rate so as to avoid aliasing.
m However, there are a variety of important examples, such as the stroboscope, which
aliasing is exploited.
San^LpUng has a tmmber of impiTtant Applications. One particularly >^ignificaju set
of applications relates to using sampling to process continuous- time signals with discrete
time systems, bymeansof ininicompuiers, microprocessors, or any of a variety of devices
specifically oriented toward discrete-time signal processing.
The basic theory of sampling is similar for both continuous-time and discrete-
time signals. In the discrete-time case there is the closely relied concept of decimaLitm, whereby the decimated sequence Is obtained by extracting values of the original sequence
at equally spaced intervals. The difference between sampling and decimation lies in the
fact that, for ih& sampled sequence, values of zero Ue In between the sample values, whereas in the decimated sequence these zero values are discarded, thereby conipressing
the sequence in time The inverse of decimation is interpolation. The idea* of decima-

SS6

Sampling

Chap. 1

tion and mteipolation arise in a variety of important practical applications of signals and systems, including communication systems, digital audio, high-delinition television, and many other applications.

Communication systems

S.O INTRODUCTION

BB2

Communication systems play a k^y role in our modem world in transmitting information
between peopJe. systems, and computers, [n genend terms, in ail communication systems
the information at die source is fiist processed by a transmitter or moduJator to change it into a form suitable for transmission over ihe communication channel. At die receiver, the
signal is then recovered through appropriate processing. Tliis processing i& required for a variety of reasons. In particular, quite typjcaliy, any specific communication channel has
associated with it a frequency range over which ic is best suited for transmitting a signal and outside of which communication is severely degraded or impossible. For example,
the atmosphere will rapidly attenuate signals in the audible frequency range (10 Hz to 20
kHz), whereas it will propagate signals at a higher frequency range over longer distances. Thus, in transmitting audio signals such as speech or music over a communication channel
that relies on prop^ation through the atmosphere, the transmitter first embeds the signal through an appropriate process into another, higher fi^uency signal.
Many of the concepts and techniques we have developed in the earlier chapters of
this test play a central role in the analysis and design of communication systems. As with
m any concept that is closely tied a wide variety of important applications, there are a large
number of detailed issues to be considered, and* as indicated in the bibliography, there are many eicellent texts on the subject. While a full and detailed analysis of communication systems is well beyond the scope of our discussions here, with the background of the
previous chapters we are now in a position to introduce some of the basic principies and
issues encountered in the design and analysis of these systems.
The general process of embedding an inftjimation -bearing signal into a second signal is typically referred to as modulation. EKtr^ting the info rmadon -bearing signai

.

Sec. 8.1

Complex E^iponent^al and Stnusoidal Amplitude Modijlation

563

is known as demodulation. As we will see, modulation techniques not only allow us to

embed mfontiaiion into signals that can be transmitted effectively, but also mate possible

the siniulEaneous transmission of more than one signal with overl^ping spectra over the

same channel, through a concept referred to as multiplexing.

TTiere are a wide variety of modulation methods used in practice, and in this chapter

wc examine several of the most impottant of these. One large class of modulation meth-

AM ods relies on the concept o\. amplitude modulaiion or

in which the signal we wish to

A transmit is used to modulate the amplitude of another signal. ver^' common furm of am-

plitude modulation is sinusoidal amplitude tm?ditla.iion^ which we e^^ploie in some detail

in Sections 8.1-8.4 tctgether with the related concepts of frequency-division multiplexing.

AM Another important class of

systems involves the modulation of the amplitude of a

pulsed signal, and in Sections 8.5 and 8.6 we examine this form of modulation as well as
the concept of nme-divi$ion multiplexing. In Section H.7 we then exjitiine a different form

of modulation, namely sinusoidalfrequency moduiation in which the information -bearing

signal is used to vary the frequency of a sinusoidal signal

All of the discussion up through Section 8.7 focuses aLlention on continuous -time

signals, since most uansmission media, such as the atmosphere, are best thought of as

continuous-time phenomena. Nevertheless, not only is it ptissible to develop analogous

techniques for discrete-time signals, but it is ot considerable prat:tica] importance to con-

sider modulaticn concepts involving such signals, and ia Section 8.S we examine sonie of

the basic ideas behind the communication of discrete-lime signals.

a. 1 COMPLEX EXPONENTLAI. AND SfNUSOIDAl. AMPLITUDE MODULATION
MiL^j communication systems rely on the concept of sinusoidal amplitude modulation, in which a complex exponential or sinusoidal signal r(r) bxs its amplitude multiplied (modulated) by the information -bearing signal ^(0 The signal x{t) is typically referred to as the moduiatin^ signal and the signal c\t) as the carrier signal. The modulated signal y{t\ is then the product of Lhese two signals:

As we discussed in Section R.O. an important objective in modulation is to produce a

signal whose frequency range is suitable fur transmission over the ctimmunication channel

to be used. In telephone transmission systems, for example, long-dLstance transmission is

often accomplished over microwave or satellite linLs. The individual voice signal.^ are
in the frequency range 200 Ha :o 4 kHz, whereas a microwave link requires signals in

the range 300 megahertz (MHz) to 300 gigahertz (GHz), and commmiicatic-n satellite

MHz links operate in the Frequency range from a few hundred

to over 40 GH/.. Thus,

for transmission over Ýese ehannels, the information in a voice signal must be shifted

into these higher ranges of frequency. .A.s we will see in dils section^ sinusoidal amplitude

modulation achieves such Ý shift in frequency in a very simple manner

BAA Amplitude Modufatton with a Complex Exponential Carrier

There are two common forms of sinusoidal ampUtude modulation, one in which the c amer
signal is a complex exponential of the form

= c{t)

(:>^'f+^.i

(8,1)

sa4

Commirrrication Systeins

Chap d

typica! in a communication system. TTie corresponding effects of, and the need for, syn-

the frequencies of the carrier signals used in both, are eitplored in detail in Problem 3.23.

B,2.2 Asynchronous i:>emoduJation

chronous demodulation avoids the need for synchronization between the modulator and demodulator. In paiticular, suppose that jr(i) is always positive and that the carrier fre-
quency Wf is much higher than ta^^ the highest frequency in the modulating signal.

ciency of the system in terras of the po^ver in the output of the modulator and the quality

of the demodulated signnl. Theni are a number of advantages and disadvantages to the asynchronous modulation-
demodulation system of Figures 8Jl and 8J2, compared with the synchronous system of Figure 8.8, The synchronousi system requires a more sophisticated demodulator because the oscillator in the demodulator must be synchronized with the oscillator in the modu-
m lntoT, b<nh in phase and in frequency. On the othei hand, the asynchronous modulator

general requires transmitting more power than the synchronous modulator, since, for the

envelope detector to operate properly, the envelope must be positive, or equivalenily. there

must be a carrier component present in the tiansmitted signal. This is often preferable in

cases such as that asi^ociated with public radio broadcasting, in which it is deiirable to

mass- prod ace large numbers of receivers (demodulators) at moderate tost. The additional

On cost in transmitted power is then offset by the savings in cost for the receiver.

the

t>ther hand, in situations in which transmitter power requirements are at a premium, as

in satellite communication, the cost of implementing a more .'>ophisticated synchronous

receiver is warranted.

8.2 FREQUENCYDIVISIQN MULTIPLEXING
Many systems used for transmitting signals provide more bandwidth than is required for any one signal. For exaoiple, a typical micmwave link ha^^ a total bandwidth of several
gigahertz, which is considerably greater than the bandwidth required for one voice chan[jel. If the individual voice signals, which are overlapping in frequency^ have their frequency content shifted hy means of sinusoidal amplitude modulation so that the spectra of the modulated signals no longer overlap, they can be transmitted simultaneously over a single ^videband channci. The resulting concept is referred to ^^frequency-division multiplexing (FDM>, Frequency nil vision multiplexing using a sinusoidal carrier is illustrated in Figtire 8.15. Th& individual signals to be transmitted are assumed to be band limited and

Telephone communicalion is one i mport ant appJ icati on of frequency-division multi-

band.

In the United States, the use of rddio frequencies for tran-smitting signiils over the range
JO kHz to 275 GHz is controlled hy the Federal Communications Coinniisjjion, and

different portions of the range are allocated for different purposes. The current allo-

cation ot frequencies is shown in Figure 8.18. As indicated, the frequency range in

MHz AM AM the neighborhotjd of I

is asisigned to the

broadcast band, where

refers

AM specificaily to the use of sjtiu^oidal amphtude modulation. Individual

radic sta-

AM tions are assigned specifit; frequencies within the

band, and thus, nnany statLons

can broadcast simultaneously through this use of frequency-divjsion multiplexirg. In

principle, at the receiver, an individual radio station can be selected by dcmultiplcx-
mg and demodulating* as illuscratt:d in Figure 8.17. The tuning dial on the receiver
would then conmd both the ceriier frequency of the bandpass filler and ihe frequency of

the demodulating oscillator. In fact, for public broadcasting, ajiynchronous modulation

and demodulation are used to simplify the receiver and reduce its co^t Furthermore,

the demultipleAing in Figure 8.17 requires a sharp cutoff bandpass filter with variable

center frequency. Variable frequenc) -selective filters are difficult to implement, and

consequently, -j, fixed tiller is implemented instead, ar^d an intermediate stage t>f mod-

ulation and filtering [referred to in a radio receiver as the intermediate -freqaencx (IF)

stage] is u'ied. The 'js^ of modulation to slide the spectrum of the signal past a fixed

bjinclpass hiter replaces the use of a variable bardpass filter in a manner similar to the

procedure discussed in Section 4.5.1. This basic procedure is inci?rpti rated into typical

AM home

cadirh retreivens. Some of tbe more detailed issues involved are considered in

Problem 8.36.

ure 8.15 the spet;trum of each individual signal is replicated at both positive and negative

frequencies, and thus the modulated signal occupies twice the bandwidth of thi^ <>riginal.

This represents an ii^etficietit use of bandwidth In the next i^ection we c^msider an al-

ternative form of sinusoidal amplitude modulation, which leads in mere efficient use of

bandwidth at the cost of a more complicated modulation system.

wm frequencies, where

ls the highest frequency present in x(t). With the use of a

the frequency band over which there is energy from the signal is still 2*^,*,, although die

modulated siignal is now compJes. With a sinusoidal carrier, on the other hand, the spec-

trum of ihe signal is shifted to +&i^ anti -w^, and thus, twice the bandwidth is required.

This suggests diat there is a basic redundancy in the modulated signal A^ith a sinusoidal

cairier. Using a technique referred to as singie-sideband maduUition. we can remove the

redundancy.

8,5.2 Time-Dtvlsion Multiplexing
Amplitude modulation with a pulse-train carrier is often used to transmit several signals
over a single channel. As indicated in Figure 8. 23, the modulated output signal v<fl is nonzcn^ only when the carrier signal c(l) is on fie., is nonzero). During the intervals in
wMch c(0 is off, other similarly modulated signals can be transmitted. TWo cquivalcnl
repieKentatioiis of this process are ahtjwn in Figure S.25. In this technique for transmitting several signals over a single channel, each signal is in effecl assigned a set of time slijts
A of duration that repeat every T seconds and that do not overlap with the slots assigned
to other signals. The Einaller the ratio A/7, the liirger the number of signals that t:in be
transmitted over the channel. This procedure is referred to as time-diviiion multiplexing
(TDM). Whereas; trequencyKiiviMon multiplexing, as discussed in Section 8.3, assigns
diff&TsnxJrequency intervals to individual signals, timeKiivision multiplexing assigns different fime intervals to individual signals. Demultiplexmg the individual signals from the composite signal in Figure 8. 25 is aceomphshed by time gating, to select the particular time slots ass[>ciated with each individual signal.

The strategy indicated in the preceding paragraph assumes that the traiiHmitted

pulses remain distinct as they propagate over the communication channel. In traxiNTnis-

sion through any realistic channel, however, the pulses can be expected to be distorted

through effects such as additive noise and filtering. Additive noise in the channel will,

of course, introduce ampliLude errors at the sampling times. Filtering due to the nonideal

frequency response of a channel causes a smearing of the individual pulses that can cause

the received puhes to overlap in rime. This interference is illustratttJ in Figure 8.28 and

is referred to as intersymbpl interference.

8.&3 Digital Pulse'Amplitude and Pulse-Code Modulation

PAM The

system described in The preceding suhaections involves the use of a diincrete set

of samples to modulate a sequence of pulses. Hiis set of samples can be thought ot as a

discrete-time signal -v[n], and in inany applications j;[¨l is in fact stored in or generated b>' a digital system, in such cases, the limited word length of a digital system implies that j:[^J Ciin take on only a finite, quantized set of values, resulting in only a finite set of possible

amplitudes for the modulated pulses.

PAM In fact, in many cases this quantized form of digital

is reduced to a system

-- -- using only a tew typically^ only two amplitude values. In particula^r, if each sample of

i[n} is represented as a binary number (i.e.. a finite string of O's and 1 \X then a pulse

with one of two possible values (one value corresponding to a and one value to a I ) can

be set for each binary digit, orbit in the string. More generally, in ordetto protect a^ainsl

tr^smission errors or provide secure communication, the sequence of binary digits rep-

resenting x[n] might first be transformed or encoded into another sequence of O's and i s
before transmission. For example, a very simple error detection mechamsm is tti transmit

one additional modulated pulse for each sample of x[nU representing a /'t:fWl> check. That

is, this additional bit would be set to I if the binary represetitation of x[ji\ has an odd number of Ts in it and to if there is an even number of 1 's. The receiver can dien che^^k the

received parity bit iigainst the other received bits in order to detect inconsistencies. Mortf

complex coding and error correction schemes can certainly be employed, and the design

of codes with particular desirable properties is an important component of communication

PAM system design. For obvious reasons, a

system modulated by an encoded sequence of

O's and I 's is referral to as a. pulse-code modulation (PCM) .^iystem.

8.7 SINUSOIDAL FREQUENCY MODULATION

In the preceding sections, we discussed a immber of specific amplitude Tnodulation sys-

tcma in whicb the modulating signal was used \o vary the amplitude of a sinusoidal or

a pulse carrier. As we hiive seen, i^uch ^y^stems are amenable tv detailed analysis using

tbt frequency-domain techniques we developed in preceding chapters In another very

important class of modulation techniques referred to ss frequency modulation {FM}, the

modulating signal is u$edto control the frequency of a sinusoidal carrier. Modulation sys-

teins of this tjpe have a number of advantages over amphtude tnodulatioti systems. As

suggested by Figure 8.10, with sinusoidal amplitude modulation the peak amplitude of

-- the envelope of the carrief is directly dependent on the amplitude of the modulating signal
xit)^ which can have a large dynamic range i.e,, can vary significantly. With frequency

FM modulation, the envelope of the carrier is constant. Consequently, an

transmitter can

FM always operate at peak power. In addition, in

systems, amplitude variations introduced

over a transmission channel due to additive disturbances or fading can^ to a large extent,

be eliminated at the receiver. For this reai^on. in public broadcasting and a. variety of other

FM AM contexts^

reception is typically belter than

reception. On the other hand, as we

will see, frequency modulation generally requires greater bandwidth than does sinusoid:d

amplitude modulation.

Frequency modulatimi systems are highly nonlinear and, Cftuseqtseittly, are not as

straightforward to anaJyze as are the amplitude modulation systems dii^cussed in the pre
ceding sections. However, the methods we have developed in earlier chapters do allow us

tu gain some understanding of the nature and operation of these systems.
We begin by introducing the general notion of dn^^e modulanon. Consider a sinu-

soidal carrier expressed in the form

cU) = Af:oiiia^t + 0,) = Acos9{n,

(8.30)

where ^(f) = w.r H- 6^ andwheretij^ is the frequency andfl^ the phase of ^e carrier. Angle
moduiatiiju, in general, corresponds to using the modulating signal to change or vary the
angle 6(i). One form that this somedmes takes is to use the modulating signal xU) to vary
the phase Oi so that the modulated signal takes the form

yif) = A coslwj t + d, (01 where 0^ is now a function of time, specifically of the form
^M = ^0 + k^xQl

J (3 1
(S32)

discrete time Furthermore, as explored in Problem 8.48, we can also consider using
a distiete-titae signal to modulate a pulse train, leading to time-division multiplexing
of discrete-tiine signals.
The liviplemcntation of discrete- time multiplexing systems provides an exceiient ex-
ample of the flexibility of discrete-time processing in general arid the importance of the
PDM Gperdtvon of ups^mpling (see Section 7.5,2) in particular. Consider a diacrete-tirrie
M M system with sequences that we wish to fretjuency-division multiplex. With channels,
it is required that the spectral energy for each input channel Xi[n\ be band limited^ that is.

If the sequences originally occupied the entire frequency band corresponding, for example, to having sampled a set of continuous-time signals at die Nyquistrate, then they would first have icj be converted to a higher sampling r^e (i.e^ upsampJed) before frequency- division multiplexing. This idea is explored further in Problem R.33.

8.8.2 Discrete^Tlm^ IVansmodulation

One context in which discrete-time modulation is widely used, together with the oper-

ations of decimat]{>n, up&ampling, and interpolatiot^ ititroduced in Chapter 7, is digital

communication systems. Typically, in such systems continuous -time signals are trans-

mitted t>ver communication channels in the form of discrcte-time signals, obtained by

sampling. The continous-time signals are often in the form of time-division -multiplexed

(TDM) or frequency-division -multiplexed (FDM) signals. The signals are then converted

to discrete-time sequences whose values are represented digitally, for storage or long-

distance transmission. Iti some systems, because of different con.straints or requirements

at the transmjtting end and the receiving end, or because sets of signals that have been in-

dividually mijltipkxed by different methods are then multiplexed together, diere is often

TDM the requirement for converting from sequences representing

signaJs to sequences

representing FT>M signals or vice versa. This conversion from one modulation or multi-

plexing scheme to another is referred to as rransmodsjtalion or transftiultipUxing, In the

context of digital conununication systems, one obvious way of implementing cransmulti-

plexing is to convert back, to continuous -time signals, demultiplex and demodulate, and

then modulate and multiplex as required. However, if the new signal is then to be con-

verted back t(J a discrete-time signal, it is clearly more efficient for the entire process to

be carried out directly in tiie discrete-time dotnain. Figure &.44 shows^ in block diagram

TDM FDM form, the steps involved in converting a discrete-time

signal to a discrete-time

TDM signal. Note thatj after demultiplexiiYg the

signal, each channel must be upsampled

in preparation for frequency-divisioti multiplexing.

8.9 SUMMARY
In this chapter, we have examined a number of the basic concepts associated with t;om
m municallan systems. In particular, we have examined die concept of modulation, which
a signal we wish to communicate is used to modulate a second ^ignnl referred to as the

Sec. 3.9 Summary

625

carrier, and we have looked in detail at the concept of amplitude modulation. The proper-
ties of amplitude modulation are most easily interpreted in the frequency domain through
the multiplication property of the Fourier transform. Amplitude mc£dulaiiori with a complex exponential or sirtusoidal carrier istypically used to shift the spectrum of a signal in frequency and is applied, for example, in comnmnication systems to place the spectrum in a frequency range jiuitahle for transmission anti to permit frequency -division multiplexing. Variatitms of sinusoidal amplitude modulatioji, such a.s the insertior^ of a carrier signal for asynchronous systems and single- and dcuble-sidcband syslem.s, were
discussed.
We alSLk examined several other form-, of modulation -based coTrimunication.
In this regard, we brietly intmduced the concepts of frequeticy and phase rrwidijlation. Although these forms of modulation are more difficult en analyze in detail, it
IS possible to gain significant insight into their charactenstics through the frequency domain.
We further examined in some dt;uil amplitude modulation of 3 pulsed signal^
which Jed us to the concepts of time-division multiplexing and pulse amplitude modulation, in which successive sampler of a discrete-time signal ^re used to modulate the amplitude of a sequence of pulses. This led in turrt to an examination of discrete-time modulation and digital communication, in which the flexibility of discretetime processing facilitates the design and implementation of more sophisticated ccmmunication systems involving concepts such as pulse-code modulation and
transmudulation.

H.Z9. Single sideband modulation is tommonly used in point-to point voice comitiunicatson. It offers; many advantages, including effective use of available power, oonservation of bandwidth, and insensitivity to some forms of random fading in the channel. In double-sideband suppressed carrier (DSB/SC) systems the spectrum
m of the modulating signal spears in its entirety in tv/o places the transrnitted
spectrum. Single- sidet>and modulation eliminates this redundancy, thus conserving bandwidth and increasing the signal-to-noise ratio within the remaimrii^ portion of the spectrum that is transmitted.
In Figure P3.29(a), two systems for generating an amplitude-modulated single-sideband signal are shown. 9
The LAPLACE TRANSFORM

9.0 INTRODUCTTON

654

In the preceding chapters, we have seen that the tools of Fourier analysis are extremely useful in the study of many probiemsof practical importance involving signals atwd LTI
systems. This is due in large partro thefaci thai broad classes of signals can be represented as linear combinations of periodic complex exponentials and that c{>mpSex e:cponentials are eigen functions of LTI systems. The continuous-tinne Fourier transform provides us with a representation for signals as linear cotnbtn^ors of complex c?tpanentials of the form
e^' with s = j<i>. However the eigenfunctton property introduced in Section 3.2 and many
of its consequences apply as well for arbitrary values of s and not only those values that are purely iniaginary. This observation leads to a generalization of the eontinuous-time
Fourier transfomi, known as the Laplace transform, which we develop in this chapter. In
the next chapter we develop the corresponding discrete-time generalization known as the
^-transform.
As we will see, d>e Laplace and z-transforms have many of the properties that make
Fourier analysis so useful. Moreover, not only do diese transfottns provide additional tools and insights for signals and systems that can be analyzed using the Fourier transform, but ihey also can be appMed in some very itnportant coMexts in which Fourier transforms cannot. For example Laplace and ^-transforms can be applied to the analysis of many un stable systems and consequently play an important role in the investigation of the stability or instability of systems. This fact£ combined with the algebraic properties that Laplace and ^-transforms share with Fourier transforms, kads to a very important set of totds for
system analysis and in particular for the analysis of feedback systems, which we develop
in Chapter 1 1

Sec, 9,1

The Laplace Jrarisfornn

6S5

Comptaring eqs. (9.14) and (9.19), we see that the algebraic expression for the

Laplace transform is identical for both of the signals consideied in Examples 9,1 and 9,2,

However, from the same equations, we also see that the set of values of s for which the

expression is valid is very different in the two examples. Ttiis serves to illustrate the fact

that, in specifying the Laplace transform of a signal, both the algebraic expres&ion and

the range of valties of s for which this expression is valid ajie required. In general, the

range of values of s for which the integral in eq.<9.3) converges \% referred to as the region

of convergence (which we abbreviate as ROC) of the Laplace transform. That is, the

ROC consists of those values of j = o- -k- jto for which the Fourier transform of a(/)^"*"

ROC converges. We will have more to &ay about the

as we develop some insight into the

properties of the Laplace transform.

sometimes referred to as the cr-axis and the jw-axis, respectively. The shaded region in

Figure 9.1(a) represents the set of points in ihe s-plane corresponding to the region of

convergence for Example 9. L The shaded region in Figure 9.1(bi indicates the region of

convergence for Example 9.2.

For rational Laplace transforms, the roots of the nutnerator polynomial are com-
monly referred to as the zeros of X(j), since, for those values of s. XU) = 0. The roots
of the denommator polynomial are referred to a& the puiex of X(ji\ and for those values of J, X{s) is infinite. The poles and &eros of X{s) in the finite i-plane completely char-
acterize the algebraic expression for X{s) to within a scale factor. The repte mentation of
X(s) through iu poles and zeros in the .t-pUne is referred to as the p^fle-zero plot of XC^).

However, as we saw in Examples 9.1 and 9.2, knowledge of the algebraic form of X(j) does

ROC not by itself identify the

for the Laplace transfomn. That is. a complete specification,

to within a scale factor, of a rational Laplace transform consists of the pole-tero plot of

ROC the transform, together with its

(which i& coimnonly shown as a shaded region in the

j-plane, as in Figures 9.1 and 9.2).

Also, while they arc not needed to specify the algebraic form of a rationaJ transfonn

Xii), it is sometimes convenient to refer to poles or zeros of X(j) at infinity. Specifically,

if the order of the denommator polynomial is greater than the order of ihc numerator poly-

nomial, then S'C^ will become zero as f approaches infimty. Conversely, if the order of the

numeratcir polynomial is greater dian die order of the denominator, then Xis) will become

unbounded as s approaches infinity. This behavtor can be interpreted as leros or poles at

infinity. For e?;ample. the Laplace transform in eq. (9.23) haj* a denominator of order 2 and

a numerator of order only 1, so in this case Xis) has one zero at infinity. The same is true

for the transfomi in eq, (9.30)£ in which the numerator is of order 2 and the denominator is

of order 3. In geneml, if the order of die denominator exceeds the order nf the numerator by k, X(s] will have k zeros at iniit^ity. Similarly* if the order of the numerator exceeds the

order of the denominator by k, X{s) witl have k poles at infinity.

= the zero at .v a to the point ii . The vaJue ofXis 1 1 then has a magnitude that is the length

From our graphical interpretation, we can alsf) see how changing the time constant
or, equivalently, the position of the polcof H(:t) changes the characteristics of afirst-Lrder system. In particular, as the pole moves farther into the left-half plane, the bieak frequency and, hence, the effective cutoff frequency of the system increases. Alsti, from eq. (9.75)
and from Figure 6. 19, we see that this same movecnent of the pok to the left corresponds
to a decrease in the time constant t, resulting in a faster decay of the impulse response and a correspondingly faster rise time in the step response. This relationship hetween the real part ftf the pole locations and the speed of the system response holds more generally;
that is, poles farther away frorn the /w-axis are associated with faster response terms in
the impulse response.

9.7 ANALYSIS AND CHAI2ACTERJZATJON OF LTI SYSTEMS USING
THE LAPLACE TRANSFORM

One of the important applications of the Laplace transform is in the analysis and character-
ization of LTI systems, Itsrole for this classof systems stems directly fn>m the convotution property (Section 9.5.6). Spet:iflcally. the Laplace transforms of the inpuL and output of an LTI sy.stem are related through multiplication by the Laplace transform of the impulse response of the system. Thus,

Yis) = mÈ)X{iX

fy.ll2)

where X(s), Y{sX and His) are the Laplace transforms of the input, output, and impulse
m response of the system, respectively. Equation (9.112) is the counterpart, the context
of Laplace transforms, of eq. (4.56) for the Fourier transfomr. In fact, for s = jw. each

of the Laplace transforms in eq. (9. 112) reduces to the respective Fourier transform, and

the equation correspondJs exactly to eq. {4.5b). Also, from our discussion in Section 3,2

im the response of LTI systems to complex exponentials, if the input to an LTI system is
= xiO e", then the output will be H(s)e'''; i.e., e^' lA an eigenfunction of the system with

eigenvalue equal to the Laplace transfonn of the impulse response.

of the Laplace transform, H(x) is commonly referred to as [he !;y.-^tem pncTwn or, alternatively the transfer function. Many properties of LTI systems can be closely associated

Abo, as illustrated in Problem 9.3S, a fourthorder system function can be written as the prodiictof two second-order system functions, each of which can be represent^ in a number of ways (e.g,, direct fbrm^ cascade, or parallel), and it can also be written as the stjm t>f lower order terms, each of which has sev-
eral different representations. In this way, simple low-order systems can serve as huilding blocks for the implenieniation of more cornplcx^ higher crder systems.

9.10 SUMMARY
In this chapter, we Itave develop&i aiid studied the Laplace transform, which c^n be viewed
as a gener^ixation of the Fourier transform. It is particularly useful as an analytical tool in the analysis and study of LTI systems- Because of the properties of Laplace transforms, LTI
systems, including those represented by line^u- constant-coefficient difTerential equations,
can be characterised and analyzed in the tran^fonn dotnain by algebraic jnanipulations. In addition, system function algebra provides a convenient tool both for analyzing interconnections of LTI systems and for constructing block diagram tepresentations ofLTI systems dE^scnbed by diffetenhal equations.
For signals and systems with rational Laplace transforms, the tratisform is often conv&nienfly represcinted in the complcj; plana (j-platw) by marking the locations of the poles
and zeros and indicating the r&gion of convergence. From the pole-zeio plot, the Fourier
transfrain can be geometrically obtained, within a scaJitig fact<^^ Causality, stability, and oiber characteristics are also ea.sily identified from knowledge of the pole locations and the region of convergence.
In this chapter, we have been concerned primarily with the bilateral Laplace trans-
form. However, we also introduced a somewhat different form of the Laplace transform known as the unilateral Laplace transform. The unilateral transform can b<e interpreted as
the bilateral transform of a signal whaae values prior to / = 0~ have been set to zcrc.
'Hiis form of the Laplace transform is especially useful for analyzing systems described by linear constant-coefficient differential eqiiations with nonzero initial conditions.

10.0 INTRODUCTION
In Chapcer 9, we developed the Laplace transform as an extension of the continuous-time Fourier transform. This ejttension was motivated in part by the fact that ii can be applied to a broader class of signals than the Fourier transform can, since there are many sig-
nals for which the Fourier transform does not converge but the Laphice transform does.
The Laplace transform allowed us, for example, 'to perform transform analysis of un&table systems and to develop additional insights and tools for LTl system analysis.
In this chapter, we u$e the same approach for discrete time as we develop tiie ztransform. which is the discrete-time counterpart of the Laplace transform. As we will
see, the motivationsi for and propeitLes of the ^-transform closely parallel those of the Laplace transform. Just a* with the relationship between continuous-time and discrete-
time Fourier transfontiA, however, we will encounter some important distinctions between
the s-u-ansform and the Laplace transform that arise froiti the fundamental differences between continuous-time and discrete-time signals and systems.
10,1 THE z-TRANSFORM
As we saw in Section 3,2, for a discrete- time linear time-invariant system with impulse
response h\n\, the response y[nl of the system to a complex eiponeittial input of the form
^ is
74T

742 where

In discussing the L^lace transform in Chapter 9, we remarked that for a rational

ROC Laplace transform, the

is always bounded by poles or infinity. We observe that in

the foregoing examples a similar statement applies to the ^-transform, and in fact, this is

al^ay^ true:

ROC Property 7: Tf the ^-transform Xiz) of x[n] is rational, then its

is bounded by

poles or extends to infinity.

Combining Property 7 with Properties 4 and 5, we have

ROC -- Property 8r If the r'transfomi Xi^) of x[n] is raticmaL and if x[n] is right sided, then

the

is the region in the ^-plane outside the ciutermosi pole i.e., oiiLside the circle

of radius equal to the largest magnitude of the poles of X(z). Furthermore, if x[n] is

< ROC causal (i.e., if it is right sided and equal to for n 0), then the

also includes

Thus, for right-sided sequences with rational transfomrs, the poles are all closer to
the origin than is any point in the ROC.

T 0.8 SYSTEM FUNCTION ALGEBRA AND BLOCK DIAGRAM
REPRESENTATIONS
Just as with the Laplace transform i;i cotitinuous tim¨, the ^-transform in discrete tirne allows us to replace time-domain operations such as convolution and time shifting with
algebraic operations. This was expltjited iti Section 10.73, where we were able to replace the difference-equation description of an LTl system with an algebraic description. The
use of the ^-transform to convert system descriptions to algebraic equations is also helpful in analyzing interconnections ofLTI systems and in representing and synttiesiiing ftystetris as interconnections of basic system building blocks.

continuous-time systems in Section 9,8. 1 , For example, the system function for the cascade of two discrete-time LTI systems is the product of the system functions for the individual systems in the cascade. Also, consider the feedback interconnection of two systems, as
shown in Figure 10. H. it is relatively involved to determine the difference equation or im-

The concepts used in constructing block-<liagram representations in the preceding examples can be applied directly to higher order systems, and several e^an^ples are considered in Problem 10.39. As in continuous time^ there in typically considerable flejLibiUty
-- in doing thin e.g., in hovv numerator and denominator factors are paired in a product rep-
resentation as in eq. (10 J 23), in the way in which each factor is implemented, and in the order in which the factors are cascaded. While all of these ^ariations lead to representations of the same system, in practice there are differences in the behaviur of the different
block diagrams. Specifically, each block-diagram representation of a system can be tranjilated directly into a computer algorithm for the iTnplementaUoii of the system. However, becaj.se the finite word length of a computer necessitates quantiz^ing the ci>effidenls in the block diagram and bec^usi^ there is numerical roundoff as the algorithm operates^ each of these representations will lead to an algorithm that only appn^xi mates the behavior of the original system. Moreover, the enrtrs in each of these approximations will be somewhat
different. Because of these differences, considerable efft>rl has been put into examinmg
the relative meriLs of the various block-diagram representations in terms of their accuracy and sensitivity to quanti/ation effects. For discussions of this subject, the reader ma>' turn
t{) the references on digital signal processing in the bibliography at the end of the book.

1 0.9.2 Properties of the Unilateral z-liansform

The unilateral ^-transform has many important properties, some of which are identical to

their bilateral counterparts and several of which differ in significant ways. Table 10.3 sum-

marizes these properties. Note that we have not included a column explicitly identifying

ROC ROC the

for the uniJateral ^-irajisfonn for each sjgnal, sint^e the

of any ujiiJateral r-

for a rational unilateral

B> ct>ntrasting this table with the corresponding Table 10.1 for bilateral ^-transtorms,
-- we can gain con.siderable insight into the nature of the unilateral cratisfcrrn. In particular
several properties namely, linearity, scaling in the ^'-domain, time e?^pansion, conjuga-
-- tion, and differentiation in the ^-domain are identical to their bilateral counterparts, as

is the imtial-vaJue tbeorem stated in Section 10.5,9, which is fundamentally a unilateral
< transform property, since it requires x[n] ^ G for /i 0. One bilateral property, namely,

the time reversal property set forth ir S&ction 10.5.4, obviously has rio meaningful coun-

teipart for the unilateral transfonn, while the remaining properties differ in important ways between the bilateral and anilaieral cases.

An important point to note here is that the convolution pmperty for unilateral z-
transforms applies only if the signals x^[h\ and x^Ch] in eq, <10.138) are both identicaJly
< zem forn 0. While it is generally true that the bilateral transform of j;t[nl*.v*[rt] equals
theproduct of the bilateral transforms of jci [n] and jtiIm], the unilateral trauKfonn of ^ [n]*
JC2[n] in general does not equal the prtxiuct of the unilateral transforms if jti [nl or x^in] is
nonzero for n < 0. This point iÈ explored further in Problem 10.41 Much of the importance of the unilateral z-tmn.sform lies in it& application to analys-
ing causal systems and, in particular, systems characterized hy linear constant-coefficient
difference equations with possibly nonzero initial condicions. In Section 10,7 we saw
-- how the bilateral transform particularly the shifting property for bilateral z-transforms

10, TO SUMMARY

In this chapter, we have developed the z-transform for discrete-time signal k and system-s.

TTte discussion and devdopmetJt closely paralkfed tfte comesponditig treatnient of the

Laplace transform for continuous-tincie signals, hut with some important differences. Foi

example, in the complex j-plane the Laplace transform reduces to the Fouiier transform on

the imaginary axis, whereas in the complex z-plane the z-transform reduces to the Fourier

ROC transform on the unit circle. For the Laplace transform the

consists of a strip or

half-plane (i,È,, a snip extending to infinity in one direction), whereas for the ;-trai\sform

ROC the

is a ring, perhaps extenditig outward to infitiity or inward to include the origin

M With the Laplace transf<in7J, time-domajn characferistics such aÈ the righi-sided, left-

sided, or two-sided nature of a sequence and ttie causality or stability of an LTI system can be associated v^ith properties of the region of convergence, \n particular, for rational

^-transforms, the$e time-domain characteristics can be associated with the pole locations

in relation to the region of convergence.

fifeL^aui^e of the properties of ^-transforms, LTI systems, includitig those described

by linear constant-coefficient difference equations, can be analyzed in the trEuisform domain by algebraic manipulaiions. System functit>n algebra also is a very useful tool for

the analysis of interconnections of LTi systems and for the construction of block diagram

representations of LTI systems described by difference equations.

CTiap. 1

Problems

797

For the most part in this chapter, we have focused on bilateral ^-transforms. However*

as with the Laplace transfomi, we have also introduced a second form of the ^-transform

known as the unilateral ^-transform. The unilateral traDsform, which can be viewed as the

< bilateral transform of a signal whose values for n

have been set to zero, is particularly

useful for analyzing systems described by linear constant-coefficient difference equations

with nonzero initial conditions.

Linear feedback systems

1 T.O INTRODUCTION

816

-- IL has long been recognized Chat in many situations there arc particular advantages to be
gaitied by using feedbacic that is, by using the output of a system to control or modify
the input. For example, it is common in electroinechanical systems, such as a motor whose
shaft position is to be maintained ^t a constant angle, to measure the eirrot between the desired and the tme po^^itjon and to use this error in the form of a signal to tum the shaft
in the appropriate du^cdon. This is illustrated in Figure 11.1, where we have depicted the use of a dc motor for the accurate pointing of a telescope. In Figure 1 LlU) we have indicated pictorially what such a system would look like, where v{t) is the input voltage to the motor and d{t) is the atigulax position of the telescope platform. The block diagram
for the motor-driven pointing system is shown in Figure II 1(b). A feedback system for
controlling the position of the telescope is illustrated in Figure 1 1 .1(c), and a block diagram equivalent to this system is shown in Figure 1 1 .1(d). The eitemal, or reference, input to
A this feedback system is the desired shaft angle Or,. potentiometer is used to convert the
angle inro a voltage K[ 6^ proportional to ifn. Similarly, a second potentiometer produces a voltage K] e(/) proportional to the actual platform angle. These two voltages are compared, producing an error voltage K] |S/3 -- &{f)], which is amplified and then used to drive the
electric motor
Figure 11.1 suggests two different methods for pointing the telescope . One of these is the feedbiick sysLem of Figures 11.1(c) and (d). Here£ the input that we must prcjvide
is the desired reference angle 6j). Alternatively, if the initial angle, the desired angle, and
the detailed electrical and mechanical characteristics of the motor-shaft assemblj were
known exactly, we could specify the precise history of the input voltage v{i) that would
first accelerate and then decelerate the shaft, bringing the platform to a stop at the desired

Linear Feedback Systems

Chap. 1

A jM^Mtion Wr'ithi^uV the use of fetdb^c^t, as in Figures 1 [ . Ka> and (t>). system opeiating in
accordaiit;e with Figures 11. Ua) and (b) is typically referred to as an open-loop xysiem, in contrast to th^ ctosed-loop system of Figur^i 11.1(c) and (dt. In s practical environment, there are clear advantages to controlling the motor-shaft angle with the closed-loop system rather than with the open-loop systeoL For example, in the closed-loop system, when the shaft has been rotated *o the correct poiition, any disturbance from this position will be sensed, and the resulting eiror will be used to provide a correction. In the open-
loop system, there is no mechanism for providing a correction. As anoTher advantage nf
the ch]sed-loop system, consider th^ effect of errors in modeling the characteristics of the motor-shaft assembly. In the open -loop system, a precis* charactenzaUon ot the system is
required to design the correct input. In the closed-loop system, the input is simpl^' the de-
sired shaft angle and does not require precise knowledge of the systeitt. This inscnsilivity of the closed-loop system to disturbance!; and to imprecise knowledge of the system are two inaportant advantages of feedback.
The control ^)f an electric mot[>risjust one of agreat many examples in whith feed-
back plays an itnportatit role. Similar uses of feedback can be found in a wide variety ^)f applications, such as chemical process control, automotive fuel systems, household heating systems, and aerospace systems, to natne just a fev^. In addition, feedback is also present
in many biological processes and in the control of human motion. For example, when a
person reaches for an object, it is usual during the reaching process to monitor visually the distance between the hand and the object so that the velocity of the haad can be smoothly
decreased as the distance (i.e., the error) between the hand and the object decreases. The effcctivftT^ess of using the system output (hand posuion) t<i contTi>l ibe input is clearly demonstrated by alternatively reaching with and without the use of visual feedback.
Jn addition to its use in providing anerTor-correcting mechanism that can reduce sen-
m sitivity lu disturbances and to errors in the modeling of the system that i^ be controlled,
another imponant charactenstie of feedback is its potential for stabilizing a system that is
m inherently unit^ble. Consider the problem of trying to balance a broomstick the palm
(]f the hand. If the hand is held stationary, small disturbances (such a^ a slight breeze or
inadvertent motion of the hand) will cause the broom to fall over Of course, if one knows
exactly whar disturbances will occur, and if one can contn>l the motion of the hand per-
fectly, it is possible to determine in advance how to move the hand to balatice the broom. This is clearly unrealistic; however, by always moving the hard in the direction in which the broom is fallings the broom can be balanced. This, of course^ requires feedback Ji
A order to f>ense the direction in which the brwim is faUing. second example that is closely
related to the balancing of a broom is the problem of contrail ling aso-callcd mvcrted pendulum, which is illustrated in Figure 1 1.2. As shown, an inverted pendulum consists of a thin rod with a wi^ight at the top. The bettom of the rod is mounted on a cart that can move
in either direction along atrack. Again, if the cart is kept stationary, the inverted pendiiluTn

Figure M.2 An inverted pendu turn

Sec. 11. 1

Linear Feedback Systerrts

8T9

will topple over. The problem of stabilizing the pendulum is one of designing -a feedback iysiem th^l will move ihe cart U^ keep the pendulum vertical This example is examined
A in Problem 1 1.5G. third example, which again bears some similarity to the balancing of
a bnHJTTi, is the problem of controlling the trajectory of a rocket. In this case, much as the movement of the hand is used to compensate for <iif>turbiioces in the position of the broom,
the direction of the thrust of the rocket is used to correct for changes in aerodynamic forces and wind disturbances that would otherwise caus& the rocket to deviate from its course. Again, feedback in. imponant, bei:ause these forces and disturbances are never precisely knoivn in advance.
The preceding examples provide some indication of why feedback may be useful. In the next two sections we introduce the basic: block diagrams atxl equations for linear
feedback systems and discuss in more detail a number of applications of feedbatik and
control, both in continuous time and in discrete time. We also point out how feedback can
have harmful as well as useful effects. These examples of the uses and effects of feedback
will give us some insight into how changes in the parameters in a feedback control system
lead to changes in the behavior of the syste m. Understanding this rel ation shi p i s es sen li a.1 ni designing feedback systems that have certain desirable characteristics. With this material
as background, we will then develop, in the remaining sections of the chapter, several
specific techniques that are of sjgniticant value in the analysis and design of cantinuoustime and discrete- time feedback systeins.

1 K2 SOME APPUCATIONS AND CONSEQUENCES OF FEEDBACK

equations (11,1) and (1 1 .2) as a starting point. Our purpose is to provide an introduction

to and an appreciation fcsrthe applications of feedback, rather than to develop an^ of these

applications in detail. Jn the sections that follow, we focus in more depth on =ieverat specific

techniques for analyzing feedback systems that ai^ useful in a wide range of problems,

we includifig many of the applications that

are about t<i describe.
