T T .Z.2 Compensation for Nonideal Elements
Another common use of feedbiitk is to correct tor some of the nonideal properties of the
open-loop system. For example, feedback is often used in the design of amplifiers to provide constant-gain amplification in a given frequency band, and in fact, lE is this application, pioneered by H. S. Black at Bell Telephone Laboratories in the 1930s, that is generally considered to have been the catalyst tor the development of feedback control a^ a practical and useful system design methodology.
Specifically, consider an open-bop frequency respimse HiJM) v^hich prov ides amplificatiim over the specified frequency band, but which is not constats over that range. For example, operational amplifiers or the vacuum tube amplifiers of concern to Black and
his c{)lleagues typically provide considerable, but not precisely controUed, amplificatu^n

822

Unear^ Feedback Sysiems

Chap 11

While such devices can provide raw amplification levels of several ordciK c>f magnitude, the price one pays for this includes uncenain levels of ampl]hcadf>[i that can fluctuate ss'ilh frequenoyn lime, temperature, etc., and that can also introduce unwanted phase and tionlineardisti^rtions What BJack prvposeil was placing.*iU*;h a powerful* buttinceTtain and erratic, amplifier in a feedback loop as in Figure 1 1.3(a) with G(s) chosen to be constant,
i.e., 0(s) == Kr In this case, the clo.sed-Ioop frequency response is

That is, the closed- Unjp frequency response is constant, as desired. This Df course assumes

must be an attenuator over the specified range of frequencies.

In general, an attenuator with approximately fiat frequency characteristics is considerably

easier to realize than an amplifier with approximately flat frequency response (since an

attenuator can be constructed from passive elements).

The use of feedback to flatten the frequency response incurs some cost, however,

and it is this fact that led to the considerable skepticism with which Black's idea was met.

In particular, from eqs. (11.6) and (117), we iiee that

^ i-eO)- \H(,jto)\

(11,8)

so that the closed-loop gain \/K will be substantialiy less than the open -loop gain |H(/w)|.
This apparently significant loss of gain, attributable to what Black referred to as di^^cneraiive or negative feedback, was initially viewed as a serious weakness in his negative-
feedback amplifier. Indeed, the effect had been known for many years and bad led to the conviction that negative feedback was not a particularly useful mechanism. Ho\\ever,
Blauk pointed out that what one gave up in overall gain was often more than offset by the reduced sensitivity of the overall closed-loop amplitier: The closed-loop system function
H w is essentiiiily equal to eq . ( 11 .7), independently of variations in I jtit >, as long a s | // ( ; )
|
IS large enough. Thus, if the open-loop amplifier is inilially dei^igned wjth considerably more gain than is actually needed, the closed-loop amphiier will provide the desired lev-
els of amplification with greatly reduced sensitivity. This concept and iLs application to extending the bandwidth of an amplifier are explored in Probletn 1 1.49.

The preceding discussion Dlustrates how feedback can be uied to stabilize continuous-time systems. The stabilization of unstable systems is an importain application uf feedback for discrete-time systems as well. B\amples of discrete-time systems that are
unstable in the absence of feedback a/e models of population growth. To illustrate hc}w feedback can prevent the unimpeded growth of populations, let us consider a simple model for the evolution of the population of asingle species of iinimal Let v[/il denote the number of animals in ihe nth generation, and assume that without the presence of any impeding influences, the birthrate is such that the population would double each generation. In this case, the basic equation for the population dynamics of the species is

external influences.
Tliis population nnodel is obviously unstable, with an impulse response that ^rows exponentially However, in any ecological system, there are a number of factors that will inhibit the growth of a population. For example, limits on the food suppK for the species
will manifest themselves through a reduction in population gniwdi when thc'number of animals becomes large. Similarly, if the species has natural enemies, it is often reasonable to assume that the population of the predators will grow when the ptiputation of the prey
increases and, ctmsequenlly, thai the presence of natural enemies will retard population
growth. In addition to natural influences such as these, there may be effet is intn>duced b>

Clearly^ this example of population growth and control is extremely simplified. For

by maiing the feedback model more complex to reflect the presence of other dynamics

in an ecological system, and the resulting models for the evolution of interacting spscies

are extremely Imponani in ecological studies. However, even without the incorpi>ratit>n
of these effects, the simple model that we have described here docs illustrate the basic ideas of how feedback: can preveni both the unlbnited proliferation of a species and its extinction. In particular, we can see at an elementary level how human-induced factors
can be used. For example, if a natural disaster or an increase in the population of natural enemies causes a drastic decrease in the population of a species, a tightening of limits on hunting or fishing and accelerated efforts to increase the population can be used to

decrease ^ in order to destabilize the system to alkiw for rapid growth, until a nnnnal-size

population is again attained.

Note also that for this type of problem, it is not usually the case that one wants

^ strict stability. If the regulating influences are such that =^1/2, and if all other external influences are zero {i.e.. if x[n\ = 0),then y[n] = - y[fi 1]. Therefore, as long as .t|n] is

essentially constant population. However, for this value of ^ the system is unstable, since

> example, by a migration of animals into a region, a value of ^ 1/2 would need to be
used to stabilize the system and thus lo keep the population within bounds and maintain
an ecological balance.

1 1 .2.5 Tracking Systems
As mentioned in Section ll.O, one of the important applications of feedback is in the design of systems in which the objective is to have the output track or folbw the input.
There is a broad range of problems in which tracking is an important component. For example, the telescope-pointing problem discussed in Section 1 1.0 is a tracking problem:
The feedback system of Figures 11.1(c) and (d)has as its input the desu^ pointing angle,
and the purpose of the feedback lowp is to pr^ivide a mechanism for driving the telescope
to follow the inpuL In airplane autopilots the input is the desired flight path of the vehicle^ and the autopilot feedback system uses the aircraft control surfaces (rudder, ailerons, and elevator) and thrust control in order to keep the aircraft on the prescribed course.
To illustrate some of Lhe issues that arise in the design of tracking systems, consider the discrete-time feedback system depicted in Figure 1 1 7(a>. The eiaminati^m [>f discrete-time tracking systems of this form often arises in analyzing the characteristics of sampled-daia tracking systems for continuoLis-time applications. One example of such a system is a digital autopilot. 1 1.2.6 DestabUt2atJon Caused by Feedback
As well as having many applications, feedback can buve undesirable effeci.s and can in
fact cause instability. For example, consider the telescope- pointing system illustrated in
Figure 11.1. From the discussion in the preceding section, we know thnt it would be desirable to have a large amplifier gain in order to achieve good performance in tracking the
desired pointing angle. On the other hand, as we increase the gain, we are likeiy n> obtain
faster U^acking response at the expense of a reduction in system dampmg, resulting in sig-
nificant overshoot and ringing in response to changes in die desired angle. FLrdiermore, instabiUiy can result if the gain is increased too much.
Another cvimraon e)iample of the possible destabilizing effect of feedback is feedback in audio systems. Consider the situaLon depicted in Figure ll.S(a). Here, a loudspeaker produces an audio signal that js an amplified version tif the sounds picked up by a microphone. Note that in addition to other audio inputs, the sound coming from the speaker
itself may be sensed by the microphone. How strong diis particular signal i"; depends upon

It is inierestirtg to note that: positive, or what Black referred to as regenerative, feedback had also been known for some time before he invented his negative feedback amplifier and, ironically, had been viewed as a very useful mechanisni (in contrast to the skeptical view of negative feedback). Indeed, positive feedback can be useful. For exam-
ple, it was already known in the 1 920s that the destabilizing infl uence of positive feedback
cotjld be used to generate oscillating signals. This use of positive feedback is illustrated in Problem 11.54,
In this section, we have described a number of the applications of feedback. These
and others, such as the use of feedback in the implementation of recursive discrete-dme filters (see Problem 11.55). are considered in more detail in tfie problems at the end of die chapter. From our examin^ion of the uses of feedback and the possible stabilizing and destabilizing effects that it can have, it is clear that some care must be taken in designing and analyzing feedback si?itcma to ensure that the ciosed-loop system behaves in a desir-
able fashion. Specifically, in Sections 1 1.2.3 and 1 1.2.6, we have seen several examples
of feedback systems in which the characterisdcs of the closed^-loop system can be signif-
m icantly altered by changing the values of one or two parameters the feedback system.
In the venaaining sections of this chapter, we develop several techniques for analyzing the
effect of changes in such parameters on the ciosed-loop (jystem and for designing sy stems to meet desired objectives such as stability, adequate damping, etc.

1 1 .3 ROOT^LOCUS AMALYSIS OF LINEAR FEEDBACK SYSTEMS

closed-loop system change, since the locations of these poles tell ns a great dea! about the behavior of the system. For example, in stabilizing ai; unstable system^ die adjustable gain
is ujied to move the polen into the left-half plane for a ^ontintious-tirEie system or inside the unit circle for a discrete-time system, tn addition, in Problem 11,4^^, we show that feedback can be used to broaden the bandwidth of a first-order system by moving the pole so ii^ to decrease the time consiantof the system. Ai one consequence of Propenies 1 through 3, consider a segment of the real axis

between two pole*i i}fO{s)H{s), with no zeros between these poles. From Property I the

root locus begins at the poles^ and from Property 3 the entire portion of the real a)(is between

Propejty 3. us \f(\ increasss toward infinity; each branch of the root locus must approach

a iCTo. Since there are no zenjs along that portion of the real axis, the emly way that this

properly of the root locus;

Ihroperty 4: Branches of the root locus between two real poles must brealt off into the complex plane for \K\ large enough.
Properties 1--4 serve to illustrate how characteristics of the root locus can be deduced from eqs. (11.45), (1 1.59J, and(ll.fiO). In many cases, plotting the poles and zeros
of Gis)ff{s) and then usii^ these four properties suffices to provide a reasonably accurate sketch of the mot locus. iSee Examples 11.2 ajid 11.3.) fn addition to these propenies, however, there aie DumeR>tis other characteristics of the ri>i"'t locus that allovn one to ob-
tain sketches of increasing accuracy. For example, from Property 2, we know that n - ttt
branches of the rool locus approach infinity. In fact, these branches appmoch infinity at specific angles that can be calculated, and therefore, the branches are asymptotically parallel to lines at these angles. Moreover, it i^; possible to draw in the asymptotes and then dciermine the point at which they intersect. These two properties and several others are
A illustrated in Problems 11.34-11.^6 and 11.41-11 42, more detailed development of
the root-locus nnethod can be f^und in more advarvced tejLts such as thf^se listed ii^ the bibliography at the end of the biMjk,

1 A1 THE NYQUIST STABJUTY CRfTERION
As developed in Section 1 1.3, the root-locus techtiique provides detailed infomriation concerning the location of closed-loop poles as :he system gain is varied. From root-lucus
K plots, one can determine the damping and the ^tahility characteristics of the system as
is varied. Determination of the root locus requires the analytic description of the sysiem

Sec. 11.4

The Nyquist Stability Criterion

847

fun^'tions of Ihe forward and feedback: path;; and is applicable onty when these transforms areraHonal Forexamplejt cannot be direcdy applied in situations in which our kmwledge of the sysiieni functions ij obtained purely from experimental ioti.
Iti this section, we introduce another method foi the determination of the stability of
feedback systems as a function of an adjustable gain parameter. This technique, referred to as the Nyifuixt criterion, differs from the root-locus method in two basic ways Unlike the root- locus methods the Nyquist criterion does not provide detailed information concerning the location of the closed-loop poles as a ftinction of K^ but rather^ simply determines
whether or not the system is si^le for any specified value of it. On the odier hand, the
Nyquist criterion can be applied to nonrational system functions and in situations in which no analytic description of the forward and feedback path system functiims is available.
Our objective iti this section is to outline the basic ideas behind the Nyquist cnlerion
for both continuous-time and discrete-time systems. As we will see, bcnh the discretetime and continuous-timt Nyquist testis are the result of the same fundamental concept,
although, aA with the root- locus method, the actual criteria for stability differ because of
the differences between continuous and discrete time. More detailed developments of the
ideas behind the Nyquist criterion and its use iii the design of feedback systems can be found in texts on the analysis and synthesis of feedback systerns and automatic control systems, including those listed in the bibliography at the end of the bogk.
To introduce the method, let u^ recall that the poles of the closed-loop .systems of Figure 1 L 10 and their discrete -time counterparts are the solutions of the equations

circle. The basis for this is the encirclement property, which we develop in the following
subsection.

IS based on the fact thai the difference in the nami:>er of poles and ieros inside a contour, for a rational function, can be determined by examining a plot of the value of the function along the contour. As we have seen in this section, the Nyquist stability critenon provides a useful methed for determining the range of values of the gain A' for which a continuous-time
or discrete-time feedback is stable Cor unstable). This criterion and che root-locus meihixl are extremely important tools in the design and implementation of feedback systems, aniJ
each has its own uses and Limitations F^ir example, the Nyquist criterion can be applied to nonrationai system functions, whereas the root-locus method eannot. On the other hand,
root-locus plots allow us to examine not only stability, but also other charaeteristie.s tif the closed-loop system response, such as damping, oscillation frequency, and so on, which are readily identifiable from the location of the poles of the closed-Joop system. In the next section, we introduce an additional tool for the analysis of feedback systems that highlights another important characteristic of dofjcd-loop system behavior

1 1 .5 GAIN AND PHASE MARGINS

En this section, we intreduce and examine the t:ontept of the margin ojsfttbitifv in a feed-

back system. It is often of interest not only to know whi^rhir a feedbaL-k system is stable,

but also to deteftnlne how much the gain in the system can be perturbed and how muL-h

additional phase shift can be added to the system before it becomes unstable. Information

such as this Is important because in many applications the forward and feedback system

functions are known only approximately or may change slightly during operation bet-MUse

of wear, the effect of high temperatures on components, or similar influences

As an example, consider the telescope-pointing system described in SeLtion i 10

and illustrated in Figures l],l(c)ajid (d>. This systemc^msistsof a motor, a potentiometer

converting the shaft angie ta a vfiltage, and an amplifier that is used lo amplify tne voltage

repnisenting the difference between the desired and the actual shaft angles. Assuming that
we have obtained approximate descriptions of each of these tompments, we can set the

amplifier gain so that the system will be stable if these descriptions are accurate. However,

the ampUJier gain and the constant of proportionality that describes the angle- voltage char-

acteristic of the potentiometer are never known exactly, and therefi^re, the actual gain in

the feedback system may differ from the nominal value assumed in designing the system

Furthermore, the damping characteristics of the motor cannot be detennined with absolute

precision, and thus, the actual time constant of the motoi response may differ from the ap-

proximate value in the specificat3on of the system. For extimple. if the actual jt^otor lime

constant is laiger than the nominal value used in the design, the motor will respond more

sluggishly than anticipated, thereby producing an effective rime delay in the feedback

system ,

time delays have the effect of Increasing the negative phase in the frequency response of a

system, and this phase shift can have a destabilizing mfluence on the system. Because of

the possible presence of gain and phase errois such as. those we have just described, it is
-- clearly desirable to set the amplifier gam so tnat there is some maigm hn error that is. so

that the actual system will remain stable even if it differs somewhat from ihe approximate
m model used the design process.

In this secLion, we introduce one method for quantifying the margin of stability in a feedback system To do this, we consider ii closed-loop system, depicted in Hgure 1 1 25. that has been designed to be stable based on nominal values for the forward- and feedback-
path system functions. For our discussion here, we let A/(s) and G{s) denote these notmnal
values. Also, since ihe basic concepts iire identical for both continuous-time and discrete-
titne systems, we will again focus our development on rhe continuous-time case, and at the
end of the section we illustrate the application of these ideas to a discrete-titne example.

To assess tlie margin of stability in out feedback systenfi, suppose that the actual
system is as depicted in Figure 1 1 .26, where we have allowed for the possibility of a gain
K ^ and phase shift 4> in the feedback path. In the nominal system is unity and is /.erti, but
in the actual system cither or both may iiave a different value. Therefore, it is of intcreit to know how much variation can be tolerated in these quiiiities withtiul losing closed-lmip
system stsbility In particular, the gain margin of the feedback system is defined liS the
minimum amount of additional gain K, with 4* = 0, that is required so that the closed-
loop system becomes unstable. Similarly, the phaie margin is the additional amount of
K pha^e shift, with =^ \^ thai ij> required for the system to be unstable. By convention, the
phase murgin Ik expressed as a positive quantity; that is, it equals the magnitude of tlie
lidditional negative phase shift at which the feedback system becomes un^itable.

In detennining gain and phase margins, it is not always of interest to identify ex-
plicitly ^^frtquency at which the poles will cross the jw-axis. As an alternative, we can
identify the gain and phase margins from a log magnitude -phase diagram. For example, the log magnitude-phase diagram for the system of Figure 1 i.27 is shown in Figure 1 1 .28. In this figure, we plot 20 logio \Oijto}Hiju>)\ versus <G{jtif)H{jii}) as f* varies from to +", Therefore, because of the conjugate symmetry of G(;a£)//(joj>, the pbt contains the same information as the Nyquist plot, in which {R^G(jtj)//<yci>)} is plotted versus
&m{GiJi())H(jui)} for -oo < Èt> < ¨, As we have indicated, the phase margin can be read
off by locating the intersection of the log magnitude-phase plot with the 0-dB line. That is, Che phase margin is the aniount of additional negative phase shift required to shift the log magnitude- phase curve so diat it intersects the 0-dB line with exactly lS(r (tt ra-
dians) of phase shift. Similarly, the gain margin U directly obtained from die intersec-
tion of the log magnitude-phase curve with the line --tt radians, and this represents the amount of additional gain needed so that the curve crosses the line - tt with a magnitude ofOdB.

nitude-phase diagramsr

1 1 ,6 SUMMARY

In this chapter, we have cxamineJ a riumber nf the applieatiL>ns and several lechrnqties for
Lhe analysis (if feedback systems. We have seen how the use of Laplace and ^-tran^forms

allows as to inaly^e these sysleiMS a.tgebraicany and graphically

we In Set:tion 11 .2

it^-

dicated several of the applieatjons of feedback, including tiie design of inverse systems,

"Fnr detailed discnis-ioii'^ orthns p).j][it tiod Lhv <sf emn and phass margin-i and l^lg ma^mtuce-pha-^e
(Jirtj^aitiMngeiktral, ^L-t The ti:>;is(>n feedback; li-^tetJ in the bibllugrdpNy dC [he end uf thi' Dcujfc.

Chap- 11

Problems

S67

the stabilization of unstable systems, and the design of tracking systems. Wc also saw that
feedback tian destabilize, as well as stabilize, a system.
In Section 1 1 .3, we described the rool-locus method for plotting the poles of the closed-loop system as a function of ti gain parameter Here, we found that the geometric
evaluation of the phase of a rational Laplace transform or ^-transform allowed us to gain a significant amount of insight into the properties yf the root locus. These properties often permi; us to obtain a reasonably accurate sketch of the root locus without performing complex calculations
In contrast in the ront-locu'^ method, the Nyquist criterion of Section 1 1,4 is a technique for determining the stability of a feedback system, again as a function of a variable
gain, without obtaining a detailed description of the location of the closed-loop poles. The Nyquist criterion is applicable to uonratiotial system functions and thus can be used when
all that is available are expcrimcmally determined frequency responses. TTie sajne is true of the gain and phase margins described in Section 1 1 ,5, These quantities pmvide a measure i)f the margin t)f stability in a feedback isysiem and therefore are of imporrance to design-
ers in that they allow them to determine how robust a feedback system is To discrepancies
between estimates of the forward- and feedback-path system function*; and their actual
values.

11*47, As mentioned at the end of Section 1 1 .5£ the phase and gain margins may provide su^ient conditions to ensure that a stable feedback system remains stable. For example, we showed that a stable feedback system will rem^n stable as the gain IS increa^ed^ until we reach a limit specified by the gain margin. This does nor imply (a) that the feedback system cannot be made unsiable by detreasmg the
gain or (b) that the system will be un.stable for ail values of gain greater than the
gain margin limit. In this problem, we illustrate these two points,
(a) Consider a continuous-time feedback: system with

(s-i)(y-i-2)(,s + ^y

K the system is unstable* for larger values of K the system is stable, while

for still larger values of K the system again becomes unstable. Find the range

of v^ucs of K for which the system is stable. Hint: Use the same method as

is employed in Example 1 1.2 and Problem 11.35 to determine the values of

A' at which branches of the root locus pass through the origm and cross the

/w-axis.

If we set our gain somewhere within the stable range That you have just

found, we can increa.se the gain somewhat and maintain stability but a large

enough increase in gain eausei the system to become unstable. This maximum

amount of increase in gain at which the closed-loop system just becomes un-
stable is the gam margin. Note that if we decrease the gain too much, we clui

aUo cajse instability,

the pesition of the cart with respect to some reference point, a(t) is the acceleriition of the cart, and xit) represents the angular acceleration resuUing from any
disturbances, such as gusts of wind.

F^tjnePII.56

Appendix
Partial-fraction expansion

Al JNTTRODUCrrON

The purpose of this appendix is to describe the technique rf partial -fraction expansion.

This tool IS of greai value in the study of signals and systems; in particular^ it is very

useful in inverting Fourier, Laplace, or ^-iransfonns and in analyzing LTL systems de-

scribed by Mnear constant-coefficient differeniial or difference equations. The method of

paitial-fraction expansion consists of taking a function that is the ratio of polynomials and

eTcpandingitas a linearcombinatjonof simpler terms of the same type. The determination

of the coefficients in the linear con^bination is the basic problem to be solved in obtaining
the expansion. As we will see, this is a relatively strai|htf(>rwaitl problem in algebra that

can be solved very efficiently with a bit of "bookkeeping."

To illustr<ite the basic idea behind and rtile of parti al-fractitm expansion, consider

the analysis developed in Section 6.5.2 for a second-order continUou.s-time LTr system

specified by the differential equation

Also, in each ofthe.se contexts, we find reasons for expanding these transfonns as sums of simpler terms such as in eq. (A.9), In this section, in order to develop a general procedure for
calculating the expansions* we consider rational functions of a general variable v\ that is* we examine functions of the form

application to the analysis of both continuous-time and di.screte-time LTI systems,

Bibliography

The purpose of this bibliogiaphy is tu provide the reader with sources for a<iditLorLal and
m mcne advaticed treatments of topics signal and system analy^i^. This is by no means

meant to be an exhaui^tive list but rather it is intended to indicate direclionf, for further

study and sevcrai references for each.

We have divided the bibliography into sixteen different subject areas. The first few

deal with the mathematical techniques of signal and sysietB analysis including texts on

background mathematics (calculus, differential and difference equations, and complex

variables), the theory of Fourier scries and of Fourier, Laplace, and ^-transforms, and addi-

tional topics in mathematit;s that are commonly encotintered and lised in signal and s>stem

analysis, Seveial of the sections that follow deal with more thorough and specialized treat-

ments of topics in .signals and sy5tems introduced in this text^ including filtering, sampling

We and discrete-time signal processing, commitnications, and feedback and ctmtnjl.

have

alsu provided a list of other basii: books on signals and systems as well as several texts on

circuit theory. In addition, we have provided lists of references on several topics lha[ rep-

resent important subjects for more advanced study for those interested either in expanding their knowledge of the methods of signals and svstems or in exploring applications that make use of these advanced techniques. In particular we include sections on state ipace

m<ide1s and methods, multidimensional signal and image processing, speech processing,

niultirate and Tnultire';c>lulion signal anaLysig, random signals and statistical" signal prt)

cessing, and nonlinear systems Finally we have included a liat of references dealing with

a sampling of i^ther applications and advanced topics. Together, the references collected in

this bibliography should prijvide the reader with an appreciation for the breadth of topics

and applications thai comprise the field of signals and systems.
